tEn: Happiness vs. Success: Two Paths to Fulfillment
tZh: 幸福与成功：通往满足的两条路
sEn: Exploring whether happiness or material success leads to a more fulfilling life, and how redefining success can change the journey.
sZh: 探讨幸福与物质成功哪个更能带来满足感，以及重新定义成功如何改变人生旅程。

consensus.en: Happiness is being satisfied with what you have; success comes from dissatisfaction.
consensus.zh: 幸福是对所拥有的感到满足；成功源于不满足。

consensus.en: The journey is the only thing that matters; 99% of your time is spent on it.
consensus.zh: 过程才是一切；你99%的时间都花在过程上。

consensus.en: Wealth creation is a positive-sum game; status games are zero-sum and limited.
consensus.zh: 财富创造是正和游戏；地位游戏是零和且有限的。

consensus.en: Self-esteem is the reputation you have with yourself; live up to your own moral code.
consensus.zh: 自尊是你对自己的声誉；遵守自己的道德准则。

consensus.en: Inspiration is perishable; act on it immediately for maximum effectiveness.
consensus.zh: 灵感易逝；立即行动才能发挥最大效果。

consensus.en: Productize yourself: find what feels like play to you but looks like work to others.
consensus.zh: 把自己产品化：找到对你而言像玩乐、对他人像工作的事情。

contrarian.en: Being happier can make you more successful, not less, as your definition of success changes.
contrarian.zh: 更幸福反而能让你更成功，因为你对成功的定义会改变。

contrarian.en: The most expensive trait is pride; it prevents learning and traps you in local maxima.
contrarian.zh: 最昂贵的特质是骄傲；它阻碍学习，让你困在局部最优。

contrarian.en: Default answer to any opportunity should be no; say yes only after careful consideration.
contrarian.zh: 任何机会的默认答案都应该是“不”；只有经过仔细考虑后才说“是”。

contrarian.en: Don't keep a schedule; delete your calendar and live spontaneously for more freedom.
contrarian.zh: 不要保留日程表；删除日历，随性生活以获得更多自由。

contrarian.en: Thinking about yourself is the source of unhappiness; focus on bigger problems or missions.
contrarian.zh: 思考自己是所有不幸福的根源；专注于更大的问题或使命。

contrarian.en: The gut decides; the mind rationalizes. Trust refined judgment over analytical pros and cons.
contrarian.zh: 直觉做决定；头脑只是合理化。相信精炼的判断而非分析利弊。

turns.en: Happiness is being satisfied with what you have. Success comes from dissatisfaction. Is success worth it then?
turns.zh: 幸福是满足于你所拥有的。成功源于不满足。那成功还值得追求吗？

turns.en: Oof. I'm not sure that statement is true anymore. Like I made that statement a long time ago and a lot of these things are just notes to myself and they're highly contextual. They come in the moment, they leave in the moment. Happiness. Okay. So very complicated topic but I always like the Socrates story where he goes into the marketplace and they show him all these luxuries and fineries and he says how many things there are in this world that I do not want right and that's a form of freedom so not wanting something is as good as having it in the old story with Alexander Dionius right Alexander goes out and conquers the world and he meets Dionius who's living in a barrel and Dionius says get out of the way you're blocking my son and Alexander says oh how I wish I you know could be like Dionius the next life and Dionis says that's the difference I don't wish that I could sorry Dioynes Dioynes Dioynes says I I I don't wish to be Alexander so two paths to happiness and uh one path is success you get what you want you satisfy your material needs or like Dioynes you just don't want in the first place and I'm not sure which one is more valid um and it also depends what you define as success if the end goal is happiness then why not cut to the chase and just go straight for
turns.zh: 唉。我不确定那句话现在是否还成立。那是我很久以前写下的笔记，很多都是写给自己的，高度依赖上下文。它们来去匆匆。幸福。好吧，这是个非常复杂的话题。但我一直喜欢苏格拉底的故事：他走进集市，人们向他展示各种奢侈品和精美物品，他说‘这世上有多少东西是我并不想要的’，这是一种自由。不想要某样东西和拥有它一样好。还有亚历山大和第欧根尼的古老故事：亚历山大征服世界后遇到住在木桶里的第欧根尼，第欧根尼说‘让开，你挡住了我的阳光’。亚历山大说‘我多么希望来世能像第欧根尼一样’，第欧根尼说‘这就是区别，我不希望成为亚历山大’。所以有两条通往幸福的路：一条是成功——得到你想要的，满足物质需求；另一条是像第欧根尼那样，一开始就不想要。我不确定哪条路更有效，这也取决于你如何定义成功。如果最终目标是幸福，那为什么不直奔主题呢？

turns.en: Does being happy make you less successful? That is a conventional wisdom. That may even be the practical earned experience of your reality. You find that when you're happy, you don't want anything. So, you don't get up and do anything. On the other hand, you know, you still got to do something. You're an animal. You're here. You're here to survive. You're here to replicate. You're driven. You're motivated. You're going to do something. You're not just going to sit there all day. Unlikely. Some people do. Maybe it's in their nature. But I think most people still want to act. they want to live in the arena. Uh I found for myself as I've become uh happier is a big word, but you know, more peaceful, more calm, more present, more uh satisfied with what I have, uh I still want to do things. I just want to do bigger things. I want to do things that are more pure, more aligned with uh what I think needs to be done and what I can uniquely do. So in that sense, I think that being happier can actually make you more successful, but your definition of success will likely change along the way.
turns.zh: 快乐会让你不那么成功吗？这是传统智慧，甚至可能是你现实中的实际经验。你会发现，当你快乐时，你什么都不想要，所以你不会起身去做任何事。但另一方面，你仍然需要做点什么。你是动物，你在这里是为了生存和繁衍。你有驱动力，有动机，你会做点什么，不会整天坐着。不太可能。有些人会，也许那是他们的天性。但我认为大多数人仍然想行动，想活在竞技场里。我发现，随着我变得更快乐——这是个很大的词，但更平和、更平静、更活在当下、更满足于我所拥有的——我仍然想做事，只是想做更大的事，更纯粹、更符合我认为需要做且我能独特完成的事。从这个意义上说，我认为更快乐实际上可以让你更成功，但你对成功的定义可能会随之改变。

turns.en: Is that a realization you think you could have gotten to had you not had some success in the first place?
turns.zh: 你认为如果你最初没有取得一些成功，你能意识到这一点吗？

turns.en: At least for me, I always wanted to take the path of material success first. I was not going to go be an aesthetic and sit there and renounce everything. That just seems too unrealistic and too painful. Uh, in the story of Buddha, he starts out as a prince and then he sees that it's all kind of meaningless because you're still going to get old and die and then he goes into the woods looking for something more. I'll take the happy route that involves material success. Thank you. I think it's quicker in some ways. You know, one of your uh insights is it's far easier to achieve our material desires than it is to renounce them. And uh it depends on the person, but I I think you have to try that path. If you want something, go get it. Uh, you know, like I I I quipped that the reason to win the game is to be free of it. So, you you play the games, you win the games, and then you get hopefully you get bored of the games. You don't want to just keep looping on the same game over and over. Although a lot of these games are very enticing and have many levels and are relatively open-ended. Uh, and then you become free of the game uh in a sense that you're no longer trying to win it. You know, you can win it. Uh, and either you move to a different game or you play the game for the sheer joy of it.
turns.zh: 至少对我来说，我总想先走物质成功的路。我不会去做苦行僧，坐在那里放弃一切。那太不现实，也太痛苦了。在佛陀的故事里，他最初是王子，然后发现一切都没有意义，因为你仍然会老去和死亡，于是他走进森林寻找更多。我会选择包含物质成功的快乐之路。谢谢。我认为这在某些方面更快。你的一个见解是，实现物质欲望远比放弃它们容易。这因人而异，但我认为你必须尝试那条路。如果你想要什么，就去得到它。我曾打趣说，赢得游戏是为了摆脱它。所以你玩游戏，赢游戏，然后希望你对游戏感到厌倦。你不想一遍又一遍地循环同一个游戏，尽管很多游戏非常诱人，有很多关卡且相对开放。然后你在某种意义上摆脱了游戏，不再试图赢它。你知道你能赢。要么你换一个游戏，要么你纯粹为了乐趣而玩。

turns.en: Yeah. You, another one of yours, most of the gains in life come from suffering in the short term so you can get paid in the long term. I think that's classic. Winning the marshmallow test on a daily basis. But, uh, there's an interesting challenge where I think people need to avoid becoming uh, a suffering addict. Sort of using suffering as the proxy for progress as opposed to the outcome of the suffering. Right? It's like I was in pain not eating the marshmallow. I was in pain doing this work. I have attached well-being and satisfaction to pain, not to what the pain gets me on the other side of it.
turns.zh: 是的。你的另一句话：生活中大部分收获来自短期的痛苦，这样你才能在长期得到回报。我觉得很经典。每天都在棉花糖测试中获胜。但有一个有趣的挑战：我认为人们需要避免成为痛苦成瘾者。把痛苦当作进步的替代品，而不是痛苦的结果。对吧？就像我因为没吃棉花糖而痛苦，我因为做这项工作而痛苦。我把幸福和满足感附着在痛苦上，而不是痛苦带给我的结果上。

turns.en: If you define pain as physical pain, then it's a real thing. It happens and you can't ignore it. But that's not what we mean by suffering. Suffering is mostly mental anguish and mental pain. And it just means you don't want to do the task at hand. Uh if you were fine doing the task at hand, then you wouldn't be suffering. And then the question is what's more effective to suffer along the way or just to interpret it in a way that it's not suffering? You hear from a lot of successful people they look back and they say oh the journey was the fun part right that was actually the entertaining part and I should have enjoyed it more. It's a common regret.
turns.zh: 如果你把痛苦定义为身体上的疼痛，那是真实存在的，发生了就无法忽视。但这不是我们所说的‘受苦’。受苦主要是精神上的痛苦和折磨。它只是意味着你不想做手头的任务。如果你做手头的任务没问题，你就不会受苦。那么问题来了：是沿途受苦更有效，还是把它解释成不是受苦更有效？你听到很多成功人士回顾时说，‘哦，旅程才是有趣的部分，那其实是娱乐的部分，我应该更享受它。’这是一种常见的遗憾。

turns.en: Uh there's a little thought exercise I like to do, which is you can go back into your own life and uh try to put yourself in the exact position you were in 5 years ago, 10 years ago, 15 years ago, 20 years ago. And you try to remember, okay, who was I with? What was I doing? What was I feeling? What were my emotions? What were my objectives? And really, really try to transport yourself back and see if there's any advice you'd give yourself. Anything you do differently. Now, you don't have new information. And don't pretend you could have gone back and, you know, bought a stock or bought bought Bitcoin or whatever, but just knowing what you know now in terms of your temperament and a little bit of age related experience, how would you have done things differently? And I think it's a worthwhile exercise to do. So don't let me rob you of the conclusion, but I'll tell you for me uh I would have done everything the same except I would have done it with less anger, less emotion, less internal suffering because that was optional. It wasn't necessary. And I would argue that someone who can do the job uh at least peacefully but maybe happily is going to be more effective than someone who has unnecessary emotional turmoil.
turns.zh: 我喜欢做一个小思维练习：回到你自己的生活中，试着把自己放在5年前、10年前、15年前、20年前的确切位置。试着回忆：我和谁在一起？我在做什么？我感觉如何？我的情绪是什么？我的目标是什么？真正地把自己带回去，看看有没有什么建议可以给自己，有没有什么可以做得不同。现在，你没有新信息。别假装你可以回去买股票或比特币什么的，但仅凭你现在知道的关于自己性情和一点年龄相关的经验，你会怎么做不同的事？我认为这是一个值得做的练习。所以别让我剥夺你的结论，但对我来说，我会做同样的一切，只是带着更少的愤怒、更少的情绪、更少的内在痛苦，因为那是可选的，不是必要的。而且我认为，一个至少能平和地、甚至快乐地完成工作的人，会比一个有不必要情绪动荡的人更有效。

turns.en: Well, you end up with a series of miserable successes, right? The outcome may have been the same, but the entire experience of getting there and and the journey is not only the reward. The journey is the only thing there is. You know, even success, it's human nature to bank it very very quickly, right? Because the normal loop that we run through is you sit around, you're bored, then you want something, then when you want something, you decide you're not going to be happy until you get that thing.
turns.zh: 嗯，你最终会得到一系列悲惨的成功，对吧？结果可能是一样的，但整个到达那里的经历和旅程不仅是奖励，旅程就是一切。你知道，即使是成功，人类天性会很快把它存入银行，对吧？因为我们运行的正常循环是：你坐着，无聊，然后想要某样东西，然后当你想要某样东西时，你决定在得到它之前不会快乐。

transcript.sec: The Cycle of Desire and Happiness

turns.en: Then you start your bout of suffering or anticipation while you strive to get that thing. If you get that thing, then you get used to it and then you get bored again. Then a few months later, you want something else. And if you don't get it, then you're unhappy for a bit and then you get over it and then you want something else. Right? That's the normal cycle. So whether you're happy or unhappy at the end, it tends not to last. Now I don't want to be glib and say that oh there's no point in making money or being successful. There absolutely is. Money solves all your money problems. So it is good to have money. That said, there are those stories. I don't know if you've seen those studies. I don't know how real these are. A lot of these psych studies don't replicate, but it's a fun little study that shows that people who break their back and people who win the lottery are back to their baseline happiness two years later. Yep. Again, I don't know if that's entirely true. I think money can buy you happiness if you earned it because then along the way you have both pride and confidence in yourself and you have a sense of accomplishment and you set out to do something and you were right. So I'll bet that lingers and as I said money solves your money problem. So I don't want to be too glib about it but I would say in general this loop that we run through of desire, dopamine, fulfillment, unfulfillment, you have to enjoy the journey. The journey is all there is, right? 99% of your time is spent on the journey. So, what kind of a journey is it if you're not going to enjoy it? How do you shortcut that desire contract? You could focus, you could decide that I don't want most things. I think we have a lot of unnecessary desires that we just pick up everywhere. We have opinions on everything, judgments on everything. So, I think just knowing that those are the source of unhappiness will make you be choosy about your desires. And frankly, if you want to be successful, you have to be choosy about your desires. You have to focus. You can't be great at everything. You're just going to waste your energy and waste your time.
turns.zh: 然后你开始受苦或期待，同时努力去得到那个东西。如果你得到了，你就会习惯它，然后再次感到无聊。几个月后，你又想要别的东西。如果你没得到，你会短暂不开心，然后恢复，接着又想要别的。对吧？这就是正常的循环。所以无论你最终是开心还是不开心，它往往都不会持久。我不想轻率地说赚钱或成功没有意义。绝对有意义。钱能解决你所有的金钱问题。所以有钱是好事。话虽如此，确实有那些故事。我不知道你是否看过那些研究。我不知道它们有多真实。很多心理学研究无法复现，但有一个有趣的小研究表明，那些摔断背的人和中了彩票的人，两年后都会回到他们的幸福基线。是的。同样，我不知道这是否完全正确。我认为如果你赚到了钱，钱可以买到幸福，因为在这个过程中你对自己有自豪感和信心，有成就感，你设定目标并实现了。我打赌这种感觉会持续，而且正如我所说，钱能解决你的金钱问题。所以我不想说得太轻率，但总的来说，我们经历的欲望、多巴胺、满足、不满足这个循环，你必须享受过程。过程就是一切，对吧？99%的时间你都在过程中。所以，如果你不打算享受它，那是什么样的过程？如何缩短那个欲望契约？你可以专注，你可以决定我不想要大多数东西。我认为我们有很多不必要的欲望，随处捡来。我们对每件事都有意见，对每件事都有评判。所以，我认为只要知道这些是不快乐的根源，你就会对欲望有所选择。坦率地说，如果你想成功，你必须对欲望有所选择。你必须专注。你不可能事事精通。你只会浪费精力和时间。

turns.en: Is fame a worthwhile goal?
turns.zh: 名声是一个值得追求的目标吗？

turns.en: Uh, it gets you invited to better parties, gets you into better restaurants. Fame is this funny thing where a lot of people know you, but you don't know them. And it does get you put on a pedestal. It can get you what you want at a distance. So, I wouldn't say it's worthless. Obviously, people want it for a reason. It's high status, so it attracts the opposite sex. Especially for men, it attracts women. That said, it is high cost. It means you have no privacy. You do have weirdos and lunatics. You do get hit up a lot for weird things. And you're on a stage, so you're forced to perform, so you're forced to be consistent with your past proclamations and actions, and you're going to have haters and all that nonsense. But the fact that we do it, the fact that we all seem to want it means that it would be disingenuous to say, 'Oh, no, no, I'm famous.' But you don't want to be famous. That said, I think fame, like anything else, is best produced as a or pursued as a byproduct of something potentially more worthwhile. Wanting to be famous and craving to be famous and being famous for being famous, these are sort of traps. Fame for fame's sake.
turns.zh: 嗯，它能让你受邀参加更好的派对，进入更好的餐厅。名声是一件有趣的事：很多人认识你，但你却不认识他们。它确实会让你被捧上神坛。它能让你在远处得到你想要的东西。所以，我不会说它毫无价值。显然，人们想要它是有原因的。它是高地位的象征，所以能吸引异性。尤其是对男人来说，它能吸引女人。话虽如此，它的代价很高。这意味着你没有隐私。你会遇到怪人和疯子。你会经常被要求做奇怪的事情。而且你站在舞台上，被迫表演，被迫与你过去的言论和行动保持一致，你会有黑粉和所有那些废话。但我们确实这样做，我们似乎都想要它，这意味着说“哦，不，不，我出名了”是不真诚的。但你不想出名。话虽如此，我认为名声，和其他事物一样，最好是作为更有价值事物的副产品而产生或追求。想要出名、渴望出名、为了出名而出名，这些都是陷阱。为名声而名声。

turns.en: Yeah. Exactly. So, it's better that it's earned fame.
turns.zh: 是的。完全正确。所以，最好是赢得的名声。

turns.en: Uh so for example earn respect in the tribe is you do things that are good for the tribe. Who are the most famous people in human history? There are people who sort of transcended the self. The Buddhas and the Jesuses and the Muhammads of the world. Who else is famous? The artists are famous. Art lasts for a long time. The scientists are famous. They discover things. The conquerors are famous presumably because they conquered for their tribe. There was someone that they were fighting for. So generally the higher up you rise by doing things for greater and greater groups of people even though it may be considered tyrannical or negative like Genghis Khan is famous but to the Mongols he was doing good to the rest of them not so much the higher level you're operating at the more people you're taking care of the more you sort of earn respect and fame and I think those are good reasons to be famous. If fame is empty, if you're famous just because your name showed up in a lot of places or your face showed up in a lot of places, then that's a hollow fame and I think deep down you will know that and so it'll be fragile and you'll always be afraid of losing it and then you'll be forced to perform. So the kind of fame that pure actors and celebrities have I wouldn't want but the kind of fame that's earned because you did something useful, why dodge that?
turns.zh: 嗯，例如在部落中赢得尊重，就是做对部落有益的事。人类历史上最著名的人是谁？有些人是超越自我的。世上的佛陀、耶稣和穆罕默德。还有谁出名？艺术家出名。艺术长久流传。科学家出名。他们发现事物。征服者出名，大概是因为他们为部落而征服。他们为之战斗的人。所以一般来说，你为越来越大的群体做事而上升得越高，即使可能被视为暴虐或负面，比如成吉思汗很有名，但对蒙古人来说他做了好事，对其他人则不然。你运作的层次越高，你照顾的人越多，你就越能赢得尊重和名声，我认为这些是出名的好理由。如果名声是空洞的，如果你出名只是因为你的名字出现在很多地方，或者你的脸出现在很多地方，那么那是空洞的名声，我认为你内心深处会知道，所以它会很脆弱，你会一直害怕失去它，然后被迫表演。所以纯粹演员和名人的那种名声我不想要，但那种因为做了有用的事而赢得的名声，为什么要回避呢？

turns.en: Now you can there's a challenge I think especially if people make very loud public proclamations about things you mentioned there about you're almost hostage to the things that you used to say that being able to update your opinions and change your mind looks very similar to the internet as hypocrisy does.
turns.zh: 现在你可以，我认为有一个挑战，尤其是当人们非常大声地公开宣称某些事情时，你提到过，你几乎成了你过去所说的话的人质，能够更新你的观点和改变主意，在互联网上看起来很像虚伪。

turns.en: No, no, no. The difference between me saying something in the past and saying something different now is perhaps I've learned, perhaps I've updated my beliefs, but so few people do it in a legitimate way. I think that the grifter shill you see this is the smoking gun that shows that he didn't really believe that thing all along. Right. And yeah, I went to a retreat in LA a couple of years ago and there was a guy that I used to follow that a big business and productivity advice content creator really really successful and he just totally stepped back from everything and went like monk mode and focused on his business. I asked him why and he said I started feeling like I had to live up to in private the things that I was saying in public.
turns.zh: 不，不，不。我过去说某件事和现在说不同的事之间的区别，可能是我学到了，可能是我更新了我的信念，但很少有人以合法的方式这样做。我认为那些骗子和托儿，你看到这就是确凿证据，表明他从来就没有真正相信过那件事。是的。几年前我去洛杉矶参加一个静修，那里有一个我以前关注的人，一个很大的商业和生产力建议内容创作者，非常成功，他完全退出了所有事情，进入了类似僧侣模式，专注于他的业务。我问他为什么，他说我开始觉得我必须在私下里兑现我在公开场合说的话。

turns.en: Right. Yeah. It's a what was it that who said it was a mein that foolish consistency is a hobgoblin of little minds right?
turns.zh: 对。是的。这是谁说的？好像是爱默生说的，愚蠢的一致性是小心灵的妖怪，对吧？

turns.en: But essentially look all life is all learning is error correction right every knowledge creation system works through correcting errors making guesses and correcting errors so by definition if you're learning you're going to be wrong most of the time and you'll be updating your priors and so for example I did this Joe Rogan podcast I don't know it's like eight or nine years ago and people will call out like the one thing that didn't turn out to be correct, right? And it's just like and they just beat on it because it helps them in their mind raise their status a little bit. Aha, I caught him in an error. Well, I think if you catch someone in a blatant lie where there's believe one thing and they say another, that's legit. That's a character flaw. They shouldn't be lying.
turns.zh: 但本质上，看，所有生命，所有学习都是纠错，对吧？每个知识创造系统都通过纠正错误、做出猜测和纠正错误来运作。所以根据定义，如果你在学习，你大多数时候都会错，你会更新你的先验。例如，我做了乔·罗根的播客，大概是八九年前，人们会指出那一件不正确的事，对吧？然后他们就一直揪着不放，因为这在他们心里有助于提升一点地位。啊哈，我抓到他错了。嗯，我认为如果你抓到某人公然撒谎，他们相信一件事却说了另一件事，那是合理的。那是性格缺陷。他们不应该撒谎。

turns.en: But on the other hand, if they just made a guess at something and they got it wrong. And by the way, mostly it's about the AI AGI thing. And I think I'm still right about that, but it's a different story. People who think we have achieved AGI just fail a Turing test from their side. But it's funny how people latch on to single proclamations. But the reality is all of us are dynamical systems. We're always changing. We're always learning. We're always growing. And hopefully we're correcting errors. What you don't want to be doing is lying in public because you're trying to look good. And I think people can smell that. What this world really lacks right now is authenticity, because everybody wants something. They want to be seen as something. They want to be something that they're not. And so you do catch a lot of people saying things that they don't really believe. And I think people are very sensitive to that. Radars have become hypersensitized to try and work out whether or not this person means the thing that they're saying.
turns.zh: 但另一方面，如果他们只是猜测某事而猜错了。顺便说一句，这主要关乎AI和AGI这件事。我认为我在这点上仍然是对的，但那是另一个故事了。那些认为我们已经实现AGI的人，只是自己没通过图灵测试。但有趣的是，人们总是抓住单一的宣言不放。但现实是，我们都是动态系统。我们一直在变化，一直在学习，一直在成长。希望我们也在纠正错误。你不应该做的是为了看起来好看而在公开场合撒谎。我觉得人们能嗅到这一点。这个世界现在真正缺乏的是真诚，因为每个人都想要点什么。他们想被看作某种人，想成为他们不是的那种人。所以你确实会抓到很多人说他们并不真正相信的话。我觉得人们对此非常敏感。雷达已经变得过度敏感，试图判断这个人是否真的相信他们说的话。

turns.en: Yeah. I mean a lot of people are wrong. Most of us are wrong most of the time, especially in any new endeavor. The difference between being wrong and disingenuous though, purposefully wrong.
turns.zh: 是的。我是说很多人都是错的。我们大多数人在大多数时候都是错的，尤其是在任何新尝试中。但错误和不诚实之间的区别在于，故意犯错。

turns.en: Correct. Exactly. So I think that's the big difference. If someone is wrong, no big deal. As long as they have a genuine reason for saying what they're saying, or believing what they're believing. But if they are lying to elevate their status or their appearance or to live up to some expectation, that's the mistake. And that's a mistake not just for the listener, it's a mistake for themselves because then you're going to get trapped in a hall of mirrors. You yourself are going to be consistent with your past proclamations. So if you're lying to others, you're going to be lying to yourself. You're puppeted by a person that you are not even.
turns.zh: 对，没错。所以我认为这是最大的区别。如果有人错了，没什么大不了的。只要他们对自己说的话或相信的事有真实的理由。但如果他们为了提升地位、外表或迎合某种期望而撒谎，那就是错误。这个错误不仅对听众有害，对他们自己也有害，因为你会陷入一个镜子迷宫。你自己会与你过去的宣言保持一致。所以如果你对别人撒谎，你也会对自己撒谎。你被一个甚至不是你的木偶操控着。

turns.en: That's right. Yeah. It's like what was that line? You're basically trying to impress people who you know don't care about you. So they don't like the real you and if they saw the real you they wouldn't care and the people who would like the real you don't get to see the real you so they pass you by, right? You only want the respect of the very very few people that you respect. Trying to demand respect from the masses is a fool's errand.
turns.zh: 没错。是的。那句话怎么说来着？你基本上是在试图打动那些你知道并不在乎你的人。所以他们不喜欢真实的你，如果看到真实的你，他们也不会在乎；而那些会喜欢真实你的人却看不到真实的你，所以他们就错过了你，对吧？你只想要你尊重的极少数人的尊重。试图从大众那里要求尊重是傻瓜的差事。

turns.en: Status games, the allure of accruing, whether it's fame, actual fame, or just the competition comparison trap, it's always there. There's a real draw of being swayed by social approval. How should people learn to get less distracted by status games in that way?
turns.zh: 地位游戏，积累的诱惑，无论是名声、实际的名声，还是竞争比较的陷阱，它总是存在。被社会认可所左右确实很有吸引力。人们应该如何学会不被地位游戏分心呢？

turns.en: I think it just helps to see that status games don't matter as much as they used to. In old society, let's go back hunter-gatherer times, there was no such thing as wealth. You just had what you could carry. There was no stored wealth. So wealth games didn't really exist; wealth creation games didn't exist. All that existed was status games. If you were high status, then you got what little was available first. But even back then, you had to earn your status by taking care of the tribe. Now we have wealth creation where you can actually create a product or a service. You can scale that product or service and you can provide abundance for a lot of people. And that's not zero sum, that's a positive sum game. I can be wealthy, you can be wealthy, we can create things together and clearly since we are all collectively far far wealthier than we were in hunter-gatherer times. Wealth creation is positive but status is limited. There's limited status to go around. It's a ranking ladder. It's a hierarchy. And so it's a rise in status; somebody else has a lower status. Now you can have multiple kinds of status. So you can expand some kinds of status, but it's not like wealth creation where it can go infinitely where we can all be, you know, living in the stars and moon bases or Mars colonies or what have you. So just realize that status games are inherently limited. They're always combative. They always require direct combat whereas wealth creation games can be just you're creating products. You don't have to fight anybody else. Yes, in the marketplace your product has to succeed, but that's not quite the same as invective against other people or being angry with other people or feeling pushed down or pushed up or having a beef with somebody. So, I would argue that wealth creation games are both more pleasant. They're positive sum and they actually have concrete material returns. If you have more money, you can buy more. Show me where you can exchange your status at the bank.
turns.zh: 我认为只要认识到地位游戏不像过去那么重要了，就会有所帮助。在旧社会，让我们回到狩猎采集时代，那时没有财富这种东西。你只有你能携带的东西。没有储存的财富。所以财富游戏并不真正存在；财富创造游戏也不存在。存在的只有地位游戏。如果你地位高，那么你就能先得到那一点点可用的东西。但即使在那时，你也必须通过照顾部落来赢得地位。现在我们有了财富创造，你可以真正创造产品或服务。你可以扩展那个产品或服务，为许多人提供富足。这不是零和游戏，而是正和游戏。我可以富有，你也可以富有，我们可以一起创造东西，显然我们所有人比狩猎采集时代要富有很多很多倍。财富创造是正和的，但地位是有限的。能分配的地位是有限的。它是一个排名阶梯，一个等级制度。所以地位的上升意味着别人地位下降。现在你可以有多种地位，所以你可以扩展某些类型的地位，但这不像财富创造可以无限增长，让我们都能住在星星上、月球基地或火星殖民地之类的。所以只要认识到地位游戏本质上是有限的。它们总是对抗性的，总是需要直接战斗，而财富创造游戏可以只是你创造产品。你不需要和任何人争斗。是的，在市场上你的产品必须成功，但这与对别人进行谩骂、对别人生气、感觉被贬低或抬高、或与某人结仇完全不同。所以我认为财富创造游戏更令人愉快。它们是正和的，而且实际上有具体的物质回报。如果你有更多钱，你可以买更多东西。告诉我你能在银行用地位兑换什么。

turns.en: Exactly. Yeah. It's vague and it's fuzzy. Now, you see people get rich, they have money, what do they want? They want status. So they go to Hollywood, start starring in movies, they donate to nonprofits, they go to KS or Davos or what have you. And they start trying to trade the money for status. So, you know, people always want what they don't have. And we are evolutionarily hardwired for status because as I said, wealth creation didn't really exist until the agricultural revolution, when you could store grain and then the industrial revolution took it to another level and now the information age is taking it to yet another level. But there's never been an easier time to make money. Yes, it's still hard, but there's never been an easier time to create wealth because there's so much leverage out there. There's so much opportunity. You still have to go find it. It's not easy. It's not going to fall on your lap, and you have to learn something and know something and do something interesting. But nevertheless, it's possible to many more people. A few hundred years ago, you were born a serf, you were going to die a serf. There was almost no way out of that. That's changed. And so I would argue that you're better off focusing on wealth games than status games. If you're trying to build up, for example, your following on a social network and get famous and then get rich off of being famous, that's a much harder path than getting rich first. And then go for your fame afterwards would be my advice.
turns.zh: 没错。是的。它模糊而含混。现在，你看到人们变富了，他们有钱了，他们想要什么？他们想要地位。所以他们去好莱坞，开始主演电影，他们向非营利组织捐款，他们去KS或达沃斯之类的地方。他们开始试图用钱换取地位。所以，你知道，人们总是想要他们没有的东西。我们在进化上天生就追求地位，因为正如我所说，财富创造直到农业革命才真正存在，那时你可以储存谷物，然后工业革命把它提升到另一个水平，现在信息时代又把它提升到另一个水平。但从来没有比现在更容易赚钱的时候了。是的，仍然很难，但从来没有比现在更容易创造财富的时候，因为有那么多杠杆可用。有那么多机会。你仍然需要去找到它。这并不容易，它不会掉到你腿上，你必须学习一些东西，知道一些东西，做一些有趣的事情。但尽管如此，它对更多人来说是可能的。几百年前，你生为农奴，死为农奴。几乎没有出路。这已经改变了。所以我认为你最好专注于财富游戏而不是地位游戏。如果你试图，比如说，在社交网络上建立粉丝群，出名，然后靠出名致富，那是一条比先致富更艰难的道路。然后之后再去追求名声，这是我的建议。

turns.en: Well, a lot of people do that as you said. It's funny how people who have achieved such a level of wealth that you don't think why do you need the status given that most people use status to then try and cash in to achieve wealth if you've achieved money already if you're post money or asset heavy as it's known. Why are you trying to go in the other direction?
turns.zh: 嗯，很多人确实像你说的那样做。有趣的是，那些已经达到如此财富水平的人，你会想为什么还需要地位，因为大多数人用地位来变现以获得财富，如果你已经有钱了，如果你已经过了财富阶段或者资产雄厚，为什么还要往相反的方向走呢？

turns.en: Well, as you said because we've got an illustrious history biologically of wanting status and wealth is kind of novel. It's new. Wealth is something that you have to understand more intellectually.
turns.zh: 嗯，正如你所说，因为我们在生物学上有着追求地位的辉煌历史，而财富是某种新奇的东西。它是新的。财富是你需要更理性地理解的东西。

turns.en: Yeah, there's a physical component, more food, more survival, but to truly understand the effects and the powers and the abilities and limitations and the advantages and disadvantages of wealth, you have to use your neocortex a lot more.
turns.zh: 是的，财富有物质层面，比如更多食物、更多生存机会，但要真正理解财富的影响、力量、能力、局限以及优缺点，你需要更多地使用你的新皮层。

turns.en: Does that mean it's not limbic? The reason to play the game is to win the game and be done with it is harder to win and be done with for status than it is for wealth. That's a good observation. I had thought that through, but you're right.
turns.zh: 这是否意味着它不是边缘系统驱动的？玩这个游戏是为了赢并结束它，但地位比财富更难赢并结束。这是个很好的观察。我想过这一点，但你是对的。

turns.en: Yeah, I think that's right. I think people will always want more status. But I think you can be satisfied at a certain level of wealth.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为没错。我觉得人们总是想要更多地位，但财富达到一定水平后，你可以感到满足。

turns.en: Well, as well you always have this sort of sense. This is what leaderboards are, right? This is the Billboard chart, right? And it is zero sum and it is, I guess, you know, the Forbes richest people on the planet thing. That one's harder to climb the ladder on. But the fact that for example iTunes and YouTube can put you in competition against your contemporaries every single day and make you go up and down and show you likes and comments and ratings. This is how much you're up.
turns.zh: 嗯，你总是有这种感觉。这就是排行榜的意义，对吧？比如公告牌榜单。它是零和的，我想，你知道，福布斯全球富豪榜也是如此。那个榜单更难爬上去。但像iTunes和YouTube这样的平台，每天都能让你与同代人竞争，让你上下波动，展示你的点赞、评论和评分。这就是你上升了多少。

turns.en: Exactly. They keep you running on that treadmill forever. Jimmy Carr has this cool idea where he says trajectory is more important than position. So if you are number 101 in the world but last year you were number 200 versus you're number two in the world but last year you were number one, there is this sense of deceleration that is very, very tangible. And it's again it goes back to evolution, you know, something that is bleeding eventually dies unless you stop the bleeding. So you're hardwired not to lose what you have, and because we evolve in conditions where we're so close to just not surviving, you don't want to give anything up. It's hardwired into us to not give anything up. So you grip tightly.
turns.zh: 没错。它们让你永远在那台跑步机上奔跑。吉米·卡尔有个很棒的想法，他说轨迹比位置更重要。所以，如果你是世界第101名，但去年你是第200名，相比之下，如果你是世界第二，但去年你是第一，这种减速感非常非常真实。这又回到了进化论，你知道，流血的东西最终会死，除非你止血。所以你天生就不想失去已有的东西，因为我们在几乎无法生存的条件下进化，你不想放弃任何东西。这刻在我们的基因里，所以你会紧紧抓住。

turns.en: That's right. If you struggle to stay asleep because your body gets too hot or too cold, this is going to help. Just add an Eight Sleep Pod 4 Ultra to your mattress like a fitted sheet and it will automatically cool down or warm up each side of your bed up to 20°. It's got integrated sensors that track your sleep time, your sleep phases, your HRV, your snoring, and your heart rate with 99% accuracy. It even starts cooling or heating your bed an hour before you get into it. That is why Eight Sleep has been clinically proven to increase total sleep up to 1 hour every night. Best of all, they've got a 30-day sleep trial, so you can buy it and sleep on it for 29 nights, and if you don't like it, they will give you your money back. Plus, they ship internationally. Right now, you can get $350 off the Pod 4 Ultra by going to the link in the description below or heading to 8sleep.com/modernwisdom and using the code modernwisdom at checkout. That's 8sleep.com/modernwisdom and modernwisdom at checkout.
turns.zh: 没错。如果你因为身体太热或太冷而难以入睡，这个会有帮助。只需将Eight Sleep Pod 4 Ultra像床单一样铺在床垫上，它就会自动将床的每一侧冷却或加热至20度。它内置传感器，能以99%的准确率追踪你的睡眠时间、睡眠阶段、心率变异性、打鼾和心率。它甚至会在你上床前一小时开始冷却或加热你的床。这就是为什么Eight Sleep被临床证明能每晚增加多达1小时的睡眠。最棒的是，他们有30天睡眠试用期，所以你可以购买并睡上29晚，如果不喜欢，他们会退款。此外，他们提供国际配送。现在，通过下方描述中的链接或访问8sleep.com/modernwisdom并使用优惠码modernwisdom结账，即可获得Pod 4 Ultra 350美元的折扣。网址是8sleep.com/modernwisdom，结账时使用modernwisdom。

turns.en: The worst outcome in the world is not having self-esteem. Why?
turns.zh: 世界上最糟糕的结果是没有自尊。为什么？

turns.en: Yeah, that's a tough one. Well, I look at the people and I don't want to offend anybody, but I look at the people who don't like themselves, and that's the toughest slot because they're always wrestling with themselves, and it's hard enough to face the outside world. And no one's going to like you more than you like yourself. So, if you're struggling with yourself, then the outside world becomes an insurmountable challenge. And it's hard to say why people have low self-esteem. It might be genetic. It might just be circumstantial. A lot of times I think it's because they just weren't unconditionally loved as a child and that sort of seeps in at a deep core level. But self-esteem issues can be the most limiting.
turns.zh: 嗯，这是个难题。我观察人们，我不想冒犯任何人，但我看到那些不喜欢自己的人，那是最艰难的处境，因为他们总是在与自己斗争，而面对外部世界已经够难了。没有人会比你自己更喜欢你。所以，如果你与自己斗争，那么外部世界就变成了不可逾越的挑战。很难说为什么人们自尊心低。可能是遗传，也可能只是环境所致。很多时候我认为是因为他们小时候没有得到无条件的爱，这深深渗透到核心层面。但自尊问题可能是最限制性的。

turns.en: One interesting thought is that to some extent self-esteem is a reputation you have with yourself. You're watching yourself at all times. You know what you're doing and you have your own moral code. Everyone has a different moral code. But if you don't live up to your own moral code, the same code that you hold others to, it will damage your self-esteem. So perhaps one way to build up your self-esteem is to live up to your own code very rigorously. Have one and then live up to it.
turns.zh: 一个有趣的想法是，在某种程度上，自尊是你对自己的声誉。你一直在观察自己。你知道自己在做什么，你有自己的道德准则。每个人都有不同的道德准则。但如果你不遵守自己的道德准则，也就是你要求别人遵守的同一准则，那就会损害你的自尊。所以，也许建立自尊的一个方法就是严格遵循自己的准则。先有一个准则，然后去遵守它。

turns.en: Another way to raise your self-esteem might be to do things for others. If I look back on my life and you know what are the moments that I'm actually proud of, there's very far and few between and it's not that often and it's not the things you would expect. It's not the material success. It's not having learned this thing or that. It's when I made a sacrifice for somebody or something that I loved. And that's when I'm actually, ironically, most proud. Now that's through an explicit mental exercise. But I'll bet you at some level I'm recording that implicitly. So that tells me that even if I am not being loved, the way to create love is to give love, to express love through sacrifice and through duty. And so I think doing things like that can build up your self-esteem really fast.
turns.zh: 提升自尊的另一个方法可能是为他人做事。如果我回顾我的一生，你知道哪些时刻我真正感到自豪，那非常少，而且不是你想的那样。不是物质上的成功，也不是学到了这个或那个。而是当我为我所爱的人或事做出牺牲的时候。讽刺的是，那才是我最自豪的时候。现在这是通过明确的心理练习得出的。但我敢打赌，在某种程度上，我在潜意识里记录着这一点。所以这告诉我，即使我没有被爱，创造爱的方式就是付出爱，通过牺牲和责任来表达爱。所以我认为做这样的事情可以很快建立你的自尊。

turns.en: It's interesting when you talk about sacrifice because a lot of the time people say, "I sacrificed so much for my job." It's like, "Yeah, but that was you sacrificing something that you wanted less for something that you wanted more" as opposed to genuinely taking some sort of cost. And I wonder whether self-esteem is you adhering to your internal, your actions and your values aligning even when it's difficult, or perhaps even more so when it's difficult. I wonder whether there is a price that people who are more introspective, high integrity pay because you think well you've got this heavy set of overheads that you need to pay in some way.
turns.zh: 有趣的是你谈到牺牲，因为很多时候人们说：“我为工作牺牲了很多。”但那是你为了更想要的东西牺牲了不那么想要的东西，而不是真正承担某种代价。我想知道自尊是否就是你在困难时，甚至更困难时，坚持你的内在，使你的行动和价值观保持一致。我想知道那些更内省、高度正直的人是否付出了代价，因为你觉得你有一大笔开销需要以某种方式支付。

turns.en: Well, if being ethical were profitable, everybody would do it, right? So, at some level it does involve a sacrifice. But that sacrifice can also be thought of as you're thinking for the long term rather than the short term. For example, the virtues are a set of beliefs that if everybody in society followed them as individuals it would lead to win-win outcomes for everybody. So if I am honest and you are honest then we can do business more easily. We can interact more easily because we can trust each other. So even though there might be a few liars in the system, as long as there aren't too many liars and too many cheaters, a high trust society where everybody's honest is better off. And I think a lot of the virtues work this way, right? If I don't go around sleeping with your wife and you don't sleep with mine, and if I don't take all the food that's at the table first and so on, then we all get along better and we can play win-win games. In game theory, the most famous game is prisoner's dilemma. But that's all about everybody cheating and the Nash equilibrium. The stable equilibrium there is everybody cheats and the only way you can play a win-win game is if you have long-term iterated moves. But that's not actually the most common game played in society.
turns.zh: 嗯，如果道德能带来利润，每个人都会去做，对吧？所以，在某种程度上，它确实涉及牺牲。但这种牺牲也可以看作是你为长期而非短期考虑。例如，美德是一套信念，如果社会中的每个人都作为个体遵循它们，就会为每个人带来双赢的结果。所以，如果我诚实，你也诚实，我们就能更容易地做生意。我们可以更容易地互动，因为我们可以互相信任。所以，即使系统中可能有少数说谎者，只要说谎者和欺骗者不太多，一个高度信任、每个人都诚实的社会会更好。我认为很多美德都是这样运作的，对吧？如果我不去勾引你的妻子，你也不勾引我的；如果我不先拿走桌上所有的食物等等，那么我们就能更好地相处，玩双赢的游戏。在博弈论中，最著名的游戏是囚徒困境。但那完全是关于每个人都作弊和纳什均衡。那里的稳定均衡是每个人都作弊，而你能玩双赢游戏的唯一方式是通过长期重复的互动。但这实际上并不是社会中最常见的游戏。

transcript.sec: Stag Hunt and High Trust Society

turns.en: The most common game played is called a stag hunt, where if we cooperate, we can bring down a big stag and both have big dinners, but if we don't cooperate, then we have to go hunt rabbits and we each have small dinners. That game has two stable equilibriums: one where we're both hunting the rabbit, and one where we're hunting the stag. So the high trust society is a more virtuous society where I can trust you to come hunt the stag with me, show up on time, do the work, and divide it up properly. You want to live in a system where everybody has their own set of virtues and follows them, and then we all win. But I would argue you don't need to do that for sacrifice. You don't need to do that for other people. You can do it purely for yourself. You will have higher self-esteem. You will attract other high virtue people.
turns.zh: 最常见的博弈叫‘猎鹿博弈’：如果合作，我们能猎到一头大鹿，两人都吃大餐；如果不合作，就只能各自去抓兔子，吃小餐。这个博弈有两个稳定均衡：一个是两人都抓兔子，另一个是两人都猎鹿。所以高信任社会是一个更道德的社会，我可以信任你和我一起去猎鹿，准时出现，干活，然后公平分配。你希望生活在一个每个人都有一套自己的美德并遵循它、最终大家都赢的系统里。但我认为你不需要为了牺牲而这样做，不需要为了别人而这样做。你可以纯粹为了自己这样做。你会拥有更高的自尊，也会吸引其他高美德的人。

turns.en: Would I go on a stag hunt with me? Correct. Yeah, that's right. And if you're the kind of person who long-term signals ethics and virtues, then you will attract other people who are ethical and virtuous. Whereas, if you are a shark, you will eventually find yourself swimming entirely amongst sharks. And that's an unpleasant existence. But again, this goes back to the equivalent of the marshmallow test. And by the way, the marshmallow test does not replicate. I saw it replication crisis hard recently. But it is about trading off the short term for the long term. And so I think for a lot of these so-called virtues, there are long-term selfish reasons to be virtuous.
turns.zh: 我会和自己一起去猎鹿吗？没错，就是这样。如果你是那种长期传递道德和美德信号的人，你就会吸引其他道德高尚的人。相反，如果你是条鲨鱼，你最终会发现身边全是鲨鱼，那是一种令人不快的存在。但这又回到了棉花糖测试的类比。顺便说一句，棉花糖测试无法复现，我最近看到它陷入了可重复性危机。但这关乎用短期换取长期。所以我认为，对于很多所谓的美德，长期来看，自私的理由就是要有美德。

turns.en: Did you deal with self-doubt in the past? Is that something that was a hurdle for you to overcome?
turns.zh: 你过去有过自我怀疑吗？这是你需要克服的障碍吗？

turns.en: Yes and no. I think I dealt with self-doubt in the sense that, oh, I don't know what I'm doing and I need to figure it out. But I didn't doubt myself in the way of somebody else knows better than me for me or that I'm an idiot or I'm not worthwhile. I guess I had the benefit of growing up with a lot of love, like the people around me loved me unconditionally. And so that just gave me a lot of confidence. Not the kind of confidence that would say I have the answer, but the kind of confidence that I will figure it out and I know what I want, or only I am a good arbiter of what I want.
turns.zh: 既是也不是。我想我确实有过自我怀疑，比如‘我不知道自己在做什么，我需要弄清楚’。但我没有那种‘别人比我更了解我’或‘我是个白痴、我不值得’的自我怀疑。我想我受益于在充满爱的环境中长大，周围的人无条件地爱我。这给了我很多自信。不是那种‘我有答案’的自信，而是那种‘我会弄明白，我知道自己想要什么，只有我自己才能判断我想要什么’的自信。

turns.en: That level of self-belief, I suppose, allows you to determine what is it that matters to me, my self-esteem, should I chase this thing or not? I can make a fair judgment on that as opposed to being so swayed. But it's such a good point about even if you think you're not consciously logging the stuff that you're doing, there is some that's in the back of your mind. Was it the Damon? Is that what the ancient Greeks or something used to talk about?
turns.zh: 这种程度的自信，我想，能让你判断什么对我重要、我的自尊、我是否应该追求某件事？我能做出公正的判断，而不是轻易被左右。但你说得对，即使你认为自己没有有意识地记录自己做的事情，有些东西也在你脑海深处。是‘代蒙’吗？古希腊人是不是谈论过这个？

turns.en: Yeah. Also in computer science there's a concept of a demon, which is a program that's always running in the background. You can't see it. But yeah, it probably comes from the ancient Greek daemon. But yeah, what you know that you don't even know you know is far greater than what you know you know, right? You can't even articulate most of the things you know. There are feelings you have that have no words for them. There are thoughts you have that are felt within the body or subconsciously that you never articulate to yourself. You can't articulate the rules of grammar yet you exercise them effortlessly when you speak. So I would argue that your implicit knowledge and your knowledge that is unknown to yourself is far greater than the knowledge you can articulate and communicate. And so at some level you're always watching yourself. That's what your consciousness is, right? It's the thing that's watching everything including your mind, including your body. So if you want to have high self-esteem, then earn your own self-respect.
turns.zh: 是的。在计算机科学中也有‘守护进程’的概念，它是一个一直在后台运行的程序，你看不到它。但它可能源自古希腊的‘代蒙’。但你知道的、甚至你自己都不知道自己知道的东西，远比你意识到的要多得多，对吧？你甚至无法表达你知道的大部分东西。有些感觉你找不到词语来形容。有些想法你只在身体里或潜意识里感受到，从未对自己表达过。你无法说出语法规则，但说话时却能毫不费力地运用它们。所以我认为，你的隐性知识和你自己都不知道的知识，远大于你能表达和沟通的知识。因此在某种程度上，你一直在观察自己。这就是你的意识，对吧？它是在观察一切的东西，包括你的思想、你的身体。所以如果你想拥有高自尊，那就赢得自己的尊重。

turns.en: I had this idea the internal golden rule. So the golden rule says treat others the way that you should be treated. You want to be treated. The internal golden rule says treat yourself like others should have treated you and it was a repost to maybe people that didn't grow up with unconditional love.
turns.zh: 我有个想法叫‘内在黄金法则’。黄金法则是‘己所不欲，勿施于人’，你希望别人怎么对待你。内在黄金法则说的是‘像别人本应对待你的那样对待自己’，这可能是对那些没有在无条件爱中长大的人的一种回应。

transcript.sec: The Feeling of Love: Being in Love vs. Being Loved

turns.en: On the love thing, one of the interesting things about love is you can try to remember the feeling of being loved. So go back to when someone was in love with you or someone did love you and really remember that feeling, really sit with it and try to recreate it within yourself. And then go to the feeling of you loving someone and when you were in love. And I'm not even talking about romantic love necessarily. So be a little careful there. I'm talking more about love for a sibling or a child or something like that or a parent. And think about when you felt love towards someone or something. And now which is better? And I would argue that the feeling of being in love is actually more exhilarating than the feeling of being loved. Being loved is a little clawing. It's a little too sweet. You kind of want to push the person away. It's a little embarrassing. And you know that if that person is too much into it that you feel constrained. On the other hand, the feeling of being in love is very expansive. It's very open. It actually makes you a better version of yourself. It makes you want to be a better person. And so you can create love anytime you want. It's just that craving to receive it. That's the problem.
turns.zh: 关于爱，有趣的一点是你可以试着回忆被爱的感觉。回想一下有人爱着你的时候，真正记住那种感觉，沉浸其中，试着在自己内心重新创造它。然后转向你爱别人、你陷入爱河时的感觉。我甚至不一定在说浪漫爱情，所以小心一点。我更多说的是对兄弟姐妹、孩子或父母的爱。想想你曾经对某人或某物感到爱的时候。现在，哪个更好？我认为，陷入爱河的感觉实际上比被爱的感觉更令人振奋。被爱有点黏人，有点太甜了，你有点想推开那个人，有点尴尬。而且你知道，如果那个人太投入，你会感到束缚。另一方面，陷入爱河的感觉非常开阔，非常开放。它实际上让你成为更好的自己，让你想成为一个更好的人。所以你可以随时创造爱，只是渴望接受爱才是问题所在。

transcript.sec: Pride as the Most Expensive Trait

turns.en: The most expensive trait is pride. How come?
turns.zh: 最昂贵的特质是骄傲。为什么？

turns.en: Oh, that was a recent one. I tweeted that just because I think that pride is the enemy of learning. So when I look at my friends and colleagues, the ones who are still stuck in the past and have grown the least are the ones who were the proudest because they sort of feel like they already had the answers. And so they don't want to correct themselves publicly. And so this goes back to the fame conversation. You get locked into something you said. It made you famous. You're known for that. And now you want to pivot or change. So pride prevents you from saying I'm wrong.
turns.zh: 哦，那是最近的一条推文。我发那条推文是因为我认为骄傲是学习的大敌。当我观察我的朋友和同事时，那些仍然停留在过去、成长最少的人，往往是最骄傲的，因为他们觉得自己已经有了答案。所以他们不想公开纠正自己。这又回到了关于名声的讨论。你被自己说过的话困住了，那句话让你出名，你因此被熟知。现在你想转向或改变，但骄傲阻止你说‘我错了’。

turns.en: What's pride in this context here?
turns.zh: 在这个语境下，骄傲是什么？

turns.en: It could be as simple as you're trading stocks and then you don't admit you were wrong. So you hang on to a lousy trade. It could be that you made a decision to marry someone or move somewhere or enter a profession, it doesn't work out and then you don't want to admit that you were wrong so you get stuck in it. It's mostly about getting trapped in local maxima as opposed to going back down and climbing up the mountain again.
turns.zh: 它可以很简单，比如你在交易股票，不承认自己错了，于是死守着一笔糟糕的交易。也可能是你决定和某人结婚、搬到某个地方或进入某个行业，结果不顺利，但你不愿承认错误，于是被困在里面。这主要是关于陷入局部最优，而不是退下来重新爬山。

turns.en: And that's why it's an expensive trade because you continue to need to repay it in one form or another.
turns.zh: 这就是为什么它是一笔昂贵的交易，因为你需要以某种形式不断偿还它。

turns.en: Yeah. You're just stuck at a suboptimal point. It's going to cost you money, success, and time.
turns.zh: 是的。你被困在一个次优的点上。它会让你付出金钱、成功和时间的代价。

turns.en: The great artists always have this ability to start over, whether it's Paul Simon or Madonna. And even the great entrepreneurs are just always willing to start over. I'm always struck by the Elon Musk story where he did PayPal as X.com originally. Actually, it was his financial institution that got merged into PayPal. It's good that you've got the domain. He's consistent. He's been using it for quite a while. And he said something like, I made $200 million from the sale of PayPal. I put $100 million into SpaceX, $80 million into Tesla, $20 million into SolarCity, and I had to borrow money for rent. This guy is a perennial risk taker. He's always willing to start over. He doesn't have any pride about being seen as successful or as a failure. He's willing to put it all in and back himself again each time. But the key thing is he's always willing to start over. Even now, his new startup is the USA, right? He's basically trying to fix it like he would fix one of his startups. And I think that is a willingness to look like a fool and a willingness to start over. A lot of people just don't have that. They become successful or rich or famous, and that's it. They're stuck. They don't want to go back to zero. And creating anything great requires zero to one, which means going back to zero, and that's really painful and hard to do.
turns.zh: 伟大的艺术家总是有能力重新开始，无论是保罗·西蒙还是麦当娜。甚至伟大的企业家也总是愿意重新开始。埃隆·马斯克的故事一直让我印象深刻，他最初做的是X.com，也就是PayPal。实际上，他的金融机构被合并进了PayPal。还好你保留了那个域名。他挺执着的，用了很久。他说过类似这样的话：我从出售PayPal中赚了2亿美元，我把1亿投进SpaceX，8000万投进特斯拉，2000万投进SolarCity，然后还得借钱付房租。这家伙是个永远的风险承担者。他总愿意重新开始。他不在乎被人看作成功还是失败。他愿意全押进去，每次都再赌自己一把。关键是，他总愿意重新开始。即使现在，他的新创业项目就是美国，对吧？他基本上是在像修复自己的创业公司一样修复它。我认为这就是一种愿意看起来像个傻瓜、愿意重新开始的态度。很多人就是做不到。他们成功了、有钱了、出名了，然后就停在那里了。他们不想回到零。而创造任何伟大的东西都需要从零到一，这意味着你要回到零，这真的很痛苦，很难做到。

turns.en: Talking about risk, something I've been thinking about a lot related to you. Any moment when you're not having a good time, when you're not really happy, you're not doing anyone any favors. I think lots of people have become unusually familiar with suffering silently in that sort of way, not having a high bar for your expectation for quality of life. A lot of it is just you're memeing yourself into a bad outcome because you think that somehow suffering is glorious or that it makes you a better person. My old quip was: if you're so smart, why aren't you happy? Why can't you figure that one out? The reality is you can be smart and happy. There are plenty of people in human history who are smart and happy. And I think it just starts with saying, you know what, I'm going to be happy. There was a guy I met in Thailand a long time ago, and he used to work for Tony Robbins. He had a great attitude. We were sitting around and he said, I realized one day that someone out there had to be the happiest person in the world. That person just has to exist. He said, why not me? I'll take on that burden. I'll be that guy. I heard that and thought, wow, that's pretty good. That's a good frame. He knew how to reframe things. So I think a lot of happiness is just a choice in the sense that you first identify yourself as someone who is going to be happy. You're going to figure it out. You're not going to lose your other predilections. You're not going to lose your ambition or your desire for success. I think a lot of people have this fear that if I'm happy, then I won't want to be successful. No, you'll just want to do things that are more aligned with the happy version of you, and you'll be successful at those things. And believe me, the happy version of you is not going to look back at the unhappy version and say, oh man, that guy was going to be more successful. I wish I was him. You're actually trying to be successful so you'll be happy. That's the whole point. You've got it backwards.
turns.zh: 说到风险，我一直在想和你有关的一件事。任何你过得不好、不开心的时候，你其实对谁都没好处。我觉得很多人已经异常习惯于默默忍受，对自己的生活质量期望不高。很多时候，你是在用自我暗示把自己推向糟糕的结局，因为你认为某种程度的痛苦是光荣的，或者能让你成为更好的人。我过去常打趣说：如果你这么聪明，为什么还不快乐？为什么你就想不通呢？现实是，你可以既聪明又快乐。人类历史上有大把聪明又快乐的人。我认为这始于你对自己说：你知道吗，我要快乐。很久以前我在泰国遇到一个人，他以前为托尼·罗宾斯工作。他态度很好。我们坐着聊天，他说：有一天我意识到，世界上必须有一个人是最快乐的。那个人一定存在。他说：为什么不能是我呢？我来承担这个负担，我来做那个人。我听了之后想，哇，这真不错。这是个好框架。他知道如何重新定义事物。所以我认为快乐在很大程度上是一种选择，首先你认定自己是一个会快乐的人。你会找到方法的。你不会失去其他偏好，不会失去野心或对成功的渴望。我觉得很多人害怕：如果我快乐了，我就不想成功了。不，你只会想做更符合快乐版本的你的事情，而且你会在那些事情上成功。相信我，快乐的你不会回头看那个不快乐的你，说：哎呀，那家伙本来会更成功的，我真希望我是他。你其实是为了快乐才追求成功的。这才是重点。你把顺序搞反了。

turns.en: You unlocked one of my trap cards. One of my favorite insights is that we sacrifice the thing we want for the thing that's supposed to get it. So we sacrifice happiness in order to be successful so that when we're finally sufficiently successful, we can actually be happy. And if you have some sort of simultaneous equation and you just sort of stripped success off from both sides, at least in my own life, I have not found there to be a trade-off. If anything, I have found that the happier I get, the more I am going to do the things that I'm good at and aligned with, and that will make me even happier. So I actually end up more successful, not less.
turns.zh: 你触发了我的一张陷阱卡。我最喜欢的洞见之一是：我们为了得到想要的东西而牺牲了那个东西本身。所以我们为了成功而牺牲快乐，以为等我们足够成功了，就能真正快乐。如果你列一个联立方程，把成功从两边都去掉，至少在我的生活中，我没有发现存在权衡。相反，我发现我越快乐，就越会去做那些我擅长且契合的事情，而这会让我更快乐。所以我实际上变得更成功，而不是更不成功。

turns.en: The aligned with thing is interesting. I'm going to try and put this across as delicately as I can. From the bit of time we've spent together, you have a really interesting trait of holistic selfishness. You're sort of prepared to put yourself first. You seem largely unfazed by saying or doing things that might result in other people feeling a little bit awkward if it's truthful for you. It's like unapologetically self-prioritizing, I guess.
turns.zh: “契合”这一点很有意思。我尽量说得委婉些。从我们相处的这段时间来看，你有一个非常有趣的特质，就是整体性的自私。你似乎准备好把自己放在第一位。如果对你来说是真实的，你似乎不太在意说或做一些可能让别人感到尴尬的事情。这大概就是毫无歉意地优先考虑自己吧。

turns.en: Yeah, I think everybody is. Maybe unapologetic is the part that's relatively rare, but I think everybody puts themselves first. That's just human nature. You're here because you survived. You're a separate organism. That's interesting. I'm maybe. But I know we like to virtue signal and pretend we're doing it for each other. How many times does somebody say, 'Yeah, of course. I'd love to come to the wedding,' when they're like, 'I don't want to be at the wedding.' How many times does someone say, 'How are you doing today?' and they don't tell you. How many— I don't go to weddings. But this is my point.
turns.zh: 是啊，我觉得每个人都是。也许“毫无歉意”这部分相对少见，但我认为每个人都把自己放在第一位。这只是人性。你在这里是因为你活下来了。你是一个独立的有机体。这很有意思。也许我是这样。但我知道我们喜欢道德标榜，假装是在为对方做事。有多少次有人说：“当然，我很乐意参加婚礼。”而实际上他们心里想：“我不想参加婚礼。”有多少次有人问：“你今天怎么样？”而他们并不真的告诉你。有多少——我不去婚礼。但这就是我的观点。

turns.en: So I don't think you're necessarily right with that. I think that people do— I don't think they put themselves first. I sometimes think that they compromise what it is that they want in order to appease socially what's in front of them.
turns.zh: 所以我不认为你一定是正确的。我认为人们确实——我不认为他们把自己放在第一位。我有时觉得他们会为了迎合眼前的社交场合而妥协自己真正想要的东西。

turns.en: Yeah, I just view it as you're wasting everyone's time on it. Don't do something you don't want to do. Why are you wasting your time? There's so little time on this earth. Life goes fast. What is it? 4,000 weeks. That's your lifespan. And yes, we hear that, but we don't remember it. But I guess I'm keenly aware of how little time I have, so I'm just not going to waste it.
turns.zh: 是啊，我只是觉得你在浪费所有人的时间。不要做你不想做的事。你为什么在浪费时间？在这个地球上时间太少了。人生过得很快。是多少？4000周。那就是你的寿命。是的，我们听说过，但我们不记得。但我想我敏锐地意识到我的时间有多短，所以我就是不会浪费它。

turns.en: How have you gotten more comfortable at being the unapologetic self-prioritizer?
turns.zh: 你是如何变得更自在地做一个毫无歉意的自我优先者的？

turns.en: Yeah, I've gotten utterly more and more ruthless on it. Mainly it's that I see or hear people's freedom and then that liberates me further. So I read a blog post by P. Mark, aka Mark Andreessen, where he said 'don't keep a schedule,' and I took that to heart. So I deleted my calendar and I don't keep a schedule. I try to remember it all in my head. If I can't remember it, I'm not going to— I'm glad you got here on time. Yeah, exactly. I hate to look things up at the last minute. So, but ironically, I don't even know if Mark himself follows that, but he made the correct point.
turns.zh: 是的，我在这方面变得越来越无情。主要是因为看到或听到别人的自由，这进一步解放了我。我读过一篇马克·安德森写的博客，他说“不要保留日程表”，我深以为然。所以我删掉了日历，不保留日程表。我试着把所有事情记在脑子里。如果我记不住，我就不会——我很高兴你准时到了。是啊，没错。我讨厌最后一刻才去查东西。但讽刺的是，我甚至不知道马克自己是否遵循这一点，但他说得对。

turns.en: I read a little story about Jack Dorsey doing all his business off his iPhone and iPad and not even going into a Mac, and I said, 'Okay, I want to do that.' So, I'm going to operate through text messaging and not put up my nasty email. Does that feel like more freedom?
turns.zh: 我读到一个小故事，说杰克·多尔西只用 iPhone 和 iPad 处理所有业务，甚至不用 Mac，我就说：‘好吧，我也想那样做。’所以，我打算通过短信来操作，不再处理我那烦人的邮件。这感觉更自由吗？

turns.en: It does. Yeah. Because you're on the go. So, I have a nasty email autoresponder that says, 'I don't check email and don't text me either.' Right. If you need to find me, you'll find me. Obviously, some of this is a luxury of success, but some of these habits I adopted long before actually the hostile email autoresponder started a long time ago. I used to own the domain coffee.com. I let it go. I used to reply from that email just so people would get the point. But I stopped being rude about it. Now I just ghost. I just disappear. My wife knows not to ever book or schedule me for anything. I'm not expected to go to couples dinners. I'm not expected to go to birthdays. I'm not expected to go to weddings. If somebody tries to rope her into having me show up, she says he makes his own decisions. You got to ask him directly.
turns.zh: 确实。是的。因为你在移动中。所以，我有一个烦人的邮件自动回复，写着：‘我不查邮件，也别给我发短信。’对。如果你需要找我，你会找到我的。显然，这有些是成功的奢侈，但这些习惯我很久以前就养成了，那个敌意的邮件自动回复很早就开始了。我曾经拥有 coffee.com 这个域名，后来放弃了。我曾经用那个邮箱回复，只是为了让人明白我的意思。但我后来不再那么粗鲁了。现在我只是消失。我妻子知道永远不要为我预订或安排任何事情。我不需要参加夫妻晚餐，不需要参加生日聚会，不需要参加婚礼。如果有人试图让她说服我出席，她会说他自己做决定，你得直接问他。

turns.en: What about vice versa? Well, are you not killing serendipity in a way?
turns.zh: 反过来呢？难道你不会在某种程度上扼杀机缘巧合吗？

turns.en: No, no. I'm freeing up all my time. So, my entire life is serendipity. I get to interact with whoever I want, whenever I want, wherever. You'll hear the invite, but make the decision because if there are fewer things incoming, you're assuming that you know what's best for you in the future. So, I'll say, 'Okay, if that thing is interesting, I'll see if I can get in that day when I'm in the mood.' But there's nothing worse than something coming up that your past self committed you to that your present self doesn't want to do. God damn it. Past. Yeah. And then it destroys your entire calendar. It destroys your day because there's like, oh, this 1 hour slot which is sitting like a turd on my calendar that I have to schedule my whole day around. I can't do anything the 20 minutes before, the 20 minutes afterwards. Even for phone calls, if someone wants to do a phone call, say, 'Okay, just text me when you're free. I'll text you when I'm free.' and we'll just do it on the fly. It's a much better way of living than this overly scheduled you know cal.com or iCal whatever. The overscheduled life is not worth living. It's not. I think it's a terrible way to live life. That's not how we evolved. It's not how we grew up. It's not how we were as children hopefully unless you're a helicopter parent or a tiger mom. Your natural order is freedom. I had a friend who said to me once, you know, 'I never want to have to be at a specific place at a specific time.' And I was like, 'Oh my god, that's freedom.' When I heard that, that changed my life right there.
turns.zh: 不，不。我解放了我所有的时间。所以，我的整个生活都是机缘巧合。我可以随时随地和任何我想互动的人互动。你会收到邀请，但由你决定，因为如果进来的事情更少，你就假设你知道未来什么对你最好。所以，我会说：‘好吧，如果那件事有趣，我会看看那天我有没有心情去做。’但没有什么比过去自己承诺的事情，而现在的自己不想做更糟糕的了。该死的。过去。是的。然后它会毁掉你整个日历。它会毁掉你的一天，因为就像，哦，这一个小时的时段像坨屎一样坐在我的日历上，我必须围绕它安排我的一整天。前后二十分钟我什么都做不了。即使是电话，如果有人想打电话，就说：‘好吧，你有空时给我发短信，我有空时给你发短信。’然后我们临时通话。这比那种过度安排的 cal.com 或 iCal 之类的生活要好得多。过度安排的生活不值得过。真的不值得。我认为这是一种糟糕的生活方式。我们不是那样进化的。我们不是那样长大的。希望我们小时候也不是那样，除非你是直升机父母或虎妈。你的自然秩序是自由。我有一个朋友曾经对我说：‘我永远不想在特定的时间出现在特定的地点。’我当时想：‘哦，天哪，那就是自由。’听到那句话，那一刻改变了我的生活。

turns.en: You still alarm clockless?
turns.zh: 你仍然不用闹钟吗？

turns.en: Yes, I'm alarm clockless. Today, I did set my alarm clock just so I wouldn't miss this. Very important. Yeah. But just so you know, I set the alarm clock from 11:00 a.m. in case I was stricken with a flu that day. I wasn't going to set my alarm clock for 8:00 a.m. or 9:00 a.m. And sure enough, I got up many hours before that. But it was sort of a backup emergency alarm. In fact, sometimes when I have something that I need to do, I don't want to look at a calendar, so I'll just set an alarm for it.
turns.zh: 是的，我不用闹钟。今天，我确实设了闹钟，只是为了不错过这个。非常重要。是的。但你知道，我把闹钟设在上午 11 点，以防那天我得了流感。我不会把闹钟设在早上 8 点或 9 点。果然，我提前好几个小时就起床了。但这只是一个备用的紧急闹钟。事实上，有时我有事要做，不想看日历，我就直接设个闹钟。

turns.en: Just sink a little bit more into that kind of you energy, that self-prioritizing energy because I think people rationally love the idea of this. I'm going to do what only I want to do. Even if they've got the level of freedom, it's not you energy in the sense that I think everyone should live their life that way to the greatest extent possible. Obviously, we have our requirements around work and obligations that are genuinely important to us. But don't fritter away your life on randomly scheduled things and things that aren't important, don't matter, and events and weddings and you know tedious dinners with tedious people that you don't want to go to. To the extent you can bring freedom into your life, optimize for that, you'll actually be more productive. You won't just be happier and more free. You will be more productive because then you can focus on what is in front of you, whatever the biggest problem of that day. When I wake up in the morning, the first 4 hours are when I have the most energy and that's when I want to solve all the hard problems. And the next 4 hours are when I kind of want to do some more outdoorsy activities or I want to work out or maybe I can have some meetings, but I'll try to do those last second based on whatever the day's priorities demand. The last 4 hours I kind of want to wind down. I want to hang out with the kids and I want to play games or read a book or something like that. So having that flexibility and freedom is really important. So you can just put whatever is most needed into the slot at that moment. And instead if I have like a meeting at 2 p.m. and then I have to get a thing and some emails done, I put that off till 6 p.m. I'm rushing. I'm not going to be productive. I'm not going to be free. You're certainly not free. I'm definitely not free. But also another thing that I really believe is that inspiration is perishable. Act on it immediately. So when you're inspired to do something, do that thing. If I'm inspired to write a blog post, I want to do it at that moment. If I'm inspired to send a tweet, I want to do it at that moment. If I'm inspired to solve a problem, I do it that moment. If I'm inspired to read a book, I want to read it right then. If I'm inspired to solve a problem, I solve it right there. If I want to learn something, I do it at the moment of curiosity. The moment the curiosity arrives, I go learn that thing immediately. I download the book. I get on Google. I get on ChatGPT, whatever. I will figure that thing out on the spot and that's when the learning happens. It doesn't happen because I've scheduled time because I've set an hour aside because when that time arrives I might be in a different mood. I might just want to do something different. So I think that spontaneity is really important. You're going to learn best when you're having fun, when you generally are enjoying the process, not when you're forced to sit there and do it. How much do you remember from school? You know you were forced to learn geography, history, mathematics on this schedule at this time according to this person. Didn't happen. All the stuff that sticks with you is you learned it when you wanted to, when you genuinely had the desire. And that freedom, that ability to act on something the moment you want to is so liberating that most of us go through our lives with very very little tastes of that. If you live your entire life that way, that is a recipe for happiness. It feels like efficiency that you have efficient also.
turns.zh: 再深入一点那种‘你能量’，那种自我优先的能量，因为我认为人们理性上喜欢这个想法：我只做我想做的事。即使他们拥有一定程度的自由，也不是那种‘你能量’，我认为每个人都应该尽可能以那种方式生活。显然，我们有工作和义务的要求，这些对我们真正重要。但不要把你的生命浪费在随机安排的事情上，那些不重要、无关紧要的事情，以及活动、婚礼，还有你不想去的和无聊的人的无聊晚餐。尽可能把自由带入你的生活，为此优化，你实际上会更有效率。你不仅会更快乐、更自由，还会更有效率，因为那样你就可以专注于眼前的事情，无论那天最大的问题是什么。当我早上醒来时，前 4 个小时是我精力最充沛的时候，那时我想解决所有难题。接下来的 4 个小时，我可能想做更多的户外活动，或者锻炼，或者也许可以开一些会，但我会尽量根据当天优先事项的需求在最后一刻安排。最后 4 个小时，我想放松一下。我想和孩子们一起玩，玩游戏或读书之类的。所以拥有这种灵活性和自由非常重要。这样你就可以把最需要的事情放在那个时刻的时段里。相反，如果我下午 2 点有一个会议，然后我必须完成一件事和一些邮件，我会把它推迟到下午 6 点。我会很匆忙，不会有效率，也不会自由。你当然不自由。我绝对不自由。但另一件我深信不疑的事情是，灵感是易逝的。要立即行动。所以当你受到启发要做某事时，就去做那件事。如果我受到启发要写一篇博客文章，我想立刻写。如果我受到启发要发一条推文，我想立刻发。如果我受到启发要解决一个问题，我立刻解决。如果我受到启发要读一本书，我想立刻读。如果我受到启发要解决一个问题，我当场解决。如果我想学点什么，我在好奇的那一刻就去做。好奇心一出现，我立刻去学那个东西。我下载那本书，上谷歌，上 ChatGPT，无论什么。我会当场弄明白，那就是学习发生的时候。它不会因为我安排了时间、留出了一个小时而发生，因为当那个时间到来时，我可能心情不同，可能只想做别的事情。所以我认为自发性非常重要。当你玩得开心、享受过程时，你学得最好，而不是被迫坐在那里学。你从学校记得多少？你知道你被迫按照这个时间表、这个时间、根据这个人学习地理、历史、数学。没记住。所有你记住的东西都是你在想要的时候、真正有渴望的时候学到的。那种自由，那种在你想要的那一刻就行动的能力，是如此解放，以至于我们大多数人一生中很少尝到这种滋味。如果你一生都那样生活，那就是幸福的秘诀。感觉也很高效，你也很高效。

transcript.sec: Procrastination and Doing What You Want

turns.en: You have the inspiration that is going to be the most frictionless time to ever do that particular task. So, oh, I've had the inspiration to do that. I'll put that off until a time when I no longer really want to do it quite so much. And while I do want to do that thing, I'll do something else that I needed to do because it's on the schedule. It does not work. Procrastination is because you don't want to do that thing right now. You want to do something else. Go do that something else.
turns.zh: 你有了灵感，那将是做那件事最顺畅的时机。所以，哦，我有灵感做那件事。我会把它推迟到我不再那么想做的时候。而在我还想做那件事的时候，我会去做另一件我需要做的事，因为它在日程上。这行不通。拖延是因为你现在不想做那件事。你想做别的事。那就去做别的事。

turns.en: I reject this frame that efficiency and productivity and success are counter to happiness and freedom. They actually go together. How so?
turns.zh: 我拒绝这种认为效率、生产力和成功与幸福和自由对立的框架。它们实际上是相辅相成的。怎么说？

turns.en: The happier you are, the more you can sustain doing something, the more likely you're going to do something that will in turn make you even happier. and you'll continue to do it and you'll outwork everybody else. The more free you are, the better you can allocate your time and the less you're caught up in a web of obligations and commitments and the more you can focus on the task at hand.
turns.zh: 你越快乐，就越能持续做某件事，就越有可能做一件会让你更快乐的事。你会继续做下去，并且比任何人都努力。你越自由，就越能更好地分配时间，越少陷入义务和承诺的网中，越能专注于手头的任务。

turns.en: In other news, this episode is brought to you by Function. Staying on top of your health requires more than just an annual physical, which is why I partnered with Function. They run lab tests twice a year to track over a 100 biomarkers and monitor for early signs of thousands of diseases. They even screen for 50 types of cancer at stage one, which is five times more data than you get from an annual physical. You receive insights from a team of expert physicians who provide a detailed written clinician summary of all their observations and then phone consultations for any critical findings. Getting these lab tests done would usually cost thousands, but with function, it is only $499. And right now, you can get the exact same blood panels that I get and bypass their weight list by going to the link in the description below or heading to functionhealth.com/modernwisdom. That's functionhealth.com/modernwisdism.
turns.zh: 另外，本期节目由 Function 赞助。保持健康不仅仅需要年度体检，所以我与 Function 合作。他们每年进行两次实验室测试，追踪超过 100 个生物标志物，并监测数千种疾病的早期迹象。他们甚至在第一阶段筛查 50 种癌症，这比年度体检多出五倍的数据。你会收到来自专家医生团队的见解，他们提供所有观察结果的详细书面临床总结，并对任何关键发现进行电话咨询。这些实验室测试通常要花费数千美元，但通过 Function 只需 499 美元。现在，你可以通过下方描述中的链接或访问 functionhealth.com/modernwisdom 获得与我完全相同的血液检测，并绕过他们的等待名单。网址是 functionhealth.com/modernwisdom。

turns.en: This is related to another insight of yours. The less you want something, the less you're thinking about it, the less you're obsessing over it, the more you're going to do it in a natural way, the more you're going to do it for yourself, you're going to do it in a way that you're good at and you're going to stick with it. The people around you will see the quality of your work is higher. But this seems like a difficult tension to navigate because an obsessive attention to detail is a competitive advantage of your work as well. So you have these two things sort of conflicting with each other.
turns.zh: 这与你另一个见解有关。你越不想要某样东西，越不去想它，越不痴迷于它，你就越会自然地去做，越会为自己而做，你会以自己擅长的方式去做，并且坚持下去。你周围的人会看到你的工作质量更高。但这似乎是一个难以处理的张力，因为对细节的痴迷关注也是你工作的竞争优势。所以这两件事有点相互冲突。

turns.en: No one is going to beat you at being you if it So one of the things I like to say is like find what feels like play to you but looks like work to others. So it looks like work to them but to you it feels like play. It's not work. So you're going to out compete them because you're doing it effortlessly. You're doing it for fun. They're doing it for work. They're doing it for some byproduct. To you it's art. It's beauty. It's joy. It's it's flow. It's fulfilling. Uh, you must enjoy podcasting. If you didn't, you wouldn't be good at it. You would either, right? If you would, if if you decided that the right way to get ahead in life was to go write books, you would nobody would have heard of you. Chris Williamson's book would be a complete flop. That's not who you are. You're a podcaster. You enjoy talking to people. You enjoy interviewing them. The more you do things that are natural to you, the less competition you have. You escape competition through authenticity. by being your own self.
turns.zh: 没有人能在做你自己这件事上打败你。所以我喜欢说的一句话是：找到那些对你来说是游戏，但对别人来说是工作的事情。所以对他们来说是工作，但对你来说是游戏。这不是工作。所以你会胜过他们，因为你毫不费力地做。你为了乐趣而做。他们为了工作而做。他们为了某种副产品而做。对你来说，这是艺术，是美，是快乐，是心流，是满足。你一定喜欢做播客。如果你不喜欢，你就不会擅长。对吧？如果你决定在人生中取得成功的方式是写书，那么没有人会听说过你。克里斯·威廉姆森的书会彻底失败。那不是你。你是一个播客主播。你喜欢与人交谈，喜欢采访他们。你越做自己自然的事情，竞争就越少。你通过真实来逃避竞争，通过做你自己。

turns.en: If I had to summarize how to be successful in life in two words, I would just say productize yourself. That's it. Just figure out what it is that you naturally do that the world might want that you can scale up and turn into a product and it'll be it'll eventually be effortless for you. Yes, there's always work required, but it won't even feel like work to you. It'll feel like play to you. And modern society gives us that opportunity. You know, if you were 2,000 years ago, you're born in a farm. Your choices are very limited, right? You're going to do stuff on that farm. Now, you can literally wake up and you can move to a different city. You can switch careers. You can switch jobs. You can change the people that you're with. Uh, you know, you can change so many things about who you are and who you're with and what you're doing that there is infinite opportunity out there for you. Literally infinite. And so it's much better to treat this like a search function to find the people who need you the most, to find the work that needs you the most, to find the place you're best suited to be at. And it's worthwhile to spend time in that exploration before diving into exploitation.
turns.zh: 如果要用两个词来总结如何在生活中成功，我会说：产品化你自己。就是这样。弄清楚你天生擅长什么，世界可能需要什么，你可以将其规模化并转化为产品，最终对你来说会毫不费力。是的，总是需要努力，但对你来说甚至不会感觉像工作。它会感觉像游戏。现代社会给了我们这个机会。如果你生活在两千年前，出生在农场，你的选择非常有限。你只能在那农场干活。现在，你可以醒来就搬到另一个城市。你可以换职业，换工作，换身边的人。你可以改变关于你是谁、和谁在一起、做什么的很多事情，机会是无限的。真的是无限的。所以最好把它当作一个搜索功能，找到最需要你的人，最需要你的工作，最适合你的地方。在投入开发之前，花时间探索是值得的。

turns.en: The biggest mistake in a world with so many choices is premature commitment. If you prematurely commit to being a lawyer or a doctor and now you've got like, you know, 5 years invested into that, you might have just completely missed. You might just end up in the wrong profession, the wrong place, or the wrong people for 30 years of your life grinding away. And yes, the best time to figure that out was before, but the second best time is now. So, just change it. And also, presumably kill things that aren't working very quickly. By default, you should kill everything. You know, if you can't decide, the answer is no. Uh, and most things you should just be saying no to. The part of my keeping my calendar free is just by default saying no to everything. Do I want to create a calendar just to add your event, right? Or to add your need or your desire.
turns.zh: 在一个有这么多选择的世界里，最大的错误是过早承诺。如果你过早承诺成为一名律师或医生，现在你已经投入了五年，你可能完全错过了。你可能最终在错误的职业、错误的地方或错误的人身边，辛苦三十年。是的，最好的弄清楚的时间是之前，但第二好的时间是现在。所以，就改变吧。而且，大概要快速扼杀那些不奏效的事情。默认情况下，你应该扼杀一切。如果你无法决定，答案就是“不”。大多数事情你应该直接说“不”。我保持日程空闲的部分就是默认对一切说“不”。我是否要创建一个日程来添加你的活动？或者添加你的需求或愿望？

turns.en: One of the other things about, you know, early on in life, you're looking for opportunities. So, you're saying yes to everything. And that is a phase that you go through. That is the exploration phase. Later, when you found the thing you want to work on, you're in the exploitation phase. You have to say no to everything by default. And if you don't say no to everything by default, if you have to even explicitly go out of your way to say no to something, that will take up time. Uh, for example, you know, there there are a lot of people out there who are into hustle culture and and a big piece of hustle culture is like, well, you're not going to get something if you don't ask for it. So, they'll hustle people. They'll always be sending you requests, messages. Yeah, this is a famous person problem, but I have it. And people are always asking me for things. And I kind of squirm when I get these messages, and I'm sure you get these two text messages, emails saying, "Hey, Chris, my friend so and so should really be on your podcast, or you should come to my event. You should write a forward for my book." And you kind of squirm when you get this, right? You have to figure out how to say no. And one of the things I learned along the way is that if you wouldn't ask somebody else to do it and then you get that request yourself, you can just dismiss it. You don't have to respond.
turns.zh: 另一件事是，在人生早期，你在寻找机会。所以你对一切都说“是”。那是你经历的一个阶段，是探索阶段。后来，当你找到了想做的事情，你就进入了开发阶段。你必须默认对一切说“不”。如果你不默认对一切说“不”，如果你甚至需要特意去说“不”，那会占用时间。例如，有很多人热衷于 hustle 文化，而 hustle 文化的一大特点是：如果你不要求，你就得不到。所以他们会 hustle 别人。他们总是给你发请求、发消息。是的，这是名人的问题，但我也有。人们总是向我索要东西。我收到这些消息时会有点不安，我相信你也会收到这样的短信、邮件，说：“嘿，克里斯，我的朋友某某应该上你的播客”，或者“你应该来参加我的活动”，“你应该为我的书写序”。你收到这些时会有点不安，对吧？你必须想办法说“不”。我学到的一件事是，如果你不会要求别人去做某件事，而你自己收到了那个请求，你可以直接忽略它。你不需要回复。

turns.en: You don't even let it enter your brain. You have to be able to delete emails and text messages without flinching if you want to scale. And scaling is very important. Scaling your time is really important. Every interruption will take you out of flow. So the only way you can remain in flow is if you get either very good at ignoring these things by default or closing yourself off like a hermit like our mutual friend Tim Ferriss does, or you just become emotionally capable of not registering these as something that causes turbulence inside of you. That not registering it emotionally thing is fundamental. That's so fundamental to so many things in life.
turns.zh: 你甚至不让它进入你的大脑。如果你想规模化，你必须能够毫不犹豫地删除电子邮件和短信。规模化非常重要。规模化你的时间非常重要。每一次打断都会让你脱离心流。所以保持心流的唯一方法是，要么你非常擅长默认忽略这些东西，要么像我们共同的朋友蒂姆·费里斯那样像隐士一样封闭自己，要么你就在情感上能够不把这些视为内心引起波澜的事情。这种不在情感上记录它的能力是根本性的。这对生活中的很多事情都至关重要。

turns.en: Can we dig into that a little bit? Because again, I've only seen you as you right now. I didn't know you 20 years ago, I didn't know you as a child. So I've only seen you with this holistic selfishness, the integrated self-prioritization, whatever we call it. Selfish is fine. I'll take selfish. I'm selfish. I'm a very selfish person. Don't contact me. Yeah, that emotional reaction. I also get the sense too that maybe people have lived an obligation life for so long that they actually kind of struggle to tap into what it is that they want. They've hidden their wants and their desires and their needs and they've deprioritized themselves so much for so long they go, 'What do I want actually? What is it? Do I want to go to this thing or not?' Because all I've done is be puppeted, right? I've been marionetted by other people's desires for so long. I can't even tap into that anymore. And saying no feels like a war crime.
turns.zh: 我们能深入探讨一下吗？因为再次，我只看到现在的你。我不认识20年前的你，也不认识小时候的你。所以我只看到你这种整体的自私，整合的自我优先化，不管我们怎么称呼它。自私没问题。我接受自私。我很自私。我是一个非常自私的人。别联系我。是的，那种情绪反应。我也有一种感觉，也许人们过义务生活太久了，以至于他们实际上很难挖掘出自己真正想要什么。他们隐藏了自己的愿望、欲望和需求，他们把自己放在次要位置太久了，以至于他们会问：'我到底想要什么？是什么？我想去这件事还是不想？'因为我所做的一切都是被操纵的，对吧？我被别人的欲望像木偶一样操纵了太久。我甚至无法再触及那个了。说'不'感觉像是一种战争罪行。

turns.en: So I think it's really good to be able to view your own mind and your own thoughts objectively. And that is the big benefit of meditation. It creates a small gap between your conscious observation self and your mind, and that lets you then look at your thoughts and evaluate them a little bit like you would a third party's statements. And if you just take your mind to be you and they're integrated in one and the same at all times and you're reacting from the mind, then you're not even going to question things that come into your mind. Anything that comes in that creates a reaction will immediately create a reaction. But if you can observe your thoughts a little bit, and not in some woo way, but you can even just do it through therapy, you can do it through journaling, you can do it any way you would like, you can just take long walks, you don't have to meditate and do lotus position. All that is unnecessary. But if you can observe your own thoughts and view them a little objectively, then you can start being a little more choosy, a little more critical, and you can realize that there are no problems in the real world other than maybe things that inflict pain on your body. Everything else has to become a problem in your mind first. You have to view it and interpret it and create a narrative that it is a problem before it becomes a problem. And then you realize that a lot of your emotional energy is spent on reacting to things that your mind is automatically saying are problems. And you don't need all those problems. Do you really need that many problems in your life? Again, I would say try to focus on just one overarching problem and then go solve that problem. It's like if you want to be successful, define success very concretely. Focus on that. In everything else, when it enters your mind, it becomes a problem. Whether it's a judgment about the girl walking down the street or the car that just cut in front of you or whether it's like your accountant did this stupid thing, yes, it's going to trigger you, but observe for a moment that it's triggering me. I've created a problem. Do I really want to have this problem right now? Do I want to spend the energy on this problem or do I want that going somewhere else? And it doesn't have to be that overt. You don't have to. The mind mud wrestling with itself is also a problem, but because it loves to do that. I have my problems have got problems and I have a real problem about fixing my problems. Yeah. Exactly. So you're going to be much happier and much more focused. Again, I think happiness and focus and success can kind of complement each other. You're going to have much more energy. Just think about it as mental energy. You have much more mental energy to focus on the actual problems you want to solve if you don't start unconsciously, subconsciously, reactively picking up problems everywhere. So before anything can be a problem that takes up your emotional energy, you have to accept it as a problem. You can be choosy about your problems. And I'm not saying I'm perfect in that regard, but I think I'm better than I used to be.
turns.zh: 所以我认为能够客观地看待自己的思想和想法是非常好的。这是冥想的一大好处。它在你意识观察的自我和你的思想之间创造了一个小间隙，让你可以审视自己的想法，就像评估第三方的陈述一样。如果你认为你的思想就是你，它们始终融为一体，你从思想中做出反应，那么你甚至不会质疑进入你脑海的东西。任何引起反应的东西都会立即引起反应。但如果你能稍微观察一下你的想法，不是用某种玄学的方式，而是通过治疗、写日记、任何你喜欢的方式，甚至只是长时间散步，你不需要冥想或盘腿打坐。所有这些都不是必需的。但如果你能观察自己的想法并稍微客观地看待它们，那么你就可以开始更有选择性、更批判性，你会意识到现实世界中除了可能给你的身体带来痛苦的事情之外，没有其他问题。其他一切首先必须成为你思想中的问题。你必须看待它、解释它并创造一个叙事，说它是一个问题，然后它才成为一个问题。然后你会意识到你很多情感能量都花在了对思想自动认为是问题的事情做出反应上。你不需要所有这些麻烦。你真的需要生活中那么多问题吗？再次，我会说试着只专注于一个首要问题，然后去解决那个问题。就像如果你想成功，就要非常具体地定义成功。专注于那个。其他一切，当它进入你的脑海时，它就变成了一个问题。无论是关于街上走着的女孩的判断，还是刚刚插到你前面的车，或者你的会计做了件蠢事，是的，它会触发你，但观察一下它正在触发我。我创造了一个问题。我现在真的想要这个问题吗？我想把精力花在这个问题上，还是想把它用到别处？而且它不必那么明显。你不必。思想与自己泥浆摔跤也是一个问题，因为它喜欢这样做。我的问题有问题，我还有一个真正的问题关于解决我的问题。是的。没错。所以你会更快乐、更专注。再次，我认为幸福、专注和成功可以相互补充。你会有更多的精力。把它当作精神能量。如果你不开始无意识地、下意识地、反应性地到处捡起问题，你就会有更多的精神能量专注于你实际想要解决的问题。所以在任何东西成为消耗你情感能量的问题之前，你必须先接受它是一个问题。你可以对你的问题有所选择。我并不是说我在那方面是完美的，但我认为我比以前更好了。

turns.en: Well, lots of people are addicted to solving problems. So much so that sometimes people create problems when we don't have any simply so that we can solve them. We have that going on. And then even worse is we take on problems that we can't affect. So, another one of my little quips was, a rational person can cultivate indifference to things that are out of their control. Or a rational person can find peace by cultivating indifference to things that are out of their control. And I'm as guilty as anybody of doom surfing on X or social media and getting worked up about things that I can't do anything about. Like, do I want to be fighting those battles in my mind when I literally cannot do anything about it? So, if you find yourself looping on a problem, like you're watching the news too much and you're getting caught up in a problem you can't do anything about, you have to step away from that.
