And modern media is a delivery mechanism for mimetic viruses. And now, what's happened now is, 100 years ago, 500 years ago, if something wasn't happening in your immediate vicinity, you wouldn't hear about it. It wouldn't be a problem for you. But now every single one of the world's problems has turned into a mimetic virus which is going into the battlefield of the news and is trying to infect your mind in real time so that you become obsessed with the war in Ukraine which is really far away, or you get obsessed with climate change, or you get obsessed with AI doom, or you get obsessed with whatever. And there's nothing as riveting as the old religion: the world is ending, the world is ending, pay attention, the world is ending. And if you don't... Cassandra complex at global scale.
turns.zh: 嗯，很多人都沉迷于解决问题。以至于有时人们在没有问题的时候也会制造问题，只是为了能解决它们。这种情况正在发生。更糟糕的是，我们还会承担我们无法影响的问题。所以，我的另一个小俏皮话是，一个理性的人可以对无法控制的事情培养冷漠。或者一个理性的人可以通过对无法控制的事情培养冷漠来找到平静。我和任何人一样，在X或社交媒体上刷负面信息，为那些我无能为力的事情感到激动。比如，我真的想在我完全无法改变的情况下在脑海里打那些仗吗？所以，如果你发现自己在一个问题上循环，比如你看太多新闻，陷入一个你无法改变的问题，你必须远离它。现代媒体是模因病毒的传递机制。现在发生的事情是，100年前、500年前，如果某件事没有发生在你附近，你就不会听说它。它不会成为你的问题。但现在世界上的每一个问题都变成了一个模因病毒，进入新闻战场，实时试图感染你的思想，让你沉迷于遥远的乌克兰战争，或者沉迷于气候变化，或者沉迷于AI末日，或者沉迷于其他任何事情。没有什么比古老的宗教更引人入胜的了：世界末日来了，世界末日来了，注意，世界末日来了。如果你不……全球规模的卡桑德拉情结。

turns.en: Well, and I would argue that large percentages of the population are essentially just infected with these mimetic viruses that have taken over their brain and are causing them to do incredible agitation about things that probably aren't even true or are greatly exaggerated.
turns.zh: 嗯，我会认为很大一部分人口基本上只是被这些模因病毒感染了，这些病毒已经接管了他们的大脑，导致他们对可能甚至不真实或被严重夸大的事情产生难以置信的激动。

turns.en: But even to the extent they are true, they're things that that person can do nothing about. And they should put their own house in order first. So, you know, another little line I have for myself is your family is broken, but you're going to fix the world. Right? People are running out there to try and fix the world when their own lives are a mess. Oh my god. Right? And I think it defies credibility if you can't fix your own life first. I'm not going to take you seriously if you can't fix your own life. Like all these philosophers who, you know, seem like people you emulate and so smart or like these brilliant celebrities and they go off and commit suicide. Well, you just kind of invalidated your whole way of life. It's like that line in No Country for Old Men where the killer is waiting for the protagonist and protagonist shows up and the killer says, 'Well, you know, if your set of rules brought you here, then what good are your rules?' It didn't work.
turns.zh: 但即使这些事是真的，也是那个人无能为力的。他们应该先把自己的家收拾好。所以，我对自己还有一句座右铭：你的家庭一团糟，却想去拯救世界？对吧？人们自己的生活一团糟，却跑出去试图修复世界。天哪。对吧？我认为如果你连自己的生活都搞不定，那就缺乏可信度。如果你连自己的生活都搞不定，我不会把你当回事。就像那些哲学家，看起来值得效仿、那么聪明，或者那些才华横溢的名人，结果自杀了。好吧，你刚刚否定了你整个生活方式。就像《老无所依》里那句台词：杀手在等主角，主角来了，杀手说：‘如果你的那套规则把你带到了这里，那你的规则还有什么用？’它没起作用。

turns.en: Um, I am holistically selfish in that I want to be objectively successful in everything I set out to do. Yeah. You have one life. Don't settle for mediocrity. And I think the only true test of intelligence is if you get what you want out of life. And there are two parts to that. One is getting what you want, so you know how to get it. And the second is wanting the right things. Knowing what to want in the first place. I could want to be a 6'8" basketball player and I'm not going to get that. So that's wanting the wrong thing. That's wanting something you can't get. It's also wanting something that you don't want. Yeah. Wanting something that's a booby prize. There are plenty of booby prizes out there, too. Right. I haven't heard that word in about 20 years. Yeah. Prizes that are just not worth having or that create their own problems.
turns.zh: 嗯，我在整体上是自私的，因为我希望在我着手做的每件事上都客观成功。是的。你只有一次生命。不要满足于平庸。我认为唯一真正的智力测试是，你是否从生活中得到了你想要的。这有两部分。一是得到你想要的，所以你知道如何得到它。二是想要正确的东西。首先知道想要什么。我可能想成为一个6英尺8英寸的篮球运动员，但我得不到。所以那是想要错误的东西。那是想要你得不到的东西。也是想要你并不真正想要的东西。是的。想要一个陷阱奖品。外面有很多陷阱奖品。对。我大概20年没听过这个词了。是的。那些不值得拥有或会带来自身问题的奖品。

turns.en: Well, if you're not careful, you can end up in a place in life not only that you don't want to be, but one that you didn't even mean to get to. That's if you're kind of proceeding unconsciously. And usually I think people end up there because they are going on autopilot with sort of societal expectations or other people's expectations. So, you know, or out of guilt or out of mimetic desire. You know, Peter Thiel has this whole thing from René Girard about how mimetic desires are desires picked up from other people. And some of those are automatically baked into society like, you know, go to law school, go to med school, go to whatever, go to business school. Or they might be from watching what your friends are doing and, you know, the other monkeys are doing. Or it might just be, you know, what your parents' expectations are. It might be guilt. You know, guilt is just society's voice speaking in your head, socially programmed, so you'll be a good little monkey and do things that are good for the tribe.
turns.zh: 嗯，如果你不小心，你最终可能会处于一个不仅你不想要，而且你甚至无意到达的生活境地。那是如果你在无意识地前进。而且我认为人们通常会这样，因为他们是在自动驾驶，遵循社会期望或他人的期望。所以，你知道，或者出于内疚，或者出于模仿欲望。你知道，彼得·蒂尔从勒内·吉拉尔那里借鉴了整套理论，关于模仿欲望是如何从他人那里习得的欲望。其中一些自动融入了社会，比如，你知道，去法学院，去医学院，去任何地方，去商学院。或者可能来自观察你的朋友在做什么，其他猴子在做什么。或者可能只是你父母的期望。可能是内疚。你知道，内疚只是社会在你脑海中说话的声音，是社会编程，让你成为一只好猴子，做对部落有益的事情。

turns.en: But I think the best outcomes come when you think it through for yourself and decide for yourself. And I don't think people spend enough time deciding. For example, we run on these four-year cycles. You know, in Silicon Valley, you go join a startup, you vest your stock over four years. That's the standard. Okay? In college, you know, you go for four years. High school, you go for four years. Some things take longer. You know, you have children, they hit puberty 9 years later. That's like a 9-year cycle until that relationship changes. But we're used to these fairly long cycles, multi-year cycles in which we are committed to things. You go to law school, you know, four or five year cycle. You go be a lawyer, 40-year cycle. These are very long cycles. The amount of time we spend deciding what to do and who to do it with, very short, very very short, right? We spend, you know, 3 months deciding, one month deciding on a job where we're going to be for 10 years or 5 years. And because a lot of discovery is path dependent where the next thing you find on the path depends on where you were on the previous path. You sort of start going down this vector that is a very long distance. People decide frivolously which city to live in and that's going to decide who their friends are, what their jobs are, their opportunity, their weather, their food supply, their air supply, quality of life. You know, it's such an important decision, but people spend so little time thinking it through. I would argue that if you're making a four-year decision, spend a year thinking it through. Like really thinking it through. 25% of the time.
turns.zh: 但我认为最好的结果来自于你自己深思熟虑并为自己做决定。我认为人们没有花足够的时间做决定。例如，我们遵循这些四年周期。你知道，在硅谷，你加入一家初创公司，你的股票在四年内归属。这是标准。对吧？在大学，你知道，你上四年。高中，你上四年。有些事情需要更长时间。你知道，你有了孩子，他们9年后进入青春期。那是一个9年的周期，直到那种关系改变。但我们习惯了这些相当长的周期，多年周期，我们在其中对事情做出承诺。你去法学院，你知道，四五年周期。你成为律师，40年周期。这些是非常长的周期。我们花在决定做什么和与谁一起做上的时间非常短，非常非常短，对吧？我们花，你知道，三个月决定，一个月决定一份我们将要做10年或5年的工作。而且因为很多发现是路径依赖的，你在路径上找到的下一个东西取决于你在前一条路径上的位置。你有点开始沿着这个向量前进，这是一个非常长的距离。人们轻率地决定住在哪个城市，而这将决定他们的朋友是谁，他们的工作是什么，他们的机会，他们的天气，他们的食物供应，他们的空气供应，生活质量。你知道，这是一个如此重要的决定，但人们花很少的时间去深思熟虑。我会说，如果你要做一个四年的决定，花一年时间去深思熟虑。真正地深思熟虑。25%的时间。

turns.en: Yeah. Exactly. There's the secretary theorem. I don't know if you know that one. Computer science. After you've done this many people, pick the best one of the next however many. That's right. Yeah. The secretary theorem is this computer science professor is trying to figure out how much time he should spend interviewing secretaries and then how long to keep the secretary. So let's say he's going to have a secretary for 10 years. Does he keep searching for you know one year, 2 years, 3 years, 1 month, 2 months? What is the optimal time? And it turns out that the optimal time is somewhere around a third. About a third of the way through, you take the best person you've worked with and try to find someone that good or better. So that by the time you've gotten about a third of the way through, you have, excuse me, seen enough that you now have a sense of what the bar is. And then anybody who meets or exceeds that bar is good enough. And this applies to dating, this applies to jobs and careers, this applies generally. But the interesting thing about the secretary theorem is that it's actually not time based. It's not based on one third of the time. It's iteration based. The number of candidates, the number of shots you took on goal.
turns.zh: 是的。没错。有个秘书定理。我不知道你是否知道这个。计算机科学。在你面试了这么多人之后，从接下来的多少人中选最好的那个。没错。是的。秘书定理是这样的：一个计算机科学教授试图弄清楚他应该花多少时间面试秘书，然后留用秘书多久。假设他要用一个秘书10年。他应该继续寻找一年、两年、三年、一个月、两个月？什么是最优时间？结果发现最优时间大约是三分之一。大约在三分之一的时候，你选出你合作过的最好的人，然后试图找到同样好或更好的人。所以当你走到大约三分之一的时候，你已经看得足够多，现在知道了标准是什么。然后任何达到或超过这个标准的人都足够好。这适用于约会，适用于工作和职业，普遍适用。但秘书定理有趣的一点是，它实际上不是基于时间的。它不是基于三分之一的时间。它是基于迭代次数的。候选人的数量，你射门的次数。

turns.en: That's right. So, you want to have lots and lots of iterations. So, that means that you need to bail out quickly and you need to be decisive quickly. That's right. You need to take opportunities quickly and bail out quickly. Correct. Like if you go back and you look through failed relationships, probably the biggest regret will be staying in the relationship after you knew it was over. Exactly. You should have left sooner. The moment you knew it wasn't going to work out, you should have moved on. So in that sense, I think Malcolm Gladwell popularized this idea of 10,000 hours to mastery. I would say it's actually 10,000 iterations to mastery. It's not actually 10,000. It's some unknown number. But it's about the number of iterations that drives a learning curve. And iteration is not repetition. Repetition is a different thing. Repeating is doing the same thing over and over.
turns.zh: 没错。所以，你想要很多很多次迭代。这意味着你需要快速退出，并且需要快速果断。没错。你需要快速抓住机会，快速退出。正确。就像如果你回顾失败的关系，最大的遗憾可能是你在知道关系结束后还继续留在里面。正是如此。你应该早点离开。当你意识到它行不通的那一刻，你就应该继续前进。所以在这个意义上，我认为马尔科姆·格拉德威尔普及了1万小时精通的概念。我会说实际上是1万次迭代精通。实际上不是1万。是某个未知的数字。但驱动学习曲线的是迭代次数。而迭代不是重复。重复是另一回事。重复是反复做同一件事。

turns.en: Iteration is modifying it with a learning and then doing another version of it. So that's error correction. So if you get 10,000 error corrections in anything, you will be an expert at it.
turns.zh: 迭代就是带着学到的东西去修改，然后做出另一个版本。这就是纠错。所以如果你在任何事情上经历一万次纠错，你就会成为专家。

turns.en: You mentioned there about the people who've got a nightmare going on at home and are trying to fix the world. But a lot of the time that cynicism and pessimism we find in ourselves. We see the world whether we want to, whether it's because we've embodied what the news or the negative people around us have said or it's a bit more endogenous than that. It's just sort of in us. It's the way that we see the world. How can people avoid cynicism and pessimism within themselves?
turns.zh: 你之前提到那些家里一团糟却想拯救世界的人。但很多时候，我们发现自己内心也有这种愤世嫉俗和悲观。我们看待世界的方式，不管愿不愿意，要么是因为我们吸收了新闻或身边负面人士的话，要么是更内生的原因——它就在我们骨子里。这就是我们看世界的方式。人们如何避免自己内心的愤世嫉俗和悲观呢？

turns.en: Cynicism and pessimism is a tough one. It's we're naturally hardwired for it. Again, I go back to evolution. I'm sorry to keep harping on evolution, but within biology, there's very few good explanatory theories. And the theory of evolution by natural selection is probably the best one. So, if you can't explain something about life or psychology or human nature through evolution, then you probably don't have a good theory for it. And I would say that pessimism is another one that comes out of this, which is in the natural environment, you're hardwired to be pessimistic. Because let's say that I see something rustling in the woods and if I move towards it and it turns out to be food and prey then good I get to eat one meal but if it turns out to be a predator I get eaten and that's the end of that. So we are hardwired to avoid ruin and just dying. So we are naturally hardwired to be pessimists but modern society is very different despite whatever problems you may have with modern society. It is far far safer than living in the jungle and just trying to survive. And the opportunities on the upside are nonlinear. For example, when you're investing, if you short a stock, the most money you can make is 2x. You just lose, you know, if the stock goes to zero, you double your money. But if the stock is the next Nvidia and it goes 100x or 1000x, you make a lot of money. So upside through leverage is nearly unlimited. Also in modern society, because there's so many different people you can interact with, if you go on a date and it fails, there are infinite more people to go on a date with. In a tribal system, there might have been 20 people and you can't even get through all of them. So, modern society is far more forgiving of failure. And you just have to sort of neocortically realize and override that. You have to realize that you're much more running a search function to find the thing that'll work. And then that one thing will pay off in massive compounding. Once you find your mate for the rest of your life, you find your wife or your husband, then you can compound in that relationship. It's okay if you had 50 failed dates in between. The same way once you find the one business you're meant to plow into and it'll compound returns. It's okay if you had 50 small failed ventures or 50 small failed job interviews. The number of failures doesn't matter. And so there's no point in being a pessimist. You want to be an optimist. But I would say you want to be skeptical about specific things. Every specific opportunity is probably a fail. But you want to be optimistic in the general. In the general you want to be like something in here is going to work out.
turns.zh: 愤世嫉俗和悲观是个棘手的问题。我们天生就有这种倾向。我又要回到进化论了，抱歉一直提进化论，但在生物学中，好的解释理论很少，而自然选择进化论可能是最好的一个。所以，如果你不能用进化论解释生活、心理学或人性的某些方面，那你可能就没有一个好的理论。我认为悲观也是由此而来的——在自然环境中，你天生就是悲观的。比方说，我看到树林里有东西在动，如果我走过去，结果是食物或猎物，那好，我能吃一顿；但如果那是捕食者，我就被吃掉了，一切就结束了。所以我们天生就倾向于避免毁灭和死亡。因此我们天生就是悲观者，但现代社会非常不同——不管你对现代社会有什么看法，它都比在丛林中求生要安全得多。而且向上的机会是非线性的。例如，投资时，如果你做空一只股票，最多能赚一倍（如果股票跌到零，你翻倍）。但如果这只股票是下一个英伟达，涨了100倍或1000倍，你就能赚大钱。所以通过杠杆，上行空间几乎是无限的。同样，在现代社会，你可以和很多人互动；如果你约会失败，还有无数人可以约会。在部落系统中，可能只有20个人，你甚至无法全部尝试。所以现代社会对失败宽容得多。你只需要用新皮层意识到并覆盖这种本能。你必须认识到，你更像是在运行一个搜索函数，寻找那个能成功的东西。然后那一个东西会带来巨大的复利。一旦你找到终身伴侣——你的妻子或丈夫——你就可以在这段关系中复利。中间有50次失败的约会也没关系。同样，一旦你找到那个你该全力投入的事业，它会带来复利回报。中间有50次小失败或50次失败的工作面试也没关系。失败次数不重要。所以悲观没有意义。你想成为一个乐观者。但我想说，你应该对具体的事情持怀疑态度。每一个具体的机会很可能都会失败。但你要在总体上保持乐观。总体上你要觉得：这里总会有东西能成。

turns.en: How do you navigate that tension?
turns.zh: 你如何驾驭这种张力？

turns.en: I mean, exactly as I said, I'm optimistic in the general that if something fails right now, then this is a little woo-woo, but it wasn't meant to be. It was a learning experience. It was an iteration. As long as I learned something from it, then it's a win. If I didn't learn from it, then it's a loss. But as long as you're learning and you keep iterating fast and cutting your losses quickly, then when you find the right thing, you have to be optimistic and compound into it. So, you don't want to jump into the first thing. And you don't want to marry the first person you date necessarily, unless you got very lucky. But you want to investigate and explore very very quickly until you find the match. And then you have to be willing to go all in. You have to be willing to move your chips to the center of the table. So both those approaches are required. So it's a barbell strategy. It's sort of black or it's white. And most people are sort of stuck in this gray bit. And I'm like half in, but I'm kind of don't really know if I am.
turns.zh: 我的意思就是，正如我说的，我在总体上乐观：如果某件事现在失败了，那——这有点玄学——就是它本不该成。这是一次学习经历，一次迭代。只要我从中学到了东西，那就是赢。如果没学到，那就是输。但只要你学习，快速迭代，迅速止损，那么当你找到正确的东西时，你必须乐观并投入其中。所以你不应该一头扎进第一个机会。你也不一定非要和第一个约会的人结婚，除非你非常幸运。但你要快速调查和探索，直到找到匹配的。然后你必须愿意全力以赴，愿意把筹码推到桌子中央。所以这两种方法都需要。这是一种杠铃策略。它要么黑要么白。而大多数人卡在灰色地带，半进半出，自己也不太确定。

turns.en: I also think labels like pessimist, optimist, cynic, introvert, extrovert, these are very self-limiting. Humans are very dynamic. There are times when you feel like being introverted. There are times when you feel like being extroverted. There are contexts in which you'll be pessimistic. There are contexts in which you'll be optimistic. Leave all those labels alone. It's better just to look at the problem at hand. Look at reality the way it is. Try to take yourself out of the equation in a sense. Like obviously you're involved, but motivated reasoning is the worst kind of reasoning. You're not going to find truth through highly motivated reasoning. You have to be objective. And objective means trying to take yourself out of it as much as possible or at least your personality out of it as much as possible. And so to the extent you run with this thick identity and personality, it's going to cloud your judgment. It's going to try and lock you into the past. If you say, 'I'm a depressed, unhappy person. Yeah, I'm going to be unhappy.' That's a way of locking yourself into your past. Even saying, 'I have trauma. I have PTSD.' Yeah, you feel something. There are memories. There are flashes. There are occasional bad feelings. But don't define yourself by it because then you'll lock it into your identity and you're just going to loop on it. It's better to stay flexible because reality is always changing and you have to be able to adapt to it. Adaptation is also intelligence. Adaptation is survival. Adaptation is kind of how you're here. You're here because you're an adapter and your ancestors were adapters. So to adapt, you will be able to see things clearly. And if you're seeing them through your own identity, it's going to cloud your judgment.
turns.zh: 我还认为，像悲观者、乐观者、愤世嫉俗者、内向者、外向者这样的标签非常自我限制。人类是非常动态的。有时候你想内向，有时候你想外向。有些情境下你会悲观，有些情境下你会乐观。别管那些标签。最好只看眼前的问题。看清现实本来的样子。试着在某种意义上把自己从方程中移除。显然你参与其中，但动机性推理是最糟糕的推理。你无法通过高度动机性的推理找到真相。你必须客观。客观意味着尽可能把自己抽离出来，至少尽可能把个性抽离出来。所以，如果你带着厚重的身份和个性行事，它会蒙蔽你的判断，把你锁在过去。如果你说‘我是一个抑郁、不快乐的人。是的，我会不快乐。’这就是把自己锁在过去。甚至说‘我有创伤。我有PTSD。’是的，你感受到一些东西。有记忆，有闪回，偶尔有不好的感觉。但不要以此定义自己，因为那样你会把它锁进身份里，然后不断循环。最好保持灵活，因为现实总是在变化，你必须能够适应。适应也是智慧。适应就是生存。适应是你之所以在这里的原因。你在这里是因为你是一个适应者，你的祖先也是适应者。所以为了适应，你才能看清事物。而如果你通过自己的身份来看，它会蒙蔽你的判断。

turns.en: A quick aside, you've probably heard me talk about Element before. In fact, you might be thinking, 'For sake, Chris, will you shut up about Element?' And the answer is no, I won't because when I take it, I genuinely feel the difference. I genuinely believe that proper hydration makes a massive difference to your performance. So, I'm going to keep talking about it until you start drinking it. Element is a tasty electrolyte drink mix with everything that you need and nothing that you don't.
turns.zh: 顺便提一句，你可能之前听我提过 Element。事实上，你可能会想：‘天哪，Chris，你能闭嘴不提 Element 吗？’答案是：不，我不会，因为我喝了它之后真的感觉到了不同。我真心相信适当补水对你的表现有巨大影响。所以我会一直说，直到你开始喝它。Element 是一种美味的电解质饮料冲剂，包含你需要的一切，没有你不需要的。

turns.en: Each grab-and-go stick pack contains a science-backed electrolyte ratio of sodium, potassium, and magnesium with no sugar, no coloring, no artificial ingredients, or any other junk. It plays a critical role in reducing muscle cramps and fatigue while optimizing brain health, regulating appetite, and curbing cravings. This orange flavor in a cold glass of water is how I've started my morning every single day for over 3 years now, and I genuinely feel the difference versus when I don't. Best of all, they have a no questions asked refund policy with an unlimited duration. So, you can buy it and try it for as long as you want. And if you don't like it for any reason, they'll just give you your money back. You don't even need to return the box. That's how confident they are that you love it. Plus, they offer free shipping within the US. And right now, you can get a free sample pack of all eight flavors with your first box by going to the link in the description below or heading to drinklnt.com/modernwisdom. That's drinklnt.com/modern wisdom.
turns.zh: 每个即溶棒包都含有科学配比的电解质——钠、钾、镁，无糖、无色素、无人工成分或任何其他垃圾。它在减少肌肉痉挛和疲劳、优化大脑健康、调节食欲和抑制渴望方面起着关键作用。这种橙子口味加在一杯冷水里，是我过去三年多每天早上开始的方式，我真的能感觉到喝与不喝的区别。最重要的是，他们有无条件退款政策，不限时长。所以你可以购买并尝试任意长时间。如果因任何原因不喜欢，他们就会退款，你甚至不需要退回盒子。他们对你喜欢它就是这么有信心。此外，美国境内免运费。现在，通过下方描述中的链接或访问 drinklnt.com/modernwisdom，你的第一盒将附赠所有八种口味的免费样品包。那就是 drinklnt.com/modernwisdom。

turns.en: Moving on to sort of thinking about happiness. Obviously a topic of yours that's a it's honestly the one that I feel least qualified to talk about. Is it like a guy that's got long arms teaching you how to bench press or a dude that's really tall teaching you how to deadlift? someone that feels like they came from behind the eightball. Yeah. Is you're you're asking a crazy person about their thoughts. So, just thought it through. Is happiness still more about peace than it is about joy?
turns.zh: 接下来谈谈幸福。这显然是你的一个话题，老实说，我觉得自己最没资格谈这个。这就像一个长臂的家伙教你卧推，或者一个高个子教你硬拉？一个觉得自己处于劣势的人。是啊。你是在问一个疯子他的想法。所以，好好想想。幸福仍然是关于平静多于快乐吗？

turns.en: It's just one of those overloaded words that means different things to different people. So, I'm not even sure we're communicating the same language. But, uh what is happiness? I think it's just basically being okay with where you are. Not wanting not wanting things to be different than the way they are. Not having the sense that anything is missing in this moment. Needing something to change. Your current positive situation being contingent on an adjustment on getting something from the outside world. Ironically, I think most people if you were to ask them when they were happiest for a sustained period of time, not for a brief moment, because pleasure can override happiness and create kind of this illusion of happiness. But if you ask people when they were happy for a sustained period of time, they were probably doing some variation of nothing.
turns.zh: 这只是那些被过度使用的词之一，对不同的人意味着不同的东西。所以，我甚至不确定我们是否在用同一种语言交流。但是，呃，什么是幸福？我认为它基本上就是对自己所处的位置感到满意。不希望事情变得不同。不觉得此刻缺少什么。不需要任何改变。你当前的积极状态不依赖于从外部世界获得某种调整。讽刺的是，我认为如果你问大多数人他们持续快乐的时候——不是短暂的瞬间，因为快乐可以覆盖幸福并制造一种幸福的幻觉——但如果你问人们他们持续快乐的时候，他们很可能是在做某种形式的“什么都不做”。

turns.en: That's interesting because in the chase is this sort of lack, this contingency. That's right. But then you get bored. If you just sit around all the time, you get bored. So you want adventure, you want surprise. Like there's a funny thought experiment of the bliss machine, right? Which is suppose I could drill a hole in your head and put electrode in. And they did this with monkeys and I can put a wire in there and I can stimulate just the right part of your brain and I can put you in bliss and you'll just be in bliss. Would you would you want that? Would that be nice? For how long? Do it and I'll tell you. Right. So most people will say, "Well, I don't want that. I want meaning. I don't want just bliss. I want meaning." And you're like, "Okay, well, I'll put an electrode in there and I'll give you meaning. How about that?" And if you kind of run this thought experiment long enough, I think most people realize actually what I want is I want surprise. I want and I want the world to surprise me and I want to wrestle with it in ways that are somewhat predictable but somewhat not. And you kind of end up back where you started. So I I don't know if necessarily for some people Pure happiness is the ultimate goal. They want to, you know, just be blissfully happy wherever they are, whenever they are. But I think other people, most people would say, well, I'm here in this world. I'm here in this life. I don't understand it or why, but I want to be I want to be engaged. I want to be surprised. I want to do things. I want to accomplish things. I want to want things and then get them. Right? That's kind of the whole game that we're all playing here.
turns.zh: 这很有趣，因为在追逐中就有这种缺失、这种偶然性。没错。但然后你会感到无聊。如果你一直坐着不动，你会感到无聊。所以你渴望冒险，渴望惊喜。就像有一个有趣的“极乐机器”思想实验，对吧？假设我可以在你头上钻个洞，放个电极进去。他们用猴子做过这个，我可以放根电线进去，刺激你大脑的某个正确部位，让你进入极乐状态，你就一直极乐。你想要那样吗？那好吗？多久？做吧，然后我告诉你。对。所以大多数人会说：“嗯，我不想要那个。我想要意义。我不只是想要极乐。我想要意义。”然后你会说：“好吧，那我放个电极进去，给你意义。怎么样？”如果你把这个思想实验进行得足够久，我认为大多数人会意识到，实际上我想要的是惊喜。我想要世界给我惊喜，我想以某种可预测但又不可预测的方式与之搏斗。然后你有点回到了起点。所以我不知道是否对某些人来说，纯粹的幸福是终极目标。他们想要，你知道的，无论在哪里、无论何时都幸福快乐。但我认为其他人，大多数人会说，我在这世上，我在这生命中。我不理解它或为什么，但我想参与其中。我想被惊喜。我想做事。我想完成事情。我想渴望东西然后得到它们。对吧？这就是我们都在玩的游戏。

turns.en: Surprises are really interesting. the sort of unpredictability. I think total bro science here, but I'm pretty sure that that's kind of how dopamine works. That things are a bit better than you expected. That within that it means that if you for the perennial insecure overachievers that uh cloy for control that really want to be able to the schedule is perfectly done and we know the itinerary, we know where we're going to be at this time. you're in some ways I guess reducing down the capacity for surprise because everything has become uh very contrived prescribed done in advance laid out your ability to be surprised actually diminishes.
turns.zh: 惊喜真的很有趣。那种不可预测性。我觉得这完全是“兄弟科学”，但我很确定这就是多巴胺的工作原理。事情比你预期的好一点。这意味着，如果你是一个长期不安全的过度成就者，渴望控制，真的希望日程完美完成，我们知道行程，我们知道这个时间点会在哪里——你在某种程度上降低了惊喜的能力，因为一切都变得非常刻意、预先规定、提前安排好，你被惊喜的能力实际上减弱了。

turns.en: Yeah. If if nothing worked out the way you expected, if it was all serendipity and you didn't want that, you would just be a ball of anxiety. On the other hand, if everything worked out as you expected and wanted, you'd be so bored you might as well be dead. So there's some, you know, the river of life kind of flows between these two banks and enjoy it.
turns.zh: 是啊。如果一切都不按你预期的方式发展，如果全是偶然，而你不想要那样，你就会变成一个焦虑的球。另一方面，如果一切都如你所愿地发展，你会无聊到不如死了算了。所以，你知道，生命之河就在这两岸之间流淌，享受它吧。

turns.en: You say thinking about yourself is the source of all unhappiness, but presumably you need to work on yourself and your weaknesses as well. So some degree of reflection is important. And if thinking about yourself is a source of unhappiness, is this a price that you need to pay? I need to sort of reflect inward. I'm going to have to diminish this level of happiness for a little while and then I can use this new level. I've got my brown belt on and I can go out into the world as a brown belt.
turns.zh: 你说思考自己是所有不快乐的根源，但大概你也需要改进自己和自己的弱点。所以一定程度的反思很重要。如果思考自己是不快乐的根源，这是你需要付出的代价吗？我需要向内反思。我不得不暂时降低这个幸福水平，然后我可以用这个新水平。我系上了棕带，我可以作为棕带走向世界。

turns.en: What I'm specifically referring to that is if if you're thinking about your personality and your ego and the character of you and uh you're obsessing over that. That's where a lot of depression and unhappiness sort of lingers and and gets cultivated. Uh so uh thinking about woe is me, this happened to me. that happened to me. I have this personality. I have this issue. I deserve this. I didn't get that. That's you're just strengthening a little beast in there that is insatiable. And that's where I think a lot of unhappiness comes from.
turns.zh: 我特别指的是，如果你在思考你的个性、你的自我和你的性格，并且你对此着迷。那是很多抑郁和不快乐徘徊和滋生的地方。所以，思考“我真可怜，这发生在我身上，那发生在我身上。我有这种性格。我有这个问题。我应得这个。我没得到那个。”——你只是在强化里面那个永不满足的小野兽。我认为很多不快乐就来源于此。

turns.en: What's the beast?
turns.zh: 那个野兽是什么？

turns.en: It's the ego. But that word is so overused that I kind of hate to use the word. Um, but it's like a it's a recurrent collection of thoughts that are very self-obsessed and will never be satisfied. Very concretized as well. So, they're not malleable, not particularly flexible. So you're just adding to them by thinking about them all the time. You're creating narratives and stories and identities. Um but that's different from solving personal problems. So if you encounter something, you learn from something, you're reflecting upon the learning, then you can reflect upon it, absorb it, and then just move on. But sitting there saying, "I'm Chris. I'm Nal. I deserve this. This happened to me. That person wronged me. This is who I am. This shouldn't have happened."
turns.zh: 那是自我。但这个词被过度使用了，我有点讨厌用这个词。嗯，但它就像一系列反复出现的、非常自我迷恋且永不满足的想法。也非常具体化。所以它们不易改变，不太灵活。你通过一直思考它们来给它们添砖加瓦。你在创造叙事、故事和身份。嗯，但这与解决个人问题不同。所以如果你遇到某事，从某事中学习，你在反思所学，那么你可以反思它，吸收它，然后继续前进。但坐在那里说：“我是克里斯。我是纳瓦尔。我应得这个。这发生在我身上。那个人冤枉了我。这就是我。这不应该发生。”

turns.en: I need to go get revenge on this or I need to fix that or change this. I think that is where a lot of mental illness comes from. So it depends if you are thinking about something to solve a problem and get it off your chest and get it off your mind. If it leaves your mind clearer at the end of it, then I think it was worthwhile. If it leaves your mind busier at the end of it, then you're probably going the wrong direction.
turns.zh: 我需要去报复这个，或者我需要修复那个，改变这个。我认为很多心理疾病就来源于此。所以，这取决于你思考某件事是为了解决问题、把它从心里吐出来、从脑海里清除掉。如果思考之后你的头脑更清晰了，那我认为是值得的。如果思考之后你的头脑更混乱了，那你可能走错了方向。

turns.en: Is this a justification for detachment, cultivated ignorance, distraction?
turns.zh: 这是否为超然、刻意无知、分心提供了理由？

turns.en: Detachment is not a goal. Detachment is a byproduct. It's just a byproduct of realizing what matters and what doesn't. And just for one moment on the self thing, I think everybody craves thinking about something more than themselves. If you want to be happy to some extent, you have to forget about your personal problems. And one way to do that is take on other problems, bigger problems. That could be a mission, that could be spirituality, that could be kids, it could be caring about the planet, although I think people take that a little far, and then they get kind of oppressive and tyrannical in support of abstract concepts. But these can be taken too far, just like religion, just like anything in excess. Generally, the less you think about yourself, the more you can think about a mission or about God or about a child or something like that.
turns.zh: 超然不是目标，超然是副产品。它只是意识到什么重要、什么不重要的副产品。关于自我这件事，我想说，每个人都渴望思考比自己更宏大的事物。如果你想在一定程度上快乐，就必须忘记自己的个人问题。一个方法是承担其他问题，更大的问题。那可以是一个使命，可以是灵性，可以是孩子，可以是关心地球——尽管我认为有些人做得有点过，变得压抑和专制，支持抽象概念。但这些都可能过度，就像宗教，就像任何过度的事物。一般来说，你越少想自己，就越能思考使命、上帝、孩子之类的东西。

turns.en: I remember Vinnie Himmath, the founder of Loom, said, 'I am rich and I have no idea what to do with my life,' and you replied, 'God, kids, on mission — pick at least one.' That's right, preferably all three. It's very liberating. I think overthinking about yourself is probably not the cause of depression, but it certainly doesn't help rumination.
turns.zh: 我记得Loom的创始人Vinnie Himmath说：‘我很有钱，但不知道该怎么过我的生活。’你回答说：‘上帝、孩子、使命——至少选一个。’没错，最好三个都选。这非常解放。我认为过度思考自己可能不是抑郁的原因，但它肯定无助于反刍思维。

turns.en: Yeah, I kind of had a self-induced Stockholm syndrome from this sort of thing because I like to think about stuff, and you provide an endless number of things to think about. So you're the prisoner and the prison guard at the same time.
turns.zh: 是的，我有点因此患上了自我诱导的斯德哥尔摩综合征，因为我喜欢思考，而你又提供了无穷无尽可思考的东西。所以你同时是囚犯和狱警。

turns.en: I had Abigail Shrier on the show. She wrote this book called Bad Therapy, pushing back against therapy culture for kids. There was a blast radius that covered pretty much everything, including CBT. I'm like, we're getting perilously close to some really evidence-based stuff here. But the more I've thought about it and looked at the evidence, there is basically a direct correlation between how much you think about yourself and how miserable you are. Therapy is great if it lets you vent and solves a thing, and then X sessions later you're done, you're clear. But if you're just looping on the same thing forever, then it's actually the opposite. You're bathing in it, you're indulging in it.
turns.zh: 我请过Abigail Shrier上节目。她写了《Bad Therapy》这本书，反对针对孩子的治疗文化。它的波及范围几乎涵盖了一切，包括CBT。我觉得，我们正危险地接近一些非常循证的东西。但我想得越多，看得越多，基本上发现，你思考自己的程度和你的痛苦程度有直接关联。治疗如果让你发泄并解决一个问题，几次后你就结束了、清晰了，那很好。但如果你永远在同一个问题上循环，那实际上适得其反。你在其中沐浴，你在沉溺。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: How have your happiness techniques developed over time?
turns.zh: 你的快乐技巧是如何随时间发展的？

turns.en: I used to have a lot of them. Now I kind of try not to have any because I think the techniques themselves are kind of a struggle. It's sort of like bidding for status implies you're low status. It reveals that you're low status. So someone who's trying to show off comes across as low status. The same way someone who's trying to be happy is sort of saying 'I'm unhappy' and creating that frame. So it's better just not to even think in terms of happiness or unhappiness anymore. I just kind of do my thing.
turns.zh: 我以前有很多技巧。现在我尽量没有，因为我觉得技巧本身就是一种挣扎。就像竞标地位暗示你地位低，它暴露了你地位低。所以试图炫耀的人显得地位低。同样，试图快乐的人等于在说‘我不快乐’，并创造了那个框架。所以最好甚至不再用快乐或不快乐来思考。我只是做我的事。

turns.en: Another question similar to a bunch of them: Do you think you could have gotten there had you not done the procedural, systematic, step-by-step approach and then come out the other side?
turns.zh: 另一个类似的问题：你认为如果你没有经历那种程序化、系统化、一步一步的方法然后走出来，你能达到那种状态吗？

turns.en: I don't think there are any formulas. I think it's unique to each person. It's like asking a successful person, 'How did you become successful?' Each one will give you a different story. You can't follow anyone else's path. And most of them are probably telling you some narratized version that isn't quite true. I continually realize that, especially as I spend more time around successful people. You hear, 'It's very important to prioritize work-life balance.' That's one of the most common things people who have attained success say. That's not my experience. But you shouldn't be asking somebody who is successful what they do to continue their success now. You should be asking them what they did to attain their success when they were where you were. And the people who are really extraordinarily successful didn't sit around watching success porn. They just went and did it. They had such an overwhelming desire to be successful at the thing they were doing that they just went and did that thing. They didn't have time to study and learn and listen; they just did it. It's the overwhelming desire that's the most important, and the focus that comes from that.
turns.zh: 我不认为有什么公式。我认为每个人都是独特的。就像问一个成功人士：‘你是怎么成功的？’每个人都会给你不同的故事。你无法复制别人的路。而且他们大多数可能告诉你一个不太真实的叙事版本。我不断意识到这一点，尤其是当我花更多时间与成功人士相处时。你会听到：‘优先考虑工作与生活的平衡非常重要。’这是成功人士最常说的话之一。那不是我的经历。但你不应该问一个成功人士现在如何维持成功。你应该问他们当初在你那个位置时是如何取得成功的。而那些真正非凡成功的人并没有坐着看成功学。他们直接去做了。他们对所做的事情有如此强烈的成功欲望，以至于他们直接去做了。他们没有时间学习、倾听；他们只是去做。强烈的欲望最重要，以及由此产生的专注。

turns.en: That tweet of yours: 'People who are good at making wealth don't need to teach anybody else how to do it.' You don't need mentors, you need action. Another one: 'The people who actually know how to make money don't need to sell you a course on it.' There it is. And another: 'If you don't lie awake at night thinking about it, you don't want it badly enough.'
turns.zh: 你的那条推文：‘擅长创造财富的人不需要教别人怎么做。’你不需要导师，你需要行动。另一条：‘真正知道如何赚钱的人不需要卖课给你。’就是这样。还有一条：‘如果你不会夜不能寐地思考它，那你就不够想要。’

turns.en: Yeah, I think you've heard me talk before about how unclosed loops, problems you're working on, can cause you to be sleepless. And I'm not a good sleeper. Tell me about that.
turns.zh: 是的，我想你听过我之前说过，未闭合的循环、你正在处理的问题会导致失眠。我睡眠不好。跟我说说这个。

turns.en: Oh, I mean my Eight Sleep hates me. It's always telling me I failed at sleeping again. And Brian Johnson thinks I'm going to die early. He's probably right. How much do you reckon you sleep a night? Any idea?
turns.zh: 哦，我的Eight Sleep恨我。它总说我睡眠又失败了。Brian Johnson觉得我会早死。他可能没错。你估计你每晚睡多少？有概念吗？

turns.en: It's so random. Some nights I'll sleep 8 hours. Some nights I'll sleep 4 hours. It's literally just random.
turns.zh: 非常随机。有些晚上我睡8小时，有些晚上睡4小时。完全是随机的。

turns.en: Are you bothered about that? Are you trying to optimize? Do you have a sleep coach teaching you?
turns.zh: 你为此烦恼吗？你在尝试优化吗？有睡眠教练教你吗？

turns.en: I don't fogg myself over things. If I want to sleep, I'll sleep. If I don't want to sleep, I don't sleep. I don't think I'm doing anything right or wrong. I don't label it good night, bad night. I work out every day because I think it gives me more energy and I've gotten into a good habit with it. Maybe I'll do the same thing with sleep. Maybe I'll develop a good habit, but I'm not going to beat myself up over it. There'll come a point where it's important to me, and when it's important to me, I'll just do it.
turns.zh: 我不为事情纠结。想睡就睡，不想睡就不睡。我不觉得自己在做对或错的事。我不给它贴上好夜、坏夜的标签。我每天锻炼，因为我觉得它给我更多能量，我已经养成了好习惯。也许我会对睡眠做同样的事，也许我会养成好习惯，但我不会为此自责。总有一天它会变得对我重要，当它重要时，我就会去做。

turns.en: You know, most of like, for example, you look at people with addictions, right? Overeating or smoking or whatever, they can kind of go through all the different methods, but it's half-hearted. And then one day they're like, 'Oh I've got lung cancer. My dad has lung cancer.' and they drop it immediately. So I think a lot of change is more about desire and understanding than it is about forcing yourself or trying to domesticate yourself. It's efficiency again, I guess, aligning the thing that you want to do with the way that you feel about what it is that you want to do. Yeah. It's not getting caught up in a half desire or a mimetic desire. It's really just being aware of what it is that you actually want at this point in time. And when you want something, then you will act on it with maximal capability. And that's the time to act on it. In the meantime, just doing it because other people tell you you should do it or society tells you you should do it or you feel slightly guilty about it. These are half-hearted efforts and half-hearted efforts don't get you there.
turns.zh: 你知道，大多数情况下，比如你看那些有瘾的人，对吧？暴饮暴食或吸烟之类的，他们可能会尝试各种方法，但都是三心二意的。然后有一天他们突然说，‘哦，我得了肺癌。我爸爸得了肺癌。’然后他们立刻就戒掉了。所以我认为很多改变更多是关于渴望和理解，而不是强迫自己或试图驯服自己。这又是效率问题，我想，就是把你想要做的事情和你对这件事的感受对齐。是的。不是陷入半心半意的欲望或模仿欲望。而是真正意识到此刻你真正想要的是什么。当你想要某样东西时，你就会以最大的能力去行动。那才是行动的时候。与此同时，仅仅因为别人告诉你应该做，或者社会告诉你应该做，或者你感到有点内疚而去做，这些都是半心半意的努力，半心半意的努力不会让你达到目标。

turns.zh: 随着年龄增长，有一件事越来越难以忽视，那就是你的睾酮水平。它影响一切，从你的精力到情绪，再到恢复能力。然而，有一些科学支持的补充剂，比如 Tonat，可以支持睾酮强度和恢复。这就是为什么我非常喜欢 Momentus 的产品，因为他们制造了地球上唯一经过 NSF 认证的 Tonat Ali。这意味着它经过了如此严格的测试，甚至奥运选手都可以使用，这也是我与他们合作的原因，因为他们制造了地球上经过最仔细测试、质量最高的补充剂。这是我大脑、身体和睡眠方案的重要组成部分，对我的认知、力量和恢复有很大影响。如果你仍然不确定，Momentous 提供 30 天退款保证。所以你可以购买并试用 29 天。如果你不喜欢，他们会退款给你。此外，他们还提供国际运输。现在，你可以通过点击下方描述中的链接或访问 livemus.com/modernwisdomd 并在结账时使用代码 modernwisdom，获得订阅 35% 的折扣和 30 天退款保证。网址是 l i v m o m e n t o.com/modernwisdom，结账时使用 modern wisdom。

turns.en: You mentioned anxiety before. Imagine how effective you'd be if you weren't anxious all the time is one of yours and anxiety is the emotion dour of the 21st century and lots of driven people very anxious very paranoid that's what's caused them to be affected. They pay so much attention detail oriented not letting things go staying up at night thinking about it. That's the paranoia coming in. What have you come to learn about anxiety and dealing with it?
turns.zh: 你之前提到过焦虑。想象一下，如果你不是一直焦虑，你会多么高效，这是你的观点之一。焦虑是21世纪的情绪阴霾，很多有动力的人非常焦虑、非常偏执，这正是他们受到影响的原因。他们过于关注细节，不放手，晚上熬夜思考。这就是偏执的表现。你对焦虑和应对它有什么了解？

turns.en: So, anxiety and stress are interesting. They're very related. Stress is when, like if you look at an iron beam, when an iron beam is under stress, it's because it's being bent in two different directions at the same time. So, when your mind is under stress, it's because it has two conflicting desires at once. So, for example, you want to be liked, but you want to do something selfish, and you can't reconcile the two, and so you're under stress. You want to do something for somebody else, you want to do something for yourself, right? You don't want to go to work but you want to make money so you're under stress. So you have two conflicting desires and I think one of the ways to get through stress is to acknowledge that, oh I actually have two conflicting desires and either I need to resolve it, I need to pick one and then be okay losing the other, or I will decide later but at least just being aware of why you're stressed can help alleviate a lot of stress. And then anxiety I think is sort of this pervasive unidentifiable stress where you're just kind of stressed out all the time and you're not even sure why and you can't even identify the underlying problem. I think the reason for that is because you have so many unresolved problems, unresolved stress points that have piled up in your life that you can no longer identify what the problems are and there's this mountain of garbage in your mind and it's a little bit of it poking out the top like an iceberg and that's anxiety. But underneath there's a lot of unresolved things. And so you just need to kind of go through very carefully every time you're anxious. Like, okay, why am I anxious this time? I don't know why. Oh, well, let me sit here and just think about it. Let me write down what the possible causes could be. Let me meditate on it. Let me journal. Let me talk to a therapist. Let me talk to my friends. Let me just kind of see like when does that stress go away? If you can kind of identify and unravel and resolve these issues, then I think that helps get rid of anxiety. A lot of the anxiety is piled up because we move through life too quickly, not observing our own reactions to things. We don't resolve them. So, this goes counter to what I was saying earlier about not reflecting too much on things, but you reflect on the problems to observe them and solve them. You don't reflect on them to feel better about yourself, to indulge them. Well, if you're doing it to just feel better about yourself, that could be strengthening your personality and your ego and could be creating a more fragile personality.
turns.zh: 所以，焦虑和压力很有趣。它们非常相关。压力就像，如果你看一根铁梁，当铁梁承受压力时，是因为它同时被向两个不同方向弯曲。所以，当你的大脑承受压力时，是因为它同时有两个相互冲突的欲望。例如，你想被人喜欢，但你又想做自私的事，你无法调和两者，所以你感到压力。你想为别人做点什么，也想为自己做点什么，对吧？你不想去上班，但你想赚钱，所以你感到压力。所以你有两个相互冲突的欲望，我认为克服压力的方法之一是承认，哦，我实际上有两个相互冲突的欲望，要么我需要解决它，我需要选择一个，然后接受失去另一个，要么我稍后再决定，但至少意识到你为什么有压力可以帮助减轻很多压力。然后焦虑，我认为是一种普遍存在的、无法识别的压力，你只是一直感到压力，甚至不确定为什么，甚至无法识别潜在的问题。我认为原因是你在生活中有太多未解决的问题、未解决的压力点堆积起来，以至于你无法再识别问题是什么，你的脑海里有一座垃圾山，只有一点点像冰山一样露出水面，那就是焦虑。但下面有很多未解决的事情。所以你需要每次焦虑时非常仔细地审视。比如，好吧，我这次为什么焦虑？我不知道为什么。哦，好吧，让我坐在这里想一想。让我写下可能的原因。让我冥想一下。让我写日记。让我和治疗师谈谈。让我和朋友谈谈。让我看看那种压力什么时候消失？如果你能识别、解开并解决这些问题，那么我认为这有助于消除焦虑。很多焦虑是因为我们生活得太快，没有观察自己对事物的反应。我们没有解决它们。所以，这与我之前说的不要过度反思相悖，但你要反思问题以观察和解决它们。你不是为了自我感觉良好或沉溺其中而反思。如果你只是为了自我感觉良好，那可能会强化你的个性和自我，并可能创造一个更脆弱的个性。

turns.en: One big anxiety resolver for me is just ruminating on death. I think that's a good one. You're going to die. It's all going to zero. You cannot take anything with you. And I know this is trite. And I know we don't spend enough time thinking about the big questions. We kind of give up on them when we're very, very young. You know, a little child might ask the big questions like, why are we here? What's the meaning of life? What is this all about? You know, is there Santa Claus? Is there God? But then as adults, we're taught not to think about these things or we've given up on them. But I think the big questions are the big questions for good reasons. And if you can keep the idea in front of you at all times that you're going to die and that everything goes literally to zero. What's there to stress about?
turns.zh: 对我来说，一个很大的焦虑解决方法是思考死亡。我认为这是一个好方法。你将会死去。一切都会归零。你什么也带不走。我知道这很老套。我知道我们没有花足够的时间思考大问题。我们在很小的时候就放弃了它们。你知道，一个小孩可能会问大问题，比如，我们为什么在这里？生命的意义是什么？这一切是怎么回事？你知道，有圣诞老人吗？有上帝吗？但作为成年人，我们被教导不要思考这些事情，或者我们已经放弃了它们。但我认为大问题之所以是大问题是有充分理由的。如果你能时刻牢记你将会死去，一切都会归零。那还有什么好压力的呢？

turns.en: For better or worse, life is very short. How should people deal with its briefness?
turns.zh: 无论好坏，生命都很短暂。人们应该如何应对它的短暂？

turns.en: Enjoy it. Make the best of it. You know, it's even briefer than that. Each moment just disappears. It's gone. There's only a present moment and it's gone instantly. So if you're not there for it, if you're stressed out or you're anxious or you're thinking about something else, you missed it. So any moment when you're not in that moment, you are dead to that moment. You might as well be dead because your mind is off doing something else or living in some imagined reality that is just a very poor substitute for the actual reality.
turns.zh: 享受它。充分利用它。你知道，它甚至比那更短暂。每一刻都消失了。它不见了。只有一个当下，它瞬间就消失了。所以如果你不在那里，如果你压力很大或焦虑，或者你在想别的事情，你就错过了它。所以任何你不在当下的时刻，你对那个时刻来说就是死的。你不如死了，因为你的思绪在别处，或者生活在某种想象的现实中，那只是对真实现实的非常糟糕的替代。

transcript.sec: On Wasted Time and Being Present

turns.en: So one of my recent realizations was what is wasted time? I don't like to waste time, but what is wasted time? Everything is wasted time in a sense because nothing matters in the ultimate. But in each moment, the thing matters. In each moment, it's the only thing that matters. Actually, what's happening in front of you literally has all the meaning in the world. So what matters is just being present for the thing. If you're doing something that you want to do and you're fully there for it, then it's not wasted time. If you don't want to do it and your mind is running away from it, reacting against it, wishing you were somewhere else, thinking about some other thing, anticipating some future, regretting some past, or being fearful of something, then that's wasted time. That's time being wasted when you're not actually present for the reality in front of you. So my definition of wasted time: yes, I do want some material things in life, and there are things that have more value than others within this life, but this life is very short and bounded. So the true wasted time is time that you're not present for, when you are not there for it. When you're not doing the thing you want to do to the best of your capability such that you're immersed in it. If you're not immersed in this moment, then you're wasting your time.
turns.zh: 所以我最近的一个领悟是：什么是浪费的时间？我不喜欢浪费时间，但什么是浪费的时间？从某种意义上说，一切都是浪费，因为最终什么都不重要。但在每一个当下，那件事很重要。在每一个当下，它是唯一重要的事。实际上，你眼前发生的一切就是世界上所有的意义。所以重要的是你全身心投入其中。如果你在做一件你想做的事，并且完全沉浸其中，那就不是浪费时间。如果你不想做，你的心在逃避、抗拒，希望自己身处别处，想着别的事，期待未来，后悔过去，或者害怕什么，那就是浪费时间。那是你没有真正面对眼前现实而浪费的时间。所以我对浪费时间的定义是：是的，我确实想要一些物质上的东西，生活中有些东西比其他的更有价值，但这一生非常短暂且有限。所以真正的浪费时间是你没有在场的时间，当你不在那里的时候。当你没有尽最大努力去做你想做的事，以至于你没有沉浸其中。如果你没有沉浸在这一刻，你就是在浪费时间。

turns.en: People get worried about dying and no longer being here, but they don't realize that so much of their life is spent not being here in any case.
turns.zh: 人们担心死亡，担心不再存在，但他们没有意识到，他们生命中的大部分时间本来就不在场。

turns.en: That's right. And I think people crave being here for it. And when you're here for it, you're actually not thinking about yourself. You are more immersed in the thing, the moment, the task at hand.
turns.zh: 没错。我认为人们渴望在场。当你真正在场时，你其实不会想着自己。你更沉浸于那件事、那个当下、手头的任务。

turns.en: We don't want peace of mind. We want peace for our mind.
turns.zh: 我们不是要内心的平静，我们要的是让内心平静。

turns.en: That's right. Yeah. You don't peace. The mind is what can eat you alive if you let it. And there's more to you than the mind.
turns.zh: 没错。是的。你不需要平静。如果你放任它，头脑会把你生吞活剥。你不仅仅是你的头脑。

turns.en: How so?
turns.zh: 怎么说？

turns.en: Well, I mean, at the end of the day, everything arises within your consciousness, right? You've got nowhere else to experience it. And that consciousness is relatively static in the sense that it's been exactly the same from the moment you were born to the moment you die. And everything that you experience—from your body, from your mind, to the world, to everything—is within that consciousness. And that thing, that base layer of being—this is what the Buddhists will tell you—is the real thing. Everything that comes and goes in the middle, including your mind, including your body, is unreal. Trying to find stability in those transient things is building your castle on sand that's going to crumble.
turns.zh: 嗯，我的意思是，归根结底，一切都在你的意识中升起，对吧？你没有其他地方可以体验它。而那个意识是相对静止的，从你出生到死亡，它始终如一。你所经历的一切——你的身体、你的头脑、世界、所有一切——都在那个意识之内。而那个东西，那个存在的基础层——佛教徒会告诉你——才是真实的。中间来来去去的一切，包括你的头脑，包括你的身体，都是不真实的。试图在那些短暂的事物中寻找稳定，就像在沙子上建城堡，终将崩塌。

turns.en: Life is going to play out the way it's going to play out. There will be some good and some bad. Most of it is actually just up to your interpretation. You're born, you have a set of sensory experiences, and then you die. How you choose to interpret those experiences is up to you. And different people interpret them in different ways. The old line about two people walking down the street: they're having the exact same experience. One is happy, one is sad, right? It's a narrative in their heads. It's how they choose to interpret.
turns.zh: 生活会按照它本来的方式展开。会有一些好，一些坏。大部分其实取决于你的解读。你出生，经历一系列感官体验，然后死去。你如何选择解读这些体验，取决于你。不同的人以不同的方式解读。那句老话：两个人走在街上，经历完全相同。一个快乐，一个悲伤，对吧？那是他们头脑中的叙事，是他们选择解读的方式。

turns.en: So when I said that, it was a long time ago. I was talking more about having positive interpretations and negative interpretations. But these days, I think it's better just not to have any interpretations and to just allow things to be. You're still going to have interpretations. You can't stop it. Nor should you try. But even that having an interpretation is just a thing you can leave alone.
turns.zh: 所以当我说那句话时，那是很久以前了。我当时更多是在谈论正面和负面的解读。但现在，我认为最好根本不要有任何解读，只是让事物存在。你仍然会有解读。你无法阻止它。你也不应该试图阻止。但即使有解读，也只是你可以不去管它的一件事。

turns.en: I really want to try and just dig in a little more to the best way to remind people that they should value their time. Just how brief it is, that the time that you spend ruminating, being distracted, fears of the past, regrets.
turns.zh: 我真的很想再深入一点，找到最好的方式来提醒人们珍惜时间。生命如此短暂，你花在沉思、分心、对过去的恐惧、后悔上的时间。

turns.en: Well, I don't want to tell anybody how to live their life. I would just say that to the extent that you want to improve your quality of life, the easiest and best way to do that is to observe your own mind and your own thoughts and be a little—not necessarily critical—but be observant of yourself more objectively. Then you'll kind of realize your own loops and patterns. It takes time. It's not overnight. It's not instantaneous.
turns.zh: 嗯，我不想告诉任何人该怎么生活。我只想说，如果你想提高生活质量，最简单最好的方法就是观察你自己的头脑和思想，稍微——不一定是批判性的——而是更客观地观察自己。然后你就会意识到自己的循环和模式。这需要时间。不是一夜之间。不是瞬间的。

turns.en: So you mean letting go is not a one-time event.
turns.zh: 所以你的意思是放手不是一次性的。

turns.en: Yeah. And letting go is not necessarily even the right answer. Like yes, if you're trying to be an enlightened being and you want to live like a god and everything's going to be perfect and be a Buddha, sure you can let go. But in practice, it's actually quite hard to do. I think I would say that you're going to find a lot of fulfillment out of life by just doing what you want to do and genuinely exploring what it is that you want, rather than doing what other people expect you to do or society expects you to do or what you might just think should be done by default. I think most older successful people will tell you that their life was best when they lived it unapologetically on their own terms. Be selfish. Holistic selfishness.
turns.zh: 是的。而且放手甚至不一定是对的答案。比如，如果你想成为一个开悟的人，想活得像个神，一切完美，成为佛陀，那你当然可以放手。但在实践中，这其实很难做到。我想说的是，你会从生活中找到很多满足感，只要做你想做的事，真正探索你想要什么，而不是做别人期望你做的事，或者社会期望你做的事，或者你只是认为默认应该做的事。我认为大多数年长成功的人会告诉你，当他们毫无歉意地按照自己的方式生活时，他们的人生最好。要自私。整体的自私。

turns.en: We can clip that little selfish. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Keep running back. Bad guy. Great.
turns.zh: 我们可以剪掉那个小自私。是的。是的。是的。继续回放。坏家伙。太好了。

turns.en: I had this insight or a question, I guess. How much do you think that we should trust the voice in our heads? Because half of wisdom suggests to rely on your sort of bottom-up intuition and then half of it has to be sort of top-down rational as possible. How do you navigate the tension between head and gut in this way?
turns.zh: 我有一个见解或问题，我想。你认为我们应该多大程度上相信头脑中的声音？因为一半的智慧建议依靠自下而上的直觉，另一半则必须尽可能自上而下地理性。你如何在这种方式下处理头脑和直觉之间的张力？

turns.en: I think the gut is what decides. The head is kind of what rationalizes it afterwards. The gut is the ultimate decision maker. If it doesn't... And what is the gut? The gut is refined judgment. It's taste aggregated, and it could be aggregated through evolution—it's in your genes and your DNA—or it could be aggregated through your experiences and what you've thought through. The mind is good at solving new problems and new problems in the external world that have defined edges—beginnings and ends and objectives. What the mind is actually really bad at is making hard decisions. So when you have a hard decision to make, I find it's better to ruminate on it, think through all the pros and cons, but then sleep on it. Wait a couple of days. Wait until the gut answer appears with conviction and it feels right. When you're younger, it takes longer because you just don't have as much experience. When you're older, it can happen much faster, which is why you have less time to...
turns.zh: 我认为是直觉在做决定。头脑只是在事后将其合理化。直觉是最终的决策者。如果它不……什么是直觉？直觉是精炼的判断。它是品味的集合，可以通过进化积累——存在于你的基因和DNA中——或者通过你的经历和思考积累。头脑擅长解决新问题，以及外部世界中那些有明确边界的问题——有始有终和目标。头脑真正不擅长的是做艰难的决定。所以当你面临艰难决定时，我发现最好是先思考，权衡所有利弊，然后睡一觉。等几天。等到直觉的答案带着确信出现，感觉对了。当你年轻时，这需要更长时间，因为你没有那么多经验。当你年长时，它可以发生得更快，这就是为什么你时间更少……

turns.en: And old people just more set in their ways as a consequence, right? They know what they want. They know what they don't want. So it takes time to develop your gut instinct and judgment. But once you've developed them, don't trust anything else because you can't go against your gut. It'll bite you in the end. Usually in relationships that failed, you can look back and say, 'Oh, actually, I knew it was going to fail because of this reason, but I kind of went ahead anyway because I wanted it to be this way, right? I wanted this person to be a different way than they are, or I wanted to get a different thing out of it than I thought I was going to than I knew I was going to get, but I just wanted it.' So sometimes desire will override your judgment and then trap.
turns.zh: 老年人也因此更固执己见，对吧？他们知道自己想要什么，不想要什么。所以培养直觉和判断力需要时间。但一旦你培养好了，就别再相信其他任何东西，因为你不能违背自己的直觉。最终它会反咬你一口。通常在失败的感情中，你回头看看会说：‘哦，其实我早知道它会因为某个原因失败，但我还是继续了，因为我希望它变成那样，对吧？我希望这个人变成另一种样子，或者我想从中得到一些我知道得不到的东西，但我就是想要。’所以有时候欲望会压倒你的判断，然后把你困住。

turns.en: Wishful thinking. It traps you into a pathway that chews up time.
turns.zh: 一厢情愿。它把你困在一条消耗时间的路上。

turns.en: What's that inside of yours? We think we can't change ourselves but we can. We think we can change other people but we can't.
turns.zh: 你内心是怎么想的？我们以为自己无法改变自己，但其实可以。我们以为自己能改变别人，但其实不能。

turns.en: Exactly. I think to add to that you can't change other people. You can change your reaction to them. You can change yourself but other people only change through trauma or their own insight on their own schedule and never in a way that you like.
turns.zh: 完全正确。我想补充一点，你无法改变别人。你可以改变自己对他们的反应。你可以改变自己，但别人只会通过创伤或自己的领悟，按照自己的节奏改变，而且永远不会以你喜欢的方式。

turns.en: Yeah. Alanderon says that people do sometimes change but rarely in relationships and never when they're told to.
turns.zh: 是的。Alanderon 说过，人有时确实会改变，但在感情中很少，而且永远不会在被要求的时候改变。

turns.en: Absolutely. The fastest way to alienate somebody is to tell them to change. In fact, the Dale Carnegie School of Public Speaking, the way that operates is, they get you up there and they realize that the number one problem with public speaking is that people are very self-conscious. So people who are practicing in the Dale Carnegie School of Public Speaking, I don't know, I never went through, I heard this secondhand so I could be wrong, but I like the story where they get up and they start speaking and the people in the audience are only allowed to compliment them, genuine compliments not fake compliments, on things that they did well, but you're not allowed to criticize them on things that they did poorly. Eventually they kind of get through it and they develop the self-confidence. The same way there's like the Michelle Thomas school of language learning. On that one, what they do is you listen to a teacher talking to a student. They're not teaching you, you're not expected to remember or memorize anything. You just listen to a student stumbling over the language. It's a better way to learn because you yourself don't feel flustered. You're not being tested, graded.
turns.zh: 绝对。最快疏远一个人的方法就是告诉他要改变。事实上，戴尔·卡耐基公众演讲学校是这样运作的：他们让你上台，然后意识到公众演讲的头号问题是人们非常自我意识过强。所以那些在戴尔·卡耐基公众演讲学校练习的人——我没上过，我是听说的，所以可能不对，但我喜欢这个故事——他们站起来开始演讲，观众只被允许赞美他们，真诚的赞美，不是假赞美，针对他们做得好的地方，但不允许批评他们做得不好的地方。最终他们挺过来了，建立了自信。同样，还有米歇尔·托马斯语言学习法。那种方法是你听老师和一个学生对话。他们不是在教你，你不需要记住或背诵任何东西。你只是听一个学生在语言上磕磕绊绊。这是一种更好的学习方式，因为你自己不会感到慌乱。你没有被测试、打分。

turns.en: Oh, so you're not in your own head as much.
turns.zh: 哦，所以你不会那么自我纠结。

turns.en: Correct. You're not in your own head and you're just, you might even be laughing at the student or you might be agreeing with the teacher or vice versa or sympathizing with the student. But because you are a passive observer, you can be more objective about it and you aren't threatened or fearful and you can learn better. And coming back to the original point of you can't change people. If you do want to change someone's behavior, I think the only effective way to do it is to compliment them when they do something you want. Not to positive.
turns.zh: 对。你不会自我纠结，你甚至可能在嘲笑那个学生，或者同意老师，或者反过来，或者同情那个学生。但因为你是被动的观察者，你可以更客观，不会感到威胁或恐惧，学习效果更好。回到最初的观点：你无法改变别人。如果你真的想改变某人的行为，我认为唯一有效的方法是在他们做了你希望的事情时赞美他们。不是正面强化。

turns.en: Exactly. Not to insult them or be negative or critical when they do something you don't want. And we can't help it. It's obviously in our nature to criticize. And I do it as well, but it just reminds me that like when somebody does something praiseworthy, don't forget to praise them. Like definitely go out of your way and it'll be genuine. It has to be genuine. It can't be a fake thing. This is not one of those dropping compliments type thing, eventually people will see through that. They want authenticity, but just don't forget to praise people when they do something praiseworthy and you'll get more of that behavior.
turns.zh: 完全正确。不是在他们做了你不希望的事情时侮辱他们或消极批评。我们忍不住，批评显然是我们天性。我也这样做，但这提醒我，当有人做了值得称赞的事情时，别忘了赞美他们。一定要特意去做，而且要真诚。必须是真诚的，不能是假的。这不是那种随便丢几句赞美的事，最终人们会看穿。他们想要真实，但只是别忘了在别人做了值得称赞的事情时赞美他们，你就会得到更多那种行为。

turns.en: There was a really famous thread on Reddit about five questions to ask yourself if you're uncertain about your relationship. One of the questions was, 'Are you truly in love with your partner or just their potential or the idea of them?' And that's the, you know, they show such great promise. They look at their ability for change and growth. They're on the right path. The partner matching thing is so hard. You know, when people come and ask me like, 'Oh, should I be with this person?' Like, 'Well, if you're asking me, the answer is clearly no.' Right? Because you wouldn't have to ask if you were with the right person. Or when you ask someone like why they're in a relationship with somebody and they start reading out his or her resume, right? That's also a bad sign.
turns.zh: Reddit 上有一个非常著名的帖子，列出了五个问题，如果你对感情不确定，可以问自己。其中一个问题是：‘你真正爱的是你的伴侣，还是他们的潜力或你心中的形象？’你知道，他们展现出很大的希望。他们看重对方改变和成长的能力。他们走在正确的道路上。伴侣匹配这件事太难了。你知道，当有人来问我：‘哦，我应该和这个人在一起吗？’我的回答是：‘嗯，如果你问我，答案显然是否定的。’对吧？因为如果你和合适的人在一起，你根本不需要问。或者当你问某人为什么和某人在一起，他们开始念对方的简历，对吧？那也是一个坏信号。

turns.en: What do you mean?
turns.zh: 什么意思？

turns.en: Oh, it's like, 'Oh, we have so much in common. We like to golf together.' It's like, it's not a basis for a relationship. Or, 'Oh, you know, she's a ballerina or, you know, he went to Harvard or what have you.' These are resume items. That's not who the person actually is.
turns.zh: 哦，就像：‘哦，我们有这么多共同点。我们喜欢一起打高尔夫。’这可不是感情的基础。或者：‘哦，你知道，她是芭蕾舞演员，或者他上了哈佛之类的。’这些都是简历上的东西。那不是这个人的真实面目。

turns.en: What's a better answer?
turns.zh: 那更好的答案是什么？

turns.en: I just love being with this person. I just trust them. I enjoy being around them. I love how capable he is. I love how kind she is. I love her spirit. I love his energy. The more materially and concretely definable the reasons are you're together, the worse they are. The ineffable is actually where the sort of true love lies because real love is a form of unity. It's a form of connection. It's connecting spirits. Oh you two, my consciousness meets your consciousness. It's the underlying drive in love, in art, in science, in mysticism is the desire for unity. It's a desire for connection. As you know, Bourhees famously wrote, 'In every human, there's a sense that something infinite has been lost.' There's a God-shaped hole in you you're trying to fill. And so, we're always trying to find that connection. Love is trying to find it in one other person and saying, 'Okay, you're male, I'm female, or whatever.' And you know, whatever your predilections are, and now we connect, now we form a whole, a connected whole. Or in mysticism it's like it's all about okay sit down meditate and you'll feel the whole. In science it's like oh atoms bouncing is mechanics but that generates heat. So thermodynamics and motion or kinetics are one combined theory that's a whole. Electricity and magnetism are one thing that's the whole creates that sense of awe. In art it's like I feel an emotion I create a piece of art around it and then you see that painting or you see the Sistine Chapel or you read the poem and you feel that emotion. So again, it's creating unity. It's creating connection. And I think everybody craves that. And so when you really love somebody, it's because you feel a sense of wholeness by being around them. And that sense of wholeness probably doesn't have anything to do with what school they went to, you know, or what career they're in.
turns.zh: 我就是喜欢和这个人在一起。我就是信任他们。我喜欢和他们相处。我爱他的能力。我爱她的善良。我爱她的精神。我爱他的能量。你们在一起的理由越具体、越物质化，就越糟糕。难以言喻的东西才是真爱所在，因为真正的爱是一种统一，一种连接，是灵魂的连接。你和我，我的意识遇见你的意识。爱、艺术、科学、神秘主义背后的驱动力都是对统一的渴望，对连接的渴望。你知道，布尔热（Bourhees）有句名言：‘每个人内心都有一种感觉，好像失去了某种无限的东西。’你心里有一个上帝形状的洞，你试图去填补。所以我们总是在寻找那种连接。爱就是试图在另一个人身上找到它，然后说：‘好了，你是男性，我是女性，或者随便什么。’无论你的偏好是什么，现在我们连接了，我们形成了一个整体，一个连接的整体。或者在神秘主义中，就是坐下来冥想，你会感受到整体。在科学中，哦，原子碰撞是力学，但会产生热量。所以热力学和运动学或动力学是一个统一的理论，那是一个整体。电和磁是一回事，那个整体创造了敬畏感。在艺术中，我感受到一种情感，我围绕它创作一件艺术品，然后你看到那幅画，或者你看到西斯廷教堂，或者你读到那首诗，你感受到那种情感。所以，这又是在创造统一，创造连接。我认为每个人都渴望这个。所以当你真正爱一个人时，是因为你在他们身边感到一种完整感。而这种完整感很可能与他们上过什么学校或从事什么职业无关。

turns.en: Just sort of tying that into the life is short, stop about. If you're faced with a difficult choice and you cannot decide, the answer is no. And the reason is modern society is full of options. Knowing this rationally sounds great, but having the courage to commit to it in reality, I think is a different task. And cutting your losses quickly in the big three, relationships, jobs, and locations is hard. What would you say to someone who may cerebrally be able to agree with you and say, 'I understand.'
turns.zh: 把这一点联系到‘人生苦短，该停就停’。如果你面临一个艰难的选择，无法决定，答案就是‘不’。原因是现代社会充满了选项。理性上知道这一点听起来很棒，但现实中要有勇气去践行，我觉得是另一回事。在三大关键领域——关系、工作和地点——迅速止损很难。你会对那些在理智上同意你、说‘我理解’的人说什么？

turns.en: My cousin said this about me. He said that what I've really noticed about you is your ability to walk away from situations that are just not great enough for you or not good enough for you. And I think that is a characteristic that I have. I will not accept second best outcomes in my life. Ultimately you will end up wherever is acceptable to you. You will get out of life whatever is acceptable to you. There are certain things to me that are very important where I will not settle for second best. But then there are a lot of other things I just don't care about because if I spend all my time caring about those things, I don't have the energy for the few things that matter. So in decision-making, I have a few heuristics for myself. Other people can use their own, but mine are: if you can't decide, the answer is no. If you're offered an opportunity, if you have a new thing that you're saying yes or no to that is a change from where you're starting, the answer is by default always no. Secondly, if you have two decisions, if you have A or B and both seem very equal, take the path that's more painful in the short term, the one that's going to be painful immediately because your brain is always trying to avoid pain. So any pain that is imminent, it is going to treat as much larger than it actually is. This is kind of like the decision-making equivalent of Taleb's surgeon. Taleb says you want the surgeon that doesn't look as good because he's more likely to be a good surgeon. It's similar in that appearances are deceiving because you're avoiding conflict. You're avoiding pain. So take the path that is more painful in the short term because your brain is creating this illusion that the short-term pain is greater than the long-term pain. Because long-term, you'll commit your future self to all kinds of long-term mañana. Exactly mañana. So take the more short-term painful one. And then finally the last one which I would credit Kapo Gupta with is that you want to take the choice that will leave you more equanimous in the long term. By equanimous he means more at peace, more mental peace in the long term. So whatever clears your mind more and will have you having less self-talk in the future, if you can model that out, that is probably the better route to go.
turns.zh: 我表弟曾这样评价我。他说：‘我注意到你最大的特点就是能够离开那些对你来说不够好、不够棒的局面。’我认为这是我的一个特质。我不会接受人生中次优的结果。最终，你会停留在你所能接受的地方。你从生活中得到的，就是你所能接受的。有些事对我非常重要，我不会将就。但还有很多其他事我根本不在乎，因为如果我把时间都花在那些事上，我就没有精力去关注那几件真正重要的事了。所以在做决定时，我有自己的一套启发式规则。别人可以用他们自己的，但我的规则是：如果你无法决定，答案就是‘不’。如果你得到一个机会，一个需要你回答‘是’或‘否’的新事物，而它是对你现状的改变，那么默认答案永远是‘不’。第二，如果你有两个选择，A或B，而且看起来差不多，那就选短期更痛苦的那个，那个会立刻带来痛苦的选择，因为你的大脑总是试图逃避痛苦。所以任何迫在眉睫的痛苦，它都会放大。这有点像塔勒布的外科医生悖论。塔勒布说，你要找那个看起来不那么好的外科医生，因为他更可能是好医生。类似地，表象具有欺骗性，因为你是在逃避冲突、逃避痛苦。所以选择短期更痛苦的路，因为你的大脑制造了一种错觉，认为短期痛苦大于长期痛苦。而长期来看，你会把未来的自己拖入各种‘明天再说’的困境。所以选短期更痛苦的那个。最后一条，我要归功于卡波·古普塔：你要选择那个能让你长期更平静的选择。‘平静’指的是更平和、更心安。所以，哪个选择能让你头脑更清晰、未来内心独白更少，如果你能模拟出来，那可能就是更好的路。

turns.en: And then I would focus decision-making down on the three things that really matter because everything else is downstream of these three decisions. Especially these are early life decisions. Later in life, you have different things to optimize for. But early in life, you're trying to figure out who you're with, what you're doing, and where you live. And I think on all three of those, you want to think pretty hard about it. And people do some of these unconsciously. You know, who you're with very often it's like, well, we were in a relationship. We stumbled along. It felt okay. It had been enough time, so we got married. Right? Not great reasons. Maybe not terrible reasons either. I mean, people who overthink these things sometimes don't get the right answer. But maybe here, if you're the kind of person that's not going to settle for second best, you iterate. You iterate on a closed time frame so you don't run out the clock. And then you decide on what you do. You try a whole bunch of different things until you find the one that feels like play to you, looks like work to others. You can't lose at it. Get some leverage, try to find some practical application of it and go into that. And then where you live, where you live is really important. I don't think people spend enough time on that one. I think people pick cities randomly based on where I went to school or where my family happened to be or where my friend was or I visited one weekend. I really liked it. You really want to think it through because where you live really constrains and defines your opportunities. It will determine your friend circle, your dating pool, your job opportunities, the food and air and water quality that you receive. It will determine your proximity to your family, which might be important as you get older and have kids. So very, very important decision. Weather, quality of life. How much do you stay inside or outside? How long are you going to live based on that? And I think people choose that one probably more poorly than they put a lot less thought into that one than the other two.
turns.zh: 然后我会把决策聚焦在真正重要的三件事上，因为其他一切都取决于这三个决定。尤其是早期的人生决定。后期你会有不同的优化目标。但早期，你要弄清楚你和谁在一起、你做什么、以及你住在哪里。我认为在这三件事上，你需要认真思考。人们有时会无意识地做出这些决定。比如，和谁在一起，常常是‘我们在一起了，凑合着过，感觉还行，时间差不多了，就结婚了’。对吧？不是很好的理由，也许也不是太糟。我的意思是，过度思考的人有时也得不到正确答案。但也许，如果你是那种不愿将就的人，你会迭代。你在一个封闭的时间框架内迭代，以免时间耗尽。然后决定你做什么。你尝试很多不同的事情，直到找到那个对你来说像玩、对别人像工作的事。你不会输。获得一些杠杆，找到它的实际应用，然后深入进去。至于你住在哪里，这非常重要。我认为人们在这上面花的时间不够。人们选择城市往往是随机的——因为我在那里上学，或者我的家人恰好在那边，或者我的朋友在，或者我某个周末去玩很喜欢。你真的需要想清楚，因为你的居住地会限制和定义你的机会。它会决定你的朋友圈、约会对象、工作机会、食物、空气和水质。它会决定你离家人的远近，这在你年纪渐长、有了孩子后可能很重要。所以这是非常非常重要的决定。天气、生活质量。你待在室内还是室外的时间有多少？基于此你能活多久？我认为人们在这个选择上可能比另外两个更糟糕，投入的思考也更少。

turns.en: In some ways, yeah, but also the... You're so right. How many people fall backward into a relationship and before they know it, we're living together, we got a dog, we got a kid, we were married and you know, yeah. And then when you have kids, because then that's half of you and half of them running around, you're never going to separate yourself from that. So once you have a child with somebody, then the most important thing in the world to you is half that other person whether you like them or not.
turns.zh: 在某种程度上，是的，但还有……你说得太对了。有多少人是稀里糊涂地陷入一段关系，还没反应过来就同居了、养狗了、有孩子了、结婚了。然后有了孩子，因为那是你的一半和对方的一半在跑，你永远无法与之分离。所以一旦你和某人生了孩子，那么对你来说世界上最重要的事就是那个人的一半，无论你喜欢与否。

turns.en: Yeah. Jeffrey Miller had a tweet a long time ago that I always think about and he said every parenting book in the world could be replaced with one book on behavioral genetics that I am a big believer in genetics. Yes, I do think a lot of behavior is downstream of genetics and I think we underplay that. We like to overplay nurture and underplay nature for societal reasons but nature is a big deal. The temperament of the person you marry is probably going to be reflected in your child by default. People want securely attached kid, pick a securely attached partner. Well, the secret to a happy relationship is two happy people, right? So, I would say if you want to be happy, then be with a happy person. Don't think you're going to be with someone who's unhappy and then make them happy down the road. Or if you're okay with them being unhappy, but there are other things you like about them, that's fine. But this goes back to the unhappiness with other things. Yes. And actually we talked a little bit about how people do connect successfully, you know, on spirit and those things, but that's maybe a little too abstract. If you want to get a little more practical, it could be based on values. And values are a set of things you won't compromise on.
turns.zh: 是的。杰弗里·米勒很久以前发过一条推文，我经常想起。他说世界上所有的育儿书都可以被一本行为遗传学书取代。我非常相信遗传学。是的，我认为很多行为都源于遗传，而我们低估了这一点。出于社会原因，我们喜欢夸大教养、贬低天性，但天性很重要。你结婚对象的性情很可能默认会反映在你的孩子身上。想要一个安全型依恋的孩子，就选一个安全型依恋的伴侣。幸福关系的秘诀是两个幸福的人，对吧？所以，我会说，如果你想幸福，那就和一个幸福的人在一起。不要以为你能和一个不幸福的人在一起，然后以后让他们幸福。或者如果你能接受他们不幸福，但你喜欢他们的其他方面，那也行。但这又回到了对其他事情的不满足。是的。实际上我们之前聊过人们如何在精神层面成功连接，但那可能有点抽象。如果想更实际一点，可以基于价值观。价值观就是一套你绝不会妥协的东西。

turns.en: Values are the tough decisions of, oh, my parent got sick. Do they move in with us or do we put them in a nursing home? Uh, you know, the ch do we give the children money or do we not? uh you know do we um do we move across the country to be closer to our family or do we stay put where we are uh you know do we argue about politics do we care or do we not right the values are way more important than checklist items uh and uh I think if people were to align much more on their values they would have much more successful relationships
turns.zh: 价值观就是那些艰难的决定：哦，我父母生病了。他们是搬来和我们住，还是我们送他们去养老院？呃，你知道，我们是给孩子钱还是不给孩子钱？呃，你知道，我们是搬到全国各地去离家人更近，还是待在原地？呃，你知道，我们是争论政治，还是在乎或不在乎？对吧，价值观比清单上的项目重要得多。呃，我认为如果人们在价值观上更加一致，他们的关系会更成功。

transcript.sec: Loss Aversion and Fear of Change

turns.en: The emotional pain of fearing change. I have this thing: the job, the location, the partner I'm going to enter or not enter this thing. For the most part, it's leaving. I think we have this sort of loss aversion that we really feel evolved loss aversion. It's just painful separating yourself in front of your friends. It's embarrassing. And how would you advise people to get past themselves with the loss aversion? That fear of change. Oh my god, am I going to...
turns.zh: 害怕改变的情感痛苦。我有这个事：工作、地点、伴侣，我要进入或不进入这个事。大部分时候，是离开。我认为我们有这种损失厌恶，我们真的感受到进化来的损失厌恶。在朋友面前离开自己是很痛苦的。很尴尬。你会如何建议人们克服自己的损失厌恶？那种对改变的恐惧。哦，天哪，我是不是要……

turns.en: Yeah, it's the hardest thing in the world. Uh starting over. It's back to the zero to one thing. It's uh it's the mountain climbing thing. You're not going to find your path to the top of the mountain in the first go around. Sometimes you go up there, you get stuck, and you come back down. And the difference between all the successful people and the ones who are not is the ones who are successful want it so badly they're willing to go back and start over again and again whether in their career or in their relationships or in anything else.
turns.zh: 是的，这是世界上最难的事。呃，重新开始。又回到了从零到一的事情。呃，这是爬山的事。你不可能第一次就找到通往山顶的路。有时你爬上去，卡住了，然后你下来。所有成功的人和不成功的人之间的区别是，成功的人非常渴望，他们愿意一次又一次地回去重新开始，无论是在事业、人际关系还是其他任何事情上。

turns.en: The more seriously you take yourself, the unhappier you're going to be. You learned how to take yourself less seriously. Well, fame doesn't help on that one because that is one of the traps of fame. People are always talking about you. They have a certain view of you and you start believing that and then you take yourself seriously and then that limits your own actions. You can't look like a fool anymore. Um you can't do new things anymore. You know, tomorrow I announce I'm a break dancer, right? That's going to be met with a lot of scorn and ridicule. But what if I want to be back? I'd back you if you want to make that. Yeah. The truth is if I want to be a break dancer, I'd be break dancing. But uh you know like I'm starting a new company from zero to one again from scratch. Let's do it you know one more time. uh and not just going and raising a big VC fund or retiring or what have you. But that's because I want to build the product. I want to see it exist. So I think that you constantly just have to force yourself. You have to remind yourself. Um look, deep down you're still the same Chris you were when you were 9 years old. Deep down you're still a kid. Uh you know, you're still curious about the world. You still have a lot of the same predilictions and desires and wants. You've got a nice veneer on it. But one of the nice things when you have kids is you realize how much closer they are to you in personality and knowledge and knowhow. Like I look at my son who's uh you know he's eight and uh I just noticed like wow he's probably has 60 to 80% of my knowledge and development wisdom and he has a lot more freedom and he has a lot more spontaneity. In some ways he's smarter and there's not a big gap here left to close. This kid's going to be, you know, done very soon. It caught up to me. And so to the extent that I think I know better or that I'm somewhere or that I'm someone, it's just an illusion. It's just a belief. What's the lineage between that and taking yourself too seriously? I shouldn't take myself too seriously because there's nothing here to take that seriously. And if I take myself too seriously, then I'm going to get trapped. I'm going to circumscribe myself again into a limited set of behaviors and outcomes that keep me from being free, keep me from being spontaneous, keep me from being happy. Um, so it just goes back to the, you know, don't think about yourself too much.
turns.zh: 你越把自己当回事，你就会越不快乐。你学会了不要把自己太当回事。嗯，名气对此没有帮助，因为那是名气的陷阱之一。人们总是谈论你。他们对你有某种看法，你开始相信它，然后你把自己当回事，这限制了你的行动。你不能再看起来像个傻瓜了。嗯，你不能再做新的事情了。你知道，明天我宣布我是一个霹雳舞者，对吧？那会遭到很多嘲笑和讽刺。但如果我想回去呢？如果你想那样做，我会支持你。是的。事实是，如果我想成为一个霹雳舞者，我会去跳霹雳舞。但是呃，你知道，就像我正在从零到一重新开始一家新公司。让我们再做一次，你知道。而不是去筹集一大笔风投资金或退休或什么的。但那是因为我想构建产品。我想看到它存在。所以我认为你必须不断强迫自己。你必须提醒自己。嗯，看，内心深处你还是那个9岁的克里斯。内心深处你还是个孩子。呃，你知道，你仍然对世界充满好奇。你仍然有很多相同的倾向、欲望和需求。你给它涂上了一层漂亮的外衣。但有孩子的一个好处是，你会意识到他们在个性、知识和技能上与你有多接近。就像我看着我的儿子，呃，你知道他八岁了，我注意到，哇，他可能拥有我60%到80%的知识和发展智慧，而且他拥有更多的自由和更多的自发性。在某些方面他更聪明，而且差距不大。这个孩子很快就会，你知道，赶上我。所以，就我认为我更懂、或者我在某个位置、或者我是某个人而言，这只是一个幻觉。这只是一个信念。这和把自己太当回事之间有什么联系？我不应该把自己太当回事，因为这里没有什么值得那么当回事的。如果我把自己太当回事，那么我会被困住。我会再次把自己限制在一套有限的行为和结果中，这让我无法自由，无法自发，无法快乐。嗯，所以这又回到了，你知道，不要想自己太多。

turns.en: There's a special type of pain in realizing that the advice that you need to hear right now is something that almost always you learned a long time ago and that you know you're basically sort of the same person you were as you were nine. You know a lot of the time people ask questions like um what advice do you wish that you would give yourself 10 years ago? People ask themselves that question almost invariably the advice that you would give yourself 10 years ago is still the advice that you need to hear today. Absolutely. That's why I did that exercise of thinking back, you know, 10 years, 20 years, 30 years ago. What advice would I give myself? For me, it's just be less emotional. Don't take don't take everything so seriously. Do the same things, but do them without all the emotional turbulence. And so, that's the advice I'm giving myself going forward. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. It's funny how we need that distance to be able to be a little bit more objective, to have a little bit more perspective. And it's almost a little bit of a trick, right? Because typically when you do that you say what would you tell a friend that was going through this right and then you try and turn the advice to the friend around onto yourself but you always think well I'm not the friend you go okay you 10 years ago there's enough distance in that you go oh I actually am still that person there's just a single line between that
turns.zh: 有一种特殊的痛苦，意识到你现在需要听到的建议几乎总是你很久以前就学到的，而且你知道你基本上还是九岁时的同一个人。你知道很多时候人们会问这样的问题：嗯，你希望10年前的自己得到什么建议？人们问自己这个问题，几乎无一例外，你给10年前的自己的建议仍然是你今天需要听到的。绝对是的。这就是为什么我做那个回顾的练习，你知道，10年、20年、30年前。我会给自己什么建议？对我来说，就是少一点情绪化。不要把每件事都看得那么重。做同样的事情，但不要带着所有的情绪波动去做。所以，这就是我未来给自己的建议。是的。是的。是的。是的。有趣的是，我们需要那种距离才能更客观一点，更有视角一点。这几乎有点小技巧，对吧？因为通常当你这样做时，你会说你会告诉一个正在经历这个的朋友什么，然后你试图把给朋友的建议转给自己，但你总是想，嗯，我不是那个朋友。然后你说，好吧，10年前的你，有足够的距离，你会说，哦，我实际上还是那个人，只有一线之隔。

turns.en: And related to this story is I think understanding is way more important than discipline. Now Jaco would have a fit but you know on physical things discipline is important if I want to build a good body, I got to work out on a regular basis. But on mental things, I think understanding is way more important. Once you see the truth of something, you cannot unsee it. All of us have had experiences where we've seen a behavior in a person and then it just changes what we think about that person. We no longer want to be friends with them or we deeply respect them if it was, you know, really good behavior that maybe was observed unintentionally. Um so when we really do see something clearly it changes our behavior immediately and that is far more efficient than trying to change your behavior through repetition. Could you give me an example?
turns.zh: 与此相关的是，我认为理解比纪律重要得多。现在雅各可能会不高兴，但你知道，在身体方面，纪律很重要，如果我想练出好身材，我必须定期锻炼。但在精神方面，我认为理解重要得多。一旦你看到了某件事的真相，你就无法忽视它。我们都有过这样的经历：我们看到一个人的行为，然后它改变了我们对那个人的看法。我们不再想和他们做朋友，或者我们深深地尊重他们，如果那是非常好的行为，也许是无意中观察到的。嗯，所以当我们真正清楚地看到某件事时，它会立即改变我们的行为，这比试图通过重复来改变你的行为有效得多。你能给我举个例子吗？

turns.en: Um if you were let's say that you know you have a friend and then that person turns out to be a thief. You see that person stealing something you're done with them.
turns.zh: 嗯，比如说，你知道你有一个朋友，然后那个人原来是个小偷。你看到那个人偷东西，你就和他们断绝关系了。

turns.en: If you are, you know, the smoking lung cancer example is a good one. Right? Someone close to you, or anytime someone close to you dies, or you even hear about someone dying, what's the first thing you do? The first thing, assuming that you weren't that close to them—obviously if you're close to them it's different—but if you weren't that close to them but you hear about someone in your extended social circle dying, you immediately start trying to distinguish yourself from them. You're like, 'Oh well, how old was this person? Did they have, were they a smoker? Did they have an issue? Do I have that issue?' Right? You immediately start comparing. What you're doing there is you're sort of just trying to see if there's an overlap. But if you see the truth in something, if you're like, 'Oh my god, this person was the same age as me and they died.' And that's starting to happen at my age where I'm starting to hear about extended circle people. It just reminds you time is really short. There's a truth there. There's a truth there that you cannot action. There's a truth there that you cannot unsee. Or for example, I think were you into bodybuilding or something back when? I don't know. Like bro lifting stuff?
turns.zh: 比如，吸烟导致肺癌的例子就很好。对吧？你亲近的人，或者任何时候你亲近的人去世了，甚至你只是听说有人去世了，你第一件事是什么？第一件事，假设你和他们不那么亲近——显然如果很亲近就不一样了——但如果你不那么亲近，只是听说你社交圈里有人去世了，你会立刻开始试图把自己和他们区分开。你会想：‘哦，这个人多大年纪？他们抽烟吗？他们有什么问题吗？我有那个问题吗？’对吧？你立刻开始比较。你实际上是在试图看看有没有重叠。但如果你看到了其中的真相，如果你觉得：‘天哪，这个人跟我同龄，他们去世了。’而这种事在我这个年纪开始发生了，我开始听说社交圈里有人去世。这提醒你时间真的很短暂。那里有一个真相。一个你无法行动的真相。一个你无法忽视的真相。或者，比如，你以前是不是练过健美什么的？我不知道。就像兄弟举铁？
