turns.en: Yeah, I was a skinny guy lifting weights.
turns.zh: 是的，我以前是个瘦子，举重健身。

turns.en: Right. But there probably was a point where you were being really aggro in the gym and you injured yourself many times. Right? And each one of those was a deep understanding of 'don't go beyond this point,' right? There was a truth there. So again, when you see these things in such a way that you can't unsee them, that changes your behavior instantly. And I would argue that that introspection to find those truths is actually very useful. Is that a justification for more experimentation, exploration, experience in life, sort of trying to find serendipity because all of these experiences are going to teach you an inescapable lesson?
turns.zh: 对。但可能有个时候你在健身房特别激进，伤了自己很多次。对吧？每一次都让你深刻理解‘不要超过这个限度’，对吧？那里有一个真相。所以，当你以无法忽视的方式看到这些事情时，它会立刻改变你的行为。我认为，这种寻找真相的内省实际上非常有用。这是否证明了生活中需要更多的实验、探索和体验，试图寻找意外发现，因为所有这些经历都会给你一个无法逃避的教训？

turns.en: You're going to do what you're going to do. I mean, your level of exploration, I think, is sort of up to you. But life is always throwing truth back at you. It's about whether you choose to see it, whether you choose to acknowledge it, even if it's painful. Truth is often painful, right? If it wasn't, we'd all be seeing truth all the time. Reality is always reflecting truth. That's all it is. Why would you not have accessed it already?
turns.zh: 你会做你该做的事。我是说，你的探索程度，我觉得，取决于你自己。但生活总是把真相抛回给你。关键在于你是否选择去看，是否选择去承认，即使它很痛苦。真相往往是痛苦的，对吧？如果不是，我们就会一直看到真相。现实总是在反映真相。仅此而已。为什么你还没有接触到它呢？

turns.en: Exactly. You know, all the philosophy that's out there, for example, it's almost trite. Most people look at philosophy like that until they discover it for themselves. Because wisdom is the set of things that cannot be transmitted. If they could be transmitted, we'd read the same five philosophy books and we'd all be done. We'd all be wise. You have to learn it for yourself. It has to be rediscovered for yourself in your own context. You have to have specific experiences that then allow you to generalize and see the truth in those things in such a way that you're not going to unsee them. But each person is going to see them in a different way. I can tell you that Socrates story and it's not going to resonate until there's something that other people desire that you realize you yourself don't want. And the moment that happens, then you'll see the truth in the general statement.
turns.zh: 没错。比如，所有那些哲学，几乎都成了陈词滥调。大多数人看待哲学就是这样，直到他们自己发现它。因为智慧是一套无法传递的东西。如果能传递，我们读同样的五本哲学书就都完事了。我们都会变得智慧。你必须自己去学。它必须在你自己的语境中重新发现。你必须拥有特定的经历，然后才能概括并看到那些事物中的真相，以你无法忽视的方式。但每个人都会以不同的方式看到它们。我可以给你讲苏格拉底的故事，但直到你遇到别人渴望的东西而你自己却不想要时，它才会产生共鸣。那一刻，你就会看到那个普遍陈述中的真理。

turns.en: I want to just read you a two-minute essay that I wrote a couple of weeks ago. So, it's called 'Unteachable Lessons.' Okay. I've been thinking about a special category of lesson, one which you cannot discover without experiencing it firsthand. There is a certain subset of advice that for some reason we all refuse to learn through instruction. These are unteachable lessons. No matter how arduous or costly or effortful it is going to be for us to find out ourselves, we prefer to disregard the mountains of warnings from our elders, songs, literature, historical catastrophes, public scandals, and instead think some version of, 'Yeah, that might be true for them, but not for me.' We decide to learn the hard lessons the hard way over and over again. Unfortunately, they all seem to be the big things, too. It's never new insights about how to put up level shelves or charmingly introduce yourself at a cocktail party. Instead, we spend most of our lives learning firsthand the most important lessons that the previous generations already warned us about. Things like money won't make you happy. Fame won't fix your self-worth. You don't love that pretty girl. She's just hot and difficult to get. Nothing is as important as you think it is when you're thinking about it. You will regret working too much. Worrying is not improving your performance. All your fears are a waste of time. You should see your parents more. You'll be fine after the breakup and will be grateful that you did it. It's perfectly okay to cut toxic people out of your life. And even reading this list back, I'm rolling my eyes at how trite it is. These are all basic obvious insights that everybody has heard before. But if they're so basic, why does everyone so reliably fall prey to them throughout our lives? And if they're so obvious, why do people who have recently become famous or wealthy or lost a parent or gone through a breakup start to proclaim these facts with the renewed grand ceremony of someone who's just gone through religious revelation? It's also a very contentious list of points to say on the internet. If you interview a billionaire who says that all of his money didn't make him happy, or a movie star who said that her fame felt like a prison, the internet will tear them apart for being ungrateful and out of touch. So, not only do we refuse to learn these lessons, we even refuse to hear the message from those warning us about them. And even more than that, I think for every one of these, if I consider a bit deeper, I can recall a time, including right now, where I convinced myself that I am the exception to the rule. That my particular mental makeup or life situation or historical wounds or dreams for the future render me immune to these lessons being applicable. No, no, no. My inner landscape would be solved by skirting around the most well-known wisdom of the ages. No, no, no. I can thread this needle properly. Watch me dance through the minefield and avoid all of the trip wires that everyone else kicks. And then you kick one. And you share a knowing look, the kind that can only occur between two people who have been hurt in the exact same way. And a voice in the back of your mind will say, 'I told you so.' That's unteachable.
turns.zh: 我想给你读一篇我几周前写的两分钟短文。它叫‘无法传授的教训’。好的。我一直在思考一种特殊的教训，一种不亲身经历就无法发现的教训。有一类建议，出于某种原因，我们都拒绝通过教导来学习。这些是无法传授的教训。无论自己去发现它有多么艰难、昂贵或费力，我们宁愿忽视来自长辈、歌曲、文学、历史灾难、公众丑闻的如山警告，而是想：‘是的，那可能对他们是真的，但对我不是。’我们决定一次又一次地用艰难的方式学习艰难的教训。不幸的是，它们似乎也都是大事。从来不是关于如何安装水平架子或在鸡尾酒会上迷人地自我介绍的新见解。相反，我们花费一生的大部分时间亲身学习前人已经警告过我们的最重要教训。比如：金钱不会让你快乐。名声不会修复你的自我价值。你不爱那个漂亮女孩。她只是性感且难以得到。没有什么事情像你想象中那么重要。你会后悔工作太多。担忧不会提高你的表现。你所有的恐惧都是浪费时间。你应该多看看你的父母。分手后你会没事的，并且会庆幸你做了。把有毒的人从你的生活中剔除完全没问题。即使重读这个列表，我也对它的陈词滥调翻白眼。这些都是每个人以前都听过的基本明显见解。但如果它们如此基本，为什么每个人在一生中都如此可靠地陷入其中？如果它们如此明显，为什么那些最近成名、富有、失去父母或经历分手的人，会以刚刚经历宗教启示的人那种重新焕发的隆重仪式来宣告这些事实？这也是一个在互联网上非常有争议的列表。如果你采访一个亿万富翁，他说他所有的钱没有让他快乐，或者一个电影明星，她说她的名声感觉像监狱，互联网会因为他们忘恩负义和脱离现实而把他们撕碎。所以，我们不仅拒绝学习这些教训，甚至拒绝听到那些警告我们的人的信息。甚至更多，我认为对于每一个教训，如果我深入思考，我可以回忆起一个时刻，包括现在，我让自己相信我是规则的例外。我特定的心理构成、生活状况、历史创伤或对未来的梦想使我免疫于这些教训的适用。不，不，不。我的内心世界可以通过绕过时代最广为人知的智慧来解决。不，不，不。我可以正确地穿针引线。看我跳舞穿过雷区，避开所有其他人踢到的绊线。然后你踢到了一个。你分享一个会意的眼神，那种只能发生在两个以完全相同方式受伤的人之间的眼神。你脑海深处会有一个声音说：‘我告诉过你。’这就是无法传授的。

turns.en: It's a good essay. I think one of the reasons why these lessons are unteachable is because they're too broad. They have to be applied in context. A number of the ones that you laid out contradict each other.
turns.zh: 这是一篇好文章。我认为这些教训无法传授的原因之一是它们太宽泛了。它们必须在上下文中应用。你列出的许多教训相互矛盾。

turns.en: Like spend more time with your parents and don't work so hard, but at the same time you do want to be successful. I think a lot of these lessons come from on high, like a famous movie star or billionaire saying you don't need to be happy. It's like, okay, then give it up. In reality, many of these contradict each other. If you studied philosophy for four years, you wouldn't know how to live life because you wouldn't know which doctrine to apply. You have to actually live life and go through issues to figure out what you want. Context matters: yes, visit your parents more, but not live with them or see them every day if you don't get along. It's highly contextual.
turns.zh: 比如多陪陪父母，别那么拼命工作，但同时又想成功。我觉得很多道理都来自高高在上的人，比如著名影星或亿万富翁说‘你不需要快乐’。那好，你放弃试试？实际上，很多道理是相互矛盾的。如果你只学四年哲学，你根本不知道怎么生活，因为你不知道在什么情况下该用哪个理论。你得真正去生活，经历各种问题，才能弄清楚自己想要什么。语境很重要：是的，多去看父母，但别和他们住一起，也别每天或每周末都见，如果合不来的话。这高度依赖具体情况。

turns.en: Once you figure it out for yourself, you can carve variations on these maxims that apply to you, and you'll have a specific experience that helps you remember and execute. You can phrase it in a way that's not trite anymore. Many of my maxims and notes to self are modernized. They say something true that might be trite if not said in a new way relevant to me today. A Nobel Prize winner said something like: everything worth saying may have been said before, but since nobody was listening, it must be said again. It has to be recontextualized for the modern age. Things change: technology, culture, people.
turns.zh: 一旦你自己想明白了，就能在这些格言上刻出适合你的变体，而且你会有一段具体的经历帮你记住并执行。你可以用不陈腐的方式表达。我的很多格言和给自己的笔记都是现代化的。它们表达的是真理，但如果不以今天对我有意义的新方式说出来，就会显得陈腐。有位诺贝尔奖得主说过：所有值得说的话可能以前都说过了，但因为没人听，所以必须再说一遍。必须为现代重新语境化。事物在变：技术、文化、人都在变。

turns.en: You've talked about the difference between seeming wise and being wise. You tried to appear smart as a kid by rote memorization masquerading as insight. I feel that a lot of this show for me has been a redemption arc from a decade where I completely suppressed intellectual curiosity. I was a professional party boy for 10 years, standing at nightclub doors giving VIP wristbands. It was fun, but coming back to academia, getting two degrees, and then this show helped me wipe off the slime of needing to prove myself. When you memorize things, it indicates you don't understand them. Rote memorization and regurgitation masquerading as wisdom. People use fluency as a proxy for truthfulness and insight, and the complexity of language.
turns.zh: 你谈到过‘显得明智’和‘真正明智’的区别。你小时候试图通过死记硬背来显得聪明，假装有洞见。我觉得这个节目对我来说，很大程度上是一个救赎弧线，弥补了我完全压制求知欲的那十年。我当了十年专业派对男孩，站在夜店门口发VIP手环。那很好玩，但回到学术圈，拿了两个学位，然后这个节目帮我洗掉了需要证明自己的污点。当你死记硬背时，说明你没理解。死记硬背和照本宣科假装有智慧。人们把流畅度当作真实和洞见的替代品，也看重语言的复杂性。

turns.en: It's the mark of a charlatan to explain a simple thing in a complex way. When people use very complicated language to explain simple things, they're either trying to impress you and obfuscate, or they don't understand it themselves.
turns.zh: 用复杂的方式解释简单的事情是江湖骗子的标志。当人们用非常复杂的语言解释简单的事情时，他们要么是想打动你并混淆视听，要么是他们自己也不懂。

turns.en: There's an allure in that. When I went to therapy, I had to turn off podcast Chris. I trained myself over 700 episodes to perform: create a nice story, put a bow on it, push it across the table, and watch your eyes light up. The therapist called me out: you're performing, you're doing the Chris Williamson thing with jazz hands. So I have my own version.
turns.zh: 这其中有诱惑力。我去做心理治疗时，必须关掉‘播客克里斯’。经过700多期节目，我训练自己表演：编个好故事，打个蝴蝶结，推到桌子对面，看着你眼睛发亮。治疗师戳穿了我：你在表演，你在做克里斯·威廉姆森那套，像爵士舞手势。所以我也有自己的版本。

turns.en: I have podcast guest Naval. Very often I'll think of an insight and want to tweet it, but in my mind I'm talking about it on a podcast. That's how my mind registers it. For a while I thought this was bad and tried to eradicate podcast Naval, but then I realized that's just how it comes out, so I might as well be okay with it.
turns.zh: 我有‘播客嘉宾纳瓦尔’。我经常想到一个洞见想发推，但在脑子里我是在播客上谈论它。我的大脑就是这样记录的。有一阵我觉得这不好，试图消灭‘播客纳瓦尔’，但后来我意识到这就是它出来的方式，所以不如坦然接受。

turns.en: Do you know the reason I'm on this podcast?
turns.zh: 你知道我为什么上这个播客吗？

turns.en: No.
turns.zh: 不知道。

turns.en: I haven't done a proper formal interview in a long time, since Rogan maybe. I reached out to you for this one. The reason is funny: when I'm playing podcast in the vault in my head, for some reason you're on the other side. I don't know why. I've only seen snippets of your podcasts, but you were the guy in the podcast in my head. So I thought I might as well just do it.
turns.zh: 我很久没做正式访谈了，可能从罗根之后就没有。我主动联系了你。原因很有趣：当我在脑子里播放播客时，不知为什么，对面坐的是你。我不知道为什么。我只看过你播客的一些片段，但你是我脑子里那个播客里的人。所以我想不如就做吧。

turns.en: I wonder if this will close that loop or further entrench it. Now it's reality plus a dream, and I can't get away from him. There are enough people I turned down saying I'm just not doing podcasts. I feel bad about that. I have nothing new to talk about, so I don't know what I'm going to say.
turns.zh: 我不知道这是会结束那个循环还是让它更根深蒂固。现在是现实加梦境，我摆脱不了他了。我拒绝过很多人，说我不做播客了。我对此感到抱歉。我没什么新东西可讲，所以不知道要说什么。

turns.en: Well, I appreciate what you said on Rogan, and this was something to kind of pay it back to you. I had a five-headed Mount Rushmore of guests before I started this show: Jordan Peterson, Sam Harris, Alain de Botton from the School of Life, you, and Rogan. That was my hydra of Mount Rushmore. I think someone had asked you at some point, maybe a tweet or something after Rogan, or maybe you even said it on Rogan, where you said, 'I don't like to say the same thing twice, at least not in the same way. I don't like sequels.' I really respected that. That was 2019. You said it was eight or nine years ago, but it wasn't that long ago. I have a terrible memory. You're right, 2019, right before COVID. I really appreciated that because in the content game, you can keep repeating yourself. I'm sure I'll have said many things today that the audience has already heard, but caring enough to have novel insights, or at least a new perspective on similar insights, to say, 'Well, in the six years since you were on Joe, a lot of these—I'm coming at them differently.' Actually, the first thing I said to you today was, 'I'm not convinced I fully agree with that thing I used to say,' which is cool. That shows that the position you put in the ground previously is not a tether. You're not held to it anymore.
turns.zh: 嗯，我很感激你在罗根节目上说的话，这也算是一种回报。在我开始这个节目之前，我有一个五头拉什莫尔山的嘉宾名单：乔丹·彼得森、萨姆·哈里斯、生命学院的阿兰·德波顿、你，还有罗根。那就是我的九头蛇拉什莫尔山。我记得有人曾经问过你，可能是罗根之后的一条推文，或者你在罗根节目上说过：‘我不喜欢把同一件事说两遍，至少不会用同样的方式。我不喜欢续集。’我真的很尊重这一点。那是2019年。你说那是八九年前，但其实没那么久。我记性很差。你说得对，2019年，就在新冠之前。我真的很感激，因为在内容创作领域，你可以不断重复自己。我敢肯定我今天说的很多话观众已经听过了，但足够关心去获得新颖的见解，或者至少对类似的见解有新的视角，然后说：‘嗯，在你上乔节目后的六年里，这些观点——我现在用不同的方式看待它们。’实际上，我今天对你说的第一句话就是：‘我不确定我是否完全同意我以前说的那个观点了。’这很酷。这表明你之前确立的立场不是一根绳子。你不再被它束缚了。

turns.en: Well, I think the reason I wanted to be on this show is because I have the impression that you engage in conversations, and I like conversations. I don't like interviews. That's why I was doing my last startup, Airchat, which was all about conversations. Conversations to me are more genuine, more authentic. There's a give and take, a back and forth, genuine curiosity. It's not to say other podcasters don't do it—they absolutely do—but for some reason, in my mind, I had you as a guy I would actually have a conversation with. And sure enough, you just read me your essay, which I don't think anyone else would really do. That implies a give and take, genuine curiosity. I think that's useful because then certain inexplicit knowledge I had will be surfaced for myself. That's helpful.
turns.zh: 嗯，我想我之所以想上这个节目，是因为我觉得你是在进行对话，而我喜欢对话。我不喜欢采访。这就是为什么我做了我上一个创业项目 Airchat，它完全是关于对话的。对我来说，对话更真实、更真诚。有来有往，有交流，有真正的好奇心。这并不是说其他播客主持人不这样做——他们当然做——但不知为何，在我心里，我把你视为一个我会真正与之对话的人。果然，你刚才读了我的文章，我觉得没有其他人会这么做。这暗示着有来有往，有真正的好奇心。我认为这很有用，因为这样我内心某些隐性的知识就会浮现出来。这很有帮助。

turns.en: You're seeing, to break the fourth wall a bit, some of the gateway drug insights you had that you just don't get to choose. I'm aware you have an anti-guru sentiment, a very strong 'don't listen to me, I don't know what I'm doing.' Guru is a trap. Do not follow me, do not bow to me, do not do any of those things. But if you see resonance in another person—and I think that's what we're all trying to find. People can complain about the mountains of content creation, maybe rightly so. But if you can find someone and see a bit of yourself in them, maybe not much, but like, 'Oh, that bit of them—their self-esteem, how they look at relationships, what they want to do, the kind of life they want, the level of peace of mind they want'—if you find a little bit of that in someone else, it's like what you said before: you can no longer be unconvinced of that. It steps in and becomes a part of you. You're maybe seeing reflected back to you this percolated, meandering insight from however long ago, and something's happened. Maybe in five years you'll be like, 'You know that thing you said about the lessons?' It's cool—that's synthesis, a blending.
turns.zh: 你在某种程度上打破了第四面墙，看到了你曾经有过的那些入门级洞见，而你是无法选择这些的。我知道你有一种反导师的情绪，一种非常强烈的‘别听我的，我不知道我在做什么’。导师是个陷阱。不要追随我，不要向我鞠躬，不要做任何那些事。但是，如果你在另一个人身上看到共鸣——我认为这就是我们都在试图寻找的东西。人们可以抱怨内容创作的山一样多，也许有道理。但如果你能找到一个人，在他们身上看到一点你自己，也许不多，但就像‘哦，他们身上的那一点——他们的自尊，他们看待关系的方式，他们想做什么，他们想要的生活，他们想要的内心平静程度’——如果你在别人身上找到了一点点，就像你之前说的：你不能再不相信那一点了。它介入并成为你的一部分。你也许看到了反射回来的、从很久以前渗透出来的、蜿蜒的洞见，然后发生了一些事情。也许五年后你会说：‘你知道你关于教训说的那些话吗？’这很酷——这就是综合，一种融合。

turns.en: The reason I spend a lot of time in San Francisco is because it's a gravitational attractor for the smartest people in the world. Despite all the problems the city has—it's mismanaged beyond belief—it does seem to pull in young, smart, creative people. Not just those building technology, but those exploring every facet of life. They're weird, sometimes repulsive and bizarre, but you talk to them and you see a very intelligent person coming at life in a completely different way, putting it through the combinatorics of human DNA, which are uncountable, and giving you a weird perspective that can twist your mind around. To do that, you always have to be learning. You can't be in a guru mentality. If I'm with someone and they're listening to every word I say and hanging on it, that's not interesting for me. I'm not going to learn anything. I want people who are intelligent, who will say something back that's a little different. I may not agree with it, but it will leave a mark, an impression. It will leave an impression to the extent that both they are correct and I choose to listen. I'll choose to listen if I don't view myself as higher status or smarter than them. The flip side is I'm not really impressed by high-status people. In fact, most of my friends who have gone on to become very famous or successful—the more famous and successful they've been, the less I spend time with them. Partially because they get surrounded by an army of sycophantic fans, which is hard to break through, and because I don't want anything from them. I don't like situations where transactional relationships are implied. That can be tough for people who are up there because the higher they climb, the fewer people don't want anything from them. So in that way, you have an even better friend, right? But they get surrounded by people who do want things and are so good at pretending they don't that it's just not worth my time to try to break out from that group. So it does get lonely at the top, so to speak. But it's also by choice. You can be your own best friend. I am my own best friend, actually. I really enjoy spending time with myself.
turns.zh: 我花很多时间在旧金山的原因，是因为它是世界上最聪明的人的引力吸引中心。尽管这座城市有很多问题——管理混乱得令人难以置信——但它似乎确实吸引了年轻、聪明、有创造力的人。不仅仅是那些建造技术的人，还有那些探索生活方方面面的人。他们很奇怪，有时令人反感、怪异，但你和他们交谈，你会看到一个非常聪明的人以完全不同的方式对待生活，把它通过人类DNA的组合（这是数不清的）进行加工，给你一个可以扭曲你思维的奇怪视角。要做到这一点，你必须不断学习。你不能有导师心态。如果我和某人在一起，他们听我说的每一个字并紧紧抓住不放，那对我来说并不有趣。我什么也学不到。我想要聪明的人，他们会说一些有点不同的话。我可能不同意，但它会留下印记，留下印象。它会在某种程度上留下印象，既因为他们正确，也因为我选择倾听。如果我不认为自己比他们地位更高或更聪明，我就会选择倾听。另一面是，我并不真正被高地位的人打动。事实上，我大多数后来变得非常出名或成功的朋友——他们越出名、越成功，我和他们在一起的时间就越少。部分原因是他们被一群谄媚的粉丝包围，很难突破，而且我不想要他们任何东西。我不喜欢暗示交易关系的情况。这对那些处于高位的人来说可能很难，因为他们爬得越高，不想要他们东西的人就越少。所以从这个意义上说，你有一个更好的朋友，对吧？但他们被那些想要东西并且非常善于假装不想要的人包围，以至于我不值得花时间去试图从那个群体中突围。所以可以说，高处确实不胜寒。但这也是选择。你可以成为自己最好的朋友。实际上，我就是自己最好的朋友。我真的很享受和自己相处。

turns.en: The smartest people aren't interested in appearing smart and don't care what you think. A lot of life is not giving a f***. A lot of the best things in life come out of that. Does this mean talking about that rote memorization masquerading as wisdom and insight thing, which I think almost certainly podcasts like this have contributed to? You hear an Alain de Botton who's like a painter with words.
turns.zh: 最聪明的人对显得聪明不感兴趣，也不在乎你怎么想。生活中很多事情就是不在乎。生活中很多最好的东西都源于此。这是不是说那种死记硬背伪装成智慧和洞见的东西——我认为几乎可以肯定像这样的播客对此有所贡献？你听到一个阿兰·德波顿，他就像一个用文字作画的画家。

turns.en: Very simple, very unpretentious, but if you're intellectually curious, you only see the production of his thoughts. You don't necessarily see the work behind them. So you confuse the presentation for the insight. Does that make sense?
turns.zh: 非常简单，非常朴实，但如果你有求知欲，你只看到他思想的产物，不一定看到背后的努力。所以你会把呈现方式误认为洞察力。有道理吗？

turns.en: Of course. A lot of my stuff is more polished. Right before this podcast, I thought maybe I should go back and read my old tweets to remember what I said and articulate it well. But then I realized that's just performance. I would just be memorizing my stuff to perform.
turns.zh: 当然。我的很多内容都更精致。就在这个播客之前，我想也许我应该回去读读我以前的推文，记住我说过的话并好好表达。但后来我意识到那只是表演。我只会记住自己的东西来表演。

turns.en: That's an extra special level of hell you've descended into.
turns.zh: 那你已经堕入了一个特别的地狱层次。

turns.en: I wrote memorizing me to be more me. Bingo. And to live up to some expectation or famous personality that I now have to become some straight jacket I have to put on. I'm having to live up to in private the things I prefer.
turns.zh: 我写下来是为了让自己更像我。没错。为了达到某种期望或成为某个名人，我现在不得不穿上束缚衣。私下里我也得活出我喜欢的样子。

turns.en: That's right. So pretty quickly I saw through that. It's nonsense and it constrains my time and it's just work. I think that's your meditation practice at work, that mindfulness gap to be like, huh? Yeah, there's that thing again.
turns.zh: 没错。所以我很快就看穿了。那是胡说八道，还占用我的时间，只是工作。我觉得那是你的冥想练习在起作用，那个正念间隙让你想，嗯？是啊，又是那个东西。

turns.en: Exactly. So it's not about changing your thoughts or fixing yourself. It's just about being observant of yourself so that it'll automatically change. Whatever change needs to happen will happen. Trying to change yourself is very circular. The mind trying to change the mind, the mind doesn't like wrestling with itself. I don't think it gets you anywhere.
turns.zh: 正是。所以不是要改变你的想法或修正你自己。只是观察自己，这样就会自动改变。任何需要发生的改变都会发生。试图改变自己是非常循环的。大脑试图改变大脑，大脑不喜欢与自己搏斗。我认为这不会让你有任何进展。

turns.en: You've spent a lot of time either creating wealth or thinking about how to create wealth. What have you learned are the best places to spend wealth?
turns.zh: 你花了很多时间创造财富或思考如何创造财富。你学到的最好的花钱方式是什么？

turns.en: I actually think Elon had this one figured out, which is he plowed his own money back into his own businesses to do bigger and better things for humanity. You could give it to nonprofits, but a lot of nonprofits are grifty or it's people who didn't earn it trying to spend it, or they don't have tight feedback loops on having a good effect. One of the things I want to do as an aside is create a little school for young physicists. That's my nonprofit. But I've underwritten media and some physics stuff. I don't like to talk about it because I think that makes it less real, more status oriented, less philanthropic. Then people look at how charitable my charity is, and people come hunting for money. There's all that disease. I don't believe in giving to schools. They have enough money. Ivy Leagues have enough money and they don't know how to spend it. So I think the best use of money is a good business that creates a product people voluntarily buy and get value out of. Steve Jobs and Elon and entrepreneurs like that have created a lot of value for the world. So I can take my own money and invest it in myself to build the next great thing that I think needs to exist. That's basically what I'm doing right now. I'm doing a new business, self-funding it, plowing a lot of money into it. I'm going to build something beautiful that I want to see exist.
turns.zh: 我实际上认为埃隆想通了这一点，他把自己的钱重新投入自己的企业，为人类做更大更好的事情。你可以捐给非营利组织，但很多非营利组织很腐败，或者是由没赚到钱的人试图花钱，或者他们没有良好的反馈循环来确保效果。顺便说一句，我想做的一件事是为年轻物理学家创办一所小学校。那是我的非营利项目。但我资助过媒体和一些物理学项目。我不喜欢谈论它，因为我觉得那会让它变得不那么真实，更注重地位，更不慈善。然后人们会看我的慈善有多慈善，还会有人来讨钱。所有这些弊端。我不相信给学校捐款。它们有足够的钱。常春藤盟校有足够的钱，而且不知道怎么花。所以我认为钱最好的用途是创办一个好企业，创造人们自愿购买并从中获得价值的产品。史蒂夫·乔布斯、埃隆和像他们这样的企业家为世界创造了大量价值。所以我可以把自己的钱投资到自己身上，去建造下一个我认为需要存在的伟大事物。这就是我现在正在做的。我在做一个新业务，自己出资，投入大量资金。我要建造一个我认为美丽、希望看到存在的东西。

turns.en: Have you spoken about this yet or is it still dark mode?
turns.zh: 你谈过这个吗，还是仍然保密？

turns.en: It's so early. Maybe I'll show it to you in a few months, hopefully 6 months. I'm excited about it and that's a good use of money.
turns.zh: 还太早了。也许几个月后我会给你看，希望是6个月。我很兴奋，这是钱的好用途。

turns.en: What about the worst places to spend wealth? What is the old line? If it flies, floats or fornicates.
turns.zh: 那最糟糕的花钱方式呢？老话怎么说？如果它会飞、会浮或会交配。

turns.en: Very nice way to change the final F. That's the way I heard it. Can't take credit for that. I'm pretty sure it's Fox, but yeah. I think it was Felix Dennis who had that quote. He said, 'If it flies, floats or fornicates, rent it.' I think the last one was a little wrong because he didn't have a family, didn't have kids. So he missed the big one. But there are lots of bad ways to spend money. I believe in investment, not consumption. You're born with a short housing position, you close that out, get yourself a nice house. Get some help to free up your time so you're not doing things others can do better. Treat people well, always overpay and expect the best. Pay them like they're the best and expect the best. But overall, a good use of money is to take risks and build things that others can't do. Align it with your own unique talent so you can keep delivering to the world. I'm not going to sit idle or retire. That's a waste of whatever time I have left. If I'm doing something I enjoy, then I'm already in perpetual retirement. Work is just a set of things you have to do that you don't want to do. So if you want to do it, it's not work. There are things I want to do that don't feel like work. I can put money behind them and instantiate them into reality. I don't want to say 'make the world a better place' because that's too trite, but more just create a product I'm proud of that wouldn't exist otherwise, that others will get tremendous value from. And it's enabled through wealth because you're able to take a level of risk you wouldn't have otherwise.
turns.zh: 把最后一个F改得很妙。我听到的就是这样。不能归功于我。我确定是福克斯，但没错。我觉得是费利克斯·丹尼斯说的那句话。他说：‘如果它会飞、会浮或会交配，就租。’我认为最后一个有点不对，因为他没有家庭，没有孩子。所以他错过了最重要的。但有很多糟糕的花钱方式。我相信投资，而不是消费。你生来就做空房子，平仓，给自己买个好房子。请些帮手来解放你的时间，这样你就不用做别人能做得更好的事。善待他人，总是多付并期待最好。像对待最好的人一样付钱，并期待最好。但总的来说，钱的好用途是冒险，建造别人做不到的东西。与你独特的天赋保持一致，这样你可以继续为世界做贡献。我不会无所事事或退休。那是浪费我剩下的时间。如果我在做我喜欢的事，那我已经在永久退休中了。工作只是一堆你不得不做但不想做的事。所以如果你想做，那就不是工作。有些我想做的事感觉不像工作。我可以把钱投进去，把它们变成现实。我不想说‘让世界变得更美好’，因为那太老套了，而是创造一件我引以为豪的产品，否则它不会存在，其他人会从中获得巨大价值。而这是通过财富实现的，因为你能承担原本无法承担的风险。

turns.en: Wealth gives you freedom. It gives you freedom to explore more options. And in my case, it gives me freedom to start businesses without having to ask other people for permission or to warp my vision based on their desires to make a return or how they think money should be made.
turns.zh: 财富给你自由。它给你自由去探索更多选择。就我而言，它给了我自由去创业，而不必征求别人的许可，或者根据他们想要回报的愿望或他们认为赚钱的方式扭曲我的愿景。

turns.en: Is there anything that you'd add to the 'how to get rich' thread? Is there anything you thought like just one if I could go in and edit and add one more?
turns.zh: 你有什么想补充到‘如何致富’系列里的吗？有没有什么你觉得只要我能进去编辑再加一条的？

turns.en: No, there's like 10,000 things. I could talk about that topic forever to be honest.
turns.zh: 没有，大概有一万件事。老实说，我可以永远谈论那个话题。

turns.en: That thread was so short and limited, crafted in a sense, though I wrote it spontaneously. It left so much on the cutting room floor that I could talk about that topic for days. But it's all contextual. Business is very contextual. You have to look at the particular business, understand what's being done, why, and how, then you can tear it apart and reassemble it properly. I like to think that's where I have specific knowledge and expertise. My specific knowledge is not in happiness or philosophy. My life is hacked to be unique, but that's not my specific knowledge. My specific knowledge is in analyzing a business, especially a technology business, taking it apart at the seams, and predicting what is likely to work and what is not. Clubhouse notwithstanding. You're still going to be wrong most of the time. It's like playing the lottery, but you know one or two ticket numbers in advance. You only have to be right a few times, or even just once, to get the big score. Peter Thiel started PayPal, but he made all his money on Facebook. That was the big winner. That's true in any power law distribution. Number one returns more than two through n combined. Two returns more than three through n combined. You're operating in a highly leveraged intellectual domain, so outcomes are nonlinear. I know a lot about that topic, but it's highly contextual. It makes more sense with a specific business or entrepreneur in front of me. I can take it apart and say, 'This won't work because you're doing it for the wrong reasons. You're doing A to get to B. Just go to B.' Or 'You're doing this to make money, but the person who loves the product will beat you.' Or 'You're raising money from the wrong people, your co-founder isn't right, your vesting schedule is wrong, you're starting in the wrong place, or approaching from the wrong angle.' Of course, I'll be wrong too, but I've seen a lot of data and have theories. That's where I feel comfortable. The problem is when I have to talk about how to create wealth. 'How to get rich' is a clickbait title deliberately. But talking about wealth creation in the abstract is difficult because you have to speak truth that applies in almost every context. That limits what you can say. The lack of specificity makes it back to philosophy. But if I can get specific, that's where real knowledge can be like a counselor for people.
turns.zh: 那条推文很短，也很有限，虽然我是即兴写的，但某种程度上是精心雕琢的。它留下了太多未讲的内容，我可以就那个话题聊上几天。但一切都要看具体情境。商业是非常依赖具体情境的。你必须审视具体的业务，理解它在做什么、为什么做、怎么做，然后才能拆解它，再正确地重新组装。我认为那才是我拥有特定知识和专长的地方。我的特定知识不在于幸福或哲学。我的生活被精心设计得很独特，但那不是我的特定知识。我的特定知识在于分析一家企业，尤其是科技企业，从接缝处拆解它，并预测什么可能成功、什么可能失败。Clubhouse 是个例外。大多数时候你仍然会犯错。这就像买彩票，但你提前知道一两个号码。你只需要对几次，甚至一次，就能大获全胜。Peter Thiel 创办了 PayPal，但他的钱主要来自 Facebook。那才是大赢家。这在任何幂律分布中都成立。第一名回报超过第二名到第 n 名的总和。第二名回报超过第三名到第 n 名的总和。你是在一个高度杠杆化的智力领域运作，所以结果是非线性的。我对这个话题了解很多，但它高度依赖具体情境。如果面前有一个具体的业务或创业者，那就更有意义了。我可以拆解它，然后说：‘这不会成功，因为你做这件事的动机不对。你做 A 是为了达到 B，直接去做 B 吧。’或者‘你这样做是为了赚钱，但真正热爱产品的人会打败你。’或者‘你从错误的人那里融资，你的联合创始人不合适，你的股权兑现计划有问题，你在错误的地方创业，或者你从错误的角度切入。’当然，我也会犯错，但我见过大量数据，有自己的理论。那是我感到自在的领域。问题在于，当我必须谈论如何创造财富时。‘如何致富’是一个故意吸引眼球的标题。但抽象地谈论财富创造非常困难，因为你必须说出在几乎所有情境下都正确的真理。这限制了你所能说的。缺乏具体性让它又回到了哲学。但如果我能具体化，那才是真正的知识，可以像顾问一样帮助别人。

turns.en: Yeah. Correct. It's back to philosophy.
turns.zh: 是的，没错。又回到了哲学。

turns.en: Part of the reason I started doing podcasts—and this is ego at play, I'll admit it freely—when I was tweeting, I kind of pioneered philosophy Twitter, or a certain kind of practical philosophy Twitter. In 140 characters, I would try to say something true in an interesting way that was insightful to me at the time. But then that got copied. There are thousands of us now, thousands of people spitting it out, ChatGPT trying to create these things all day long. Although I like to think my stuff is incompressible; I'm saying it in the tightest way possible, which comes from a little failed poetry background. But what I realized was if you truly have a deep understanding of something, then you can talk about it all day long. You can rederive everything you need from that understanding. No memorization required. You can get to it from first principles, and every piece of what you know is like a Lego block that fits in and forms a steel frame. It's solid, locked in there. So on a podcast, I can unload much more deeply about some of these topics. For example, we can talk about any business you like, but it has to be in context, real, an actual problem. Then we can solve it. I just really love that heuristic: if you're having to memorize something, it's because you don't understand it. If you understand something, you don't have to memorize it.
turns.zh: 我开始做播客的部分原因——这是自我在作祟，我坦率承认——当我发推文时，我算是开创了哲学 Twitter，或者说某种实用哲学 Twitter。在 140 个字符里，我试图用一种有趣的方式说出一些真实且当时对我有启发的话。但后来那被复制了。现在有成千上万的人在做同样的事，ChatGPT 整天试图生成这些东西。虽然我倾向于认为我的内容是不可压缩的；我用最紧凑的方式表达，这源于一点失败的诗歌背景。但我意识到，如果你真正深刻理解某件事，那么你可以整天谈论它。你可以从那种理解中重新推导出你需要的一切。不需要记忆。你可以从第一性原理出发，你所知的每一块都像乐高积木一样拼合起来，形成钢架结构。它很坚固，牢牢锁定。所以在播客上，我可以更深入地展开这些话题。例如，我们可以谈论任何你喜欢的业务，但必须在具体情境中，必须是真实的、实际的问题。然后我们可以解决它。我真的很喜欢那个启发式原则：如果你不得不记忆某件事，那是因为你不理解它。如果你理解了，你就不需要记忆。

turns.en: That's right. If you have to memorize something, it's because you don't understand it. And if you understand something, you don't have to memorize it.
turns.zh: 没错。如果你不得不记忆某件事，那是因为你不理解它。如果你理解了，你就不需要记忆。

turns.en: Yeah. Again, just to sort of call out a lot of what I tried to do—this redemption arc thing—if I sound smart, that's like being smart, right? You go, well, ChatGPT has memorized the entire internet. Good luck competing with that. You're not going to beat it in memorization. You're not even going to beat a library at memorization. You're not going to beat any 10 books in memorization. So memorization is not the thing. The value of memorization is going down by the day. It's already so low. Understanding is a thing. Being able to—judgment is the thing. Taste is a thing. Understanding, judgment, taste—these come out of having real problems and then solving them and then finding the commonalities. What is philosophy? Everyone, if you live long enough, you'll be a philosopher. Philosophy is just when you find the hidden generalizable truths among the specific experiences you've had in life, and then you know how to navigate future specific experiences based on some heuristics, and you create a philosophy around that. Any subject pursued deeply enough will eventually lead to philosophy. Mastery in anything, literally anything, will lead you to being a philosopher. You just have to stick with it long enough and generalize the truths back out. And these are universal truths. It's back to the unity and variety. You can find unity in anything if you go deep enough. And that's why the trite stuff unfortunately sort of keeps coming back around. You're like, well, look, this is cliche for a reason. It's cliche for reasons. But sometimes you learn new things. Sometimes you do figure out new things too. Even in philosophy, for example, science has advanced, and as science has advanced, it's actually expanded our boundaries of philosophy. When we used to think the earth was the center of the universe, you would have a different philosophical outlook than when you think the universe is vast and we're infinitesimally small.
turns.zh: 是的。再次，为了指出我试图做的很多事——这种救赎弧线——如果我听起来很聪明，那就像聪明本身，对吧？你会说，ChatGPT 已经记住了整个互联网。祝你在记忆方面与它竞争。你在记忆方面甚至比不过一个图书馆。你比不过任何十本书。所以记忆不是关键。记忆的价值每天都在下降。它已经很低了。理解才是关键。能够——判断才是关键。品味才是关键。理解、判断、品味——这些来自于面对真实问题，解决它们，然后找到共性。什么是哲学？每个人，如果你活得够久，你就会成为哲学家。哲学就是你在生活中具体的经历中找到隐藏的可概括的真理，然后你知道如何基于一些启发式原则来应对未来的具体经历，并围绕它建立一种哲学。任何学科，如果深入钻研，最终都会通向哲学。对任何事情的精通，真的任何事情，都会让你成为哲学家。你只需要坚持足够久，然后把真理概括出来。而这些是普世真理。又回到了统一性与多样性。如果你深入足够，你可以在任何事物中找到统一性。这就是为什么那些陈词滥调不幸地不断出现。你会想，好吧，看，这是陈词滥调是有原因的。它有原因。但有时你也会学到新东西。有时你确实会发现新东西。即使在哲学中，例如，科学进步了，随着科学的进步，它实际上扩展了哲学的边界。当我们曾经认为地球是宇宙的中心时，你会有一个不同的哲学观，而不是当你认为宇宙浩瀚而我们微不足道时。

turns.en: It will give you a different philosophical outlook. The same way if you think that nature is driven by angels and demons and gods versus if there are laws of physics that are computable and understandable, that will lead you to a different philosophical outlook. If you think that knowledge is something that is passed down from above and through generations versus something that is created on the fly and then tested against reality, that will lead you to a different philosophical outlook. If you think humans are created by God as opposed to humans evolved from some unicellular organism, it still doesn't solve the original problem. Who created that? But at least it takes you further down the road. Even simulation theory is an attempt at reformulating philosophy based on what we know about computers, even though it kind of leads to a lot of the same conclusions as a creator. But it is at least philosophy that is informed by technology and by science.
turns.zh: 它会给你一种不同的哲学观。就像如果你认为自然是由天使、恶魔和神灵驱动，而不是存在可计算、可理解的物理定律，那会导向不同的哲学观。如果你认为知识是自上而下、代代相传的，而不是即时创造并经过现实检验的，那也会导向不同的哲学观。如果你认为人类是上帝创造的，而不是从某种单细胞生物进化而来的，这仍然没有解决最初的问题——谁创造了上帝？但至少它让你走得更远。甚至模拟理论也是一种基于我们对计算机的了解来重新构建哲学的尝试，尽管它得出了许多与造物主相似的结论，但它至少是一种受技术和科学启发的哲学。

turns.en: Philosophy can also evolve. Moral philosophy evolves, right? There was a time when every culture that was a conquering culture practiced slavery. Now almost all cultures abhor slavery. That's moral philosophy having evolved. There was even a fairly large group of doctors based on studies who believed until the 1980s that babies couldn't feel pain. So even to this day, I think circumcision is done without anesthesia because under the theory that very young children, babies, don't feel pain. That's ludicrous. There was a study that came out in the 80s that said no, they do feel pain. It's like, oh yeah, of course. So people can be stuck in bad philosophical traps for a long period of time. So even philosophy can make progress.
turns.zh: 哲学也可以进化。道德哲学在进化，对吧？曾几何时，几乎每个征服文化都实行奴隶制。现在几乎所有文化都憎恶奴隶制。这就是道德哲学的进化。甚至有一大群医生，基于研究，直到20世纪80年代还相信婴儿感觉不到疼痛。所以直到今天，我认为割礼仍然在没有麻醉的情况下进行，因为理论是婴幼儿感觉不到疼痛。这很荒谬。80年代有一项研究说，不，他们确实感到疼痛。然后人们才恍然大悟。所以人们可能会长期陷入糟糕的哲学陷阱。因此，即使是哲学也能取得进步。

turns.en: As an example, one of the realizations I had, thanks to David Deutsch and my friend James Pierce, and also thinking it through a little bit, is that there are these timeless old questions where the answers seem like paradoxes. So we stop thinking about them. An example is free will. Do you have free will? Or does anything matter? Is there a meaning to life? We get stuck in them because, for example, is there a meaning to life? Yes, life has a meaning because you're right here. You create your own meaning. This moment has all the meaning you could imagine. It's all the meaning there is. On the other hand, you're going to die. It all goes to zero. Heat death. The universe has no meaning. So which one is it? The reason it seems paradoxical is because you're asking the question of a human here and now at a certain scale and a certain time, and then you're answering it from the viewpoint of the universe over infinite time. So you pull the trick: you switch the level at which you're answering the question. Questions should be answered at the level at which they're asked. So if you ask the question, is there meaning? You, Chris, are asking that question. Yes. To Chris, there is meaning. There's meaning right here. This is the meaning. You can interpret any meaning you want onto it. Don't ask the question as Chris and then answer it as God or as the universe. That's the trick you're playing. That's why it seems paradoxical.
turns.zh: 举个例子，我意识到的一个问题，这要感谢大卫·多伊奇和我的朋友詹姆斯·皮尔斯，以及我自己的思考，就是有一些永恒的老问题，答案看起来像是悖论。所以我们停止思考它们。比如自由意志。你有自由意志吗？或者任何事情重要吗？生命有意义吗？我们被困在其中，因为，例如，生命有意义吗？是的，生命有意义，因为你就在这里。你创造自己的意义。这一刻有你所能想象的一切意义。它就是所有存在的意义。另一方面，你会死。一切归零。热寂。宇宙没有意义。那么到底是哪一个？它看起来矛盾的原因是你从一个特定尺度、特定时间的人类角度提问，然后从宇宙无限时间的视角回答。所以你耍了个花招：你切换了回答问题的层次。问题应该在被问的层次上回答。所以如果你问“有意义吗？”你，克里斯，在问这个问题。是的。对克里斯来说，有意义。意义就在这里。这就是意义。你可以赋予它任何你想要的意义。不要以克里斯的身份提问，然后以上帝或宇宙的身份回答。这就是你耍的花招。这就是为什么它看起来矛盾。

turns.en: The same way you can say, do I have free will? People debate free will all day long. The question is answered at the wrong frame. They ask the question: do I as an individual have free will? Hell yeah, I have free will. My mind-body system can't predict what I'm going to do next. The universe is infinitely complex. I'm making a choice in my mind and I'm doing something. There's my free will. So answer at the level at which you were asked. Of course I have free will because I feel like I have free will and I treat you like you have free will and you treat me like I have free will. We have free will. The problem then is you start trying to answer the question as if you're the universe. You're like, well on the universal scale, big bang, particle collisions, no one makes any choices. How could you be any different than what the universe wants you to be? It's all one block universe. So you don't have free will. Don't answer the question at the level at which it wasn't asked. So if God asked the question, is there free will? No, there is no free will. The universe asks the question, there is no free will. But if an individual asks the question right now, then yes, there is free will. So a lot of these paradoxes resolve themselves, philosophical paradoxes that people have been struggling with since the beginning of time, when you just realize you're answering them at a scale and time different than they were asked.
turns.zh: 同样，你可以说，我有自由意志吗？人们整天争论自由意志。问题是在错误的框架下回答的。他们问：作为个体，我有自由意志吗？当然有，我有自由意志。我的心身系统无法预测我下一步要做什么。宇宙是无限复杂的。我在脑海中做出选择，然后做某事。这就是我的自由意志。所以在你被问的层次上回答。我当然有自由意志，因为我感觉我有自由意志，我对待你就像你有自由意志一样，你对待我也像我有自由意志一样。我们有自由意志。问题在于你开始试图以宇宙的身份回答这个问题。你会说，在宇宙尺度上，大爆炸、粒子碰撞，没有人做任何选择。你怎么可能不同于宇宙希望你成为的样子？一切都是一个块宇宙。所以你没有自由意志。不要在你没有被问的层次上回答问题。所以如果上帝问这个问题，有自由意志吗？不，没有自由意志。宇宙问这个问题，没有自由意志。但如果一个个体现在问这个问题，那么是的，有自由意志。所以很多这样的悖论就自行解决了——那些人们从古至今一直在挣扎的哲学悖论——当你意识到你是在不同于问题被问的尺度和时间上回答它们时。

turns.en: Speaking of updating beliefs, is there anything that you've changed your mind around recently? Very recently.
turns.zh: 说到更新信念，你最近有什么改变想法的事情吗？非常近期的。

turns.en: I mean, all the time. But are you talking about philosophical existential things or technological things?
turns.zh: 我的意思是，一直在变。但你是说哲学存在层面的东西还是技术层面的？

turns.en: Yeah, philosophical existential things or anything that comes to mind. If there's anything that's front of mind where you go, ah, yeah, that's a pretty big OS update.
turns.zh: 是的，哲学存在层面的东西，或者任何你想到的。如果有什么是你脑海中浮现的，你会说，啊，对，那是一次相当大的系统更新。

turns.en: Yeah, I'm less laissez-faire than I used to be on a societal level. I think that culture and religion are good cooperating systems for humans. So if you want to operate in a high trust society, you need to have sets of rules that people need to follow and obey so they get along, even if they're one-size-fits-all and doesn't work for everybody.
turns.zh: 是的，在社会层面，我不像以前那样自由放任了。我认为文化和宗教是人类良好的合作系统。所以如果你想在一个高信任社会中运作，你需要有一套人们必须遵守和服从的规则，这样他们才能和睦相处，即使这些规则是通用的，并不适合每个人。

turns.en: Moved up a little bit from libertarian.
turns.zh: 从自由意志主义稍微移动了一点。

turns.en: Yeah. I think pure libertarians get outcompeted and die, right? They get overrun because it's every man for himself. They can't coordinate.
turns.zh: 是的。我认为纯粹的自由意志主义者会在竞争中被淘汰并消亡，对吧？他们会被淹没，因为那是人人为己。他们无法协调。

turns.en: They can't coordinate. Exactly.
turns.zh: 他们无法协调。正是如此。

turns.en: Right. So the coordination problems. Culture exists to solve fundamental coordination problems. Religion solves coordination problems. Ethnicity solves coordination problems historically. And when you break down those coordination systems too fast and don't replace them with anything else, you get societal breakdown. So you can have very malfunctioning societies. Go to Japan versus go to any Western city and you can see the difference between a culture that's working and a culture that's not. So I think that's a broader set of things that I've changed my mind on a fair bit. I used to be much more laissez-faire on that stuff, let's put it that way.
turns.zh: 对。所以是协调问题。文化存在是为了解决基本的协调问题。宗教解决协调问题。种族在历史上解决协调问题。当你过快打破这些协调系统而不代之以其他东西时，你就会得到社会崩溃。所以你可能会有非常功能失调的社会。去日本和去任何西方城市，你可以看到运作良好的文化和运作不佳的文化之间的区别。所以我认为这是一系列我改变了很多想法的事情。我过去在那方面要自由放任得多，这么说吧。

turns.en: Mhm. What else?
turns.zh: 嗯。还有什么？

turns.en: On child raising, I've gotten a lot looser. I'm still not completely laissez-faire, but I'm much more realized that kids are going to be kids and you kind of let them do their thing.
turns.zh: 在育儿方面，我变得宽松多了。我仍然不是完全自由放任，但我更意识到孩子就是孩子，你让他们做自己的事。

turns.en: The debate with them. Is it a Talib that has the ascending levels of like anarchism versus conservatism? Is that his insight? Like at the local level, I'm this. It seems like you've gone the other way.
turns.zh: 和他们辩论。是不是塔利布有那种上升的层次，比如无政府主义对保守主义？那是他的见解吗？比如在地方层面，我是这个。看起来你走了相反的方向。

transcript.sec: On political spectrum and changing views

turns.en: It's like at the child level I'm an anarchist and at the societal level I'm a conservative.
turns.zh: 这就像在孩子的层面我是无政府主义者，在社会层面我是保守派。

turns.en: No, he was quoting somebody else, some brothers, I forget which ones, but he was making the point eloquently as he often does. That at the family local level he's a communist. At the family level you're communist. At maybe the extended family level you're a socialist, at the local level you're kind of a democrat, and so on until at the federal level you're a libertarian, right? So you've done it the other way. You're being a libertarian with the kids and you're being a religious conservative at the societal level.
turns.zh: 不，他是在引用别人的话，某个兄弟，我忘了是谁，但他像往常一样雄辩地阐述了观点。在家庭本地层面他是共产主义者。在家庭层面你是共产主义者。在大家庭层面你是社会主义者，在本地层面你有点像民主党人，以此类推，直到联邦层面你是自由意志主义者，对吧？所以你反过来了。你对孩子是自由意志主义者，在社会层面是宗教保守派。

turns.en: No, that's a funny way of looking at it. I don't know if the scale is that simple. What else have I changed my mind on? I mean, I think modern AI is really cool. I think it's but I think these are natural language computers. They're starting to show evidence of reasoning at some levels but I don't think they do creativity.
turns.zh: 不，这是一种有趣的看法。我不知道这个尺度是否那么简单。我还改变了什么想法？我的意思是，我认为现代AI真的很酷。但我觉得这些是自然语言计算机。它们开始在某些层面显示出推理的证据，但我不认为它们有创造力。

turns.en: One of my favorite takes is from Dwash Patel and he says if you gave any human on the planet 0.00001% of the consumption that an LLM has, any LLM, they would have come up with thousands of new ideas, right? Give me one new idea, one fundamental new idea that's been generated. Yeah. Like I'm big into poetry. Every poem ever written by an LLM is garbage. I think even their fiction writing is terrible. Even the new GPT-4.5, with all due respect to Sam and crew, I think they're terrible, terrible writers. I find them really bad at summarizing. They're really good at extrapolating paperwork. They're very bad at actually distilling the essence of something and what's important. They don't have opinions or a point of view. But they're still unbelievably powerful breakthroughs. They solve search. They solve natural language computing. They make English a programming language. They solve driving. They solve simple coding and backup coding. They solve translation. They solve transcription. They are a fundamental breakthrough in computing. It is a different way to program a computer: rather than you explicitly speak its language and write the code and then run the data through it, you just run enough data through it until it figures out how to write the program. That's huge. But are they AGI? Not yet. And I don't see a direct path from here to there. Maybe we'll have to solve a few more problems before that happens. And I think ASI is a fantasy. I don't think there's any such thing as artificial super intelligence where it has some kind of intelligence that humans can't fathom.
turns.zh: 我最喜欢的观点来自Dwash Patel，他说如果你给地球上任何一个人一个LLM消耗量的0.00001%，任何LLM，他们都会想出成千上万的新想法，对吧？给我一个由它产生的新想法，一个根本性的新想法。是的。比如我很喜欢诗歌。LLM写的每一首诗都是垃圾。我认为它们的小说写作也很糟糕。即使是新的GPT-4.5，对Sam和他的团队没有不敬，我觉得他们是糟糕透顶的作家。我发现它们非常不擅长总结。它们非常擅长外推文书工作。它们实际上非常不擅长提炼事物的本质和重点。它们没有意见或观点。但它们仍然是难以置信的强大突破。它们解决了搜索问题。它们解决了自然语言计算。它们让英语成为编程语言。它们解决了驾驶问题。它们解决了简单编码和备份编码。它们解决了翻译。它们解决了转录。它们是计算领域的根本性突破。这是一种不同的编程计算机的方式：不是你明确地说它的语言并编写代码然后运行数据，而是你运行足够多的数据直到它学会如何编写程序。这很了不起。但它们是AGI吗？还不是。而且我没有看到从这里到那里的直接路径。也许在那之前我们还需要解决更多问题。我认为ASI是幻想。我不认为存在什么人工超级智能，拥有人类无法理解的智能。

turns.en: Okay. Yeah, it seems like I don't know if you're from the less wrong camp or whatever. No, I'm not an AI doomer. I think that's such a flawed line of reasoning. But let's say that you came out of the lesswrong.com like Slate Star Codex world and there was this sort of lineage from computers and AI gets more powerful more powerful more powerful and then you end up AGI ASI and it seems like LLMs have been this sort of orthogonal move from that which are you saying you don't believe they are a step on that path?
turns.zh: 好的。是的，看起来我不知道你是否来自LessWrong阵营之类的。不，我不是AI末日论者。我认为那是一种有缺陷的推理思路。但假设你来自lesswrong.com这样的Slate Star Codex世界，那里有一条从计算机到AI越来越强大越来越强大，最终达到AGI和ASI的谱系，而LLMs似乎是与此正交的运动，你是说你不认为它们是那条路上的一步？

turns.en: I think it's a different form of intelligence. I think Steven Wolf puts it better: it's a different form of intelligence. It's like if you see a jaguar in the jungle, it has a different form of intelligence, and a plant has a form of intelligence in how it can photosynthesize and grow. It's a different form of intelligence. It's not intelligence again like love or happiness is this overloaded word that means many things to many people. But by my definition where the true test is you get what you want out of life, it doesn't even have a life. It doesn't even want anything. It's a different thing. I do think it's unbelievably useful. I'm glad that it exists. You don't see it much yet in large scale production systems replacing humans because of this tendency to hallucinate. So you can't put it into anything mission critical: confidently wrong one time out of ten. Correct. And it doesn't even know when it's wrong. Maybe they'll get that one out of ten down to one out of a hundred. But you'll kind of always want human oversight for critical things.
turns.zh: 我认为这是一种不同形式的智能。我觉得Steven Wolf说得更好：这是一种不同形式的智能。就像你在丛林中看到一只美洲虎，它有不同形式的智能，而植物有通过光合作用和生长体现的智能。这是一种不同形式的智能。它不是那种像爱或幸福一样被过度使用的词，对不同的人意味着不同的事情。但根据我的定义，真正的考验是你从生活中得到你想要的东西，它甚至没有生命。它甚至不想要任何东西。这是不同的东西。我确实认为它非常有用。我很高兴它存在。你还没有看到它在大规模生产系统中取代人类，因为它有幻觉的倾向。所以你不能把它用于任何关键任务：十分之一的情况下自信地犯错。没错。而且它甚至不知道什么时候错了。也许他们会把十分之一降到百分之一。但对于关键的事情，你总是需要人类监督。

turns.en: I always feel so bitter. I'm petty sometimes. My less economist version of me is petty and I always want to teach it a lesson if it gets something wrong. Like how dare you, you were so confident. I'm treating it but I'm anthropomorphizing. It doesn't have a point of view. And they are going to get a lot better and they might get to the point where the error rates are so low that you can put them into certain bounded problems like self-driving. I think will be solved completely because it's a bounded problem: cars don't go off-road and drive through houses and stuff like that. And same way, certain kinds of coding, the creative side of coding I think doesn't go away. I think if anything programmers get even more leverage and more powerful. And rather than computing replacing programmers, programmers use AI to replace everybody else.
turns.zh: 我总是感到有点苦涩。我有时很小气。我那个不那么经济学家的版本很小气，如果它犯了错，我总是想教训它一顿。比如你怎么敢，你那么自信。我在对待它，但我在拟人化。它没有观点。而且它们会变得更好，可能会达到错误率很低的地步，以至于你可以把它们用于某些有边界的问题，比如自动驾驶。我认为这会被完全解决，因为这是一个有边界的问题：汽车不会越野穿过房子之类的东西。同样，某些类型的编码，我认为编码的创造性方面不会消失。我认为程序员反而会获得更大的杠杆和力量。而不是计算取代程序员，程序员用AI取代其他人。

turns.en: On Tesla versus Waymo, would you bet on software or hardware for self-driving?
turns.zh: 关于特斯拉对Waymo，你会押注软件还是硬件来实现自动驾驶？

turns.en: Yeah. So I think Tesla's in the stronger, longer term position, but it's hard to argue with what's working right now. And Waymo is working right now. So I would not underestimate them because there's a learning curve that you go through when you actually deploy something. And Waymo is way ahead in that regard. But Tesla's camera-only approach if it works is superior. It's much more scalable and Tesla knows how to print cars, right? They can just mass manufacture cars. But I think they'll both be around. They'll both be fine. It's everybody else who doesn't have a self-driving vehicle that's screwed.
turns.zh: 是的。所以我认为特斯拉处于更强势的长期位置，但很难反驳现在有效的东西。Waymo现在就在工作。所以我不会低估他们，因为当你实际部署某样东西时，你会经历一个学习曲线。Waymo在这方面领先很多。但特斯拉的纯摄像头方法如果成功的话是更优越的。它更具可扩展性，而且特斯拉知道如何批量生产汽车，对吧？他们可以大规模制造汽车。但我认为两者都会存在。两者都会很好。其他没有自动驾驶汽车的人就惨了。

turns.en: You mentioned kids there and you had a tweet that said, "I'm not convinced that declining fertility needs to be proactively fought." I forgot that one. You're going to have to... I dug deep. Why?
turns.zh: 你提到了孩子，你有一条推文说：“我不认为需要积极对抗生育率下降。”我忘了那条。你得……我挖得很深。为什么？

turns.en: Well, I mean, think back like what was it 30 years ago, 20 years ago? Everybody was saying overpopulation of the earth is going to be a problem. Malthusian ending, we're going to have too many people. And now all of a sudden we're going to have too few people. So part of it is just the doomerism meme is always alive and well, right? Or it just gets repackaged. Yeah. We're running out of oil. We have too much oil, right? You know, it's like the world is cooling, the world is warming. Like there's always something to scream about. The world is ending. There's no progress in technology. AI is going to blow up the world, right? So people tend to overdo in both directions. Now, what is the actual fertility problem? Well, people are having less kids.
turns.zh: 嗯，我的意思是，回想一下30年前、20年前？每个人都说地球人口过剩会是个问题。马尔萨斯式的结局，我们会有太多人。而现在突然我们会有太少人。所以部分原因只是末日论 meme 总是活跃着，对吧？或者只是被重新包装了。是的。我们快没油了。我们有太多油了，对吧？你知道，就像世界在变冷，世界在变暖。总是有什么可以尖叫的。世界末日。技术没有进步。AI会炸毁世界，对吧？所以人们倾向于在两个方向上都过度反应。那么，实际的生育问题是什么？嗯，人们生的孩子更少了。

transcript.sec: Declining birth rates: causes and consequences

turns.en: Are they having less kids because there's a disease? Was there a virus? Did they lose their fertility? The microplastics in the testicles, right? No, it's people are having less kids because they're choosing to have less kids, right? Women have gotten emancipation, independence in the workforce, and they're making more money. People don't need kids as insurance policies. They have less kids. Maybe they're living hedonistic lives. God bless them, right? They want to have more fun. They want to have less kids. I don't see the act of choosing to have less kids as a problem.
turns.zh: 他们生更少的孩子是因为疾病吗？有病毒吗？他们失去生育能力了吗？睾丸里的微塑料，对吧？不，人们生更少的孩子是因为他们选择生更少的孩子，对吧？女性获得了解放，在职场中独立，赚了更多钱。人们不再需要孩子作为保险。他们生更少的孩子。也许他们过着享乐的生活。上帝保佑他们，对吧？他们想要更多乐趣。他们想要更少的孩子。我不认为选择生更少孩子是个问题。

turns.en: Okay, so let's move one level up. It's because of retirees. It's because a large percentage of the population is essentially retiring at the guaranteed age of 65 or 70 thanks to social security. And so they need other people to pay for it. They need more workers in the workforce. And if the workforce is shrinking, then you have a small number of people who are supporting a large number of retirees. And in democracies, you can't take pensions away. The voters vote you out. So they slowly strangle the economy. So what do you do? Then you have a bunch of immigration and then the whole culture changes. You end up in a low trust society and people start fighting over limited resources and how do you control which immigrants come in and how do you make sure that they're good taxpayers after they're in and so on. So you end up in this kind of trap where the low fertility rate is upstream of the downstream problems that are cultural and societal. But I'm not sure that you're going to solve that by making people have more kids. How are you going to meme them into having more kids? And I'm not even sure it's necessarily a problem because keep in mind, you have more resources now. You have less of a burden. Now, there's a flip side where every kid is a lottery ticket and an invention. So, there's some benefit to having more kids, but you can't force it. I think it'll work itself out, right?
turns.zh: 好，那我们往上走一层。这是因为退休人员。因为很大一部分人口基本上在65或70岁的保证年龄退休，多亏了社会保障。所以他们需要别人来买单。他们需要更多劳动力。如果劳动力在萎缩，那么就是少数人在支撑大量退休人员。在民主国家，你不能取消养老金。选民会把你选下去。所以他们慢慢扼杀经济。那怎么办？然后你就有大量移民，整个文化就变了。你最终陷入低信任社会，人们开始争夺有限资源，如何控制哪些移民进来，如何确保他们进来后成为好纳税人等等。所以你陷入这种陷阱，低生育率是下游文化和社会问题的上游。但我不确定通过让人们生更多孩子就能解决这个问题。你怎么用模因让他们生更多孩子？我甚至不确定这一定是个问题，因为记住，你现在有更多资源。负担更轻了。但另一方面，每个孩子都是一张彩票和一项发明。所以生更多孩子有一些好处，但你不能强迫。我认为它会自行解决，对吧？

turns.en: Scott Adams has this great law which he calls the Adams law of slow-moving disasters. When disasters are very slow-moving like peak oil or global warming or population collapse and everyone can kind of see them coming, economics and society as a force solve them because enough individual people have incentives to go solve them. So I don't know exactly how it gets solved but I think it could get solved in various ways. One example could be, you know, maybe people retire later. Maybe AI and automation and robots take care of the older people. Maybe we figure out how to have immigrants while still keeping a high trust society. We kind of put more rules around immigration that protect some of the high trust benefits. Maybe we outsource more things. Maybe we just, you know, have more land and housing to go around. Believe me, if we were having too many kids, everybody would complain about how there's no housing and there's no land, right? So they'll always find something to care about. So, I just don't view this as like a thing that any individual or government action is going to solve. I think economics and incentives over time will solve it. And I'm not even convinced it's like that big of a problem.
turns.zh: 斯科特·亚当斯有一条很棒的定律，他称之为‘缓慢灾难的亚当斯定律’。当灾难非常缓慢，比如石油峰值、全球变暖或人口崩溃，每个人都能看到它们来临，经济和社会作为一种力量会解决它们，因为足够多的个体有动力去解决。所以我不确切知道它会如何解决，但我认为它可以通过各种方式解决。一个例子可能是，人们更晚退休。也许AI、自动化和机器人照顾老人。也许我们找到在保持高信任社会的同时接纳移民的方法。我们对移民施加更多规则，保护一些高信任的好处。也许我们外包更多事情。也许我们只是有更多土地和住房可用。相信我，如果我们孩子太多，每个人都会抱怨没有住房和土地，对吧？所以他们总会找到关心的事。所以我不认为这是任何个人或政府行动能解决的问题。我认为经济和激励会随着时间解决它。我甚至不确定它有那么大问题。

turns.en: Is there anything that you do think is a it may be self-correcting too, which is that if there are too few kids in society and the returns to having kids literally might just go up. It might just be easier to have the incentive to now have a child because there's so few around, they're going to get the best job. They're going to have opportunity resources. It's like everyone wants to... I suppose if you could come at it from a pain side which is you look at all of the other people around who don't have kids. Let's say that pensions completely drop off and the only way that old people are able to survive is if their children pay them some sort of stipend like reverse, you know, send money back up the generations. You go okay well that's a pretty good incentive. That's a good incentive. I also think that people have been memed into thinking that kids make your life worse and that's a pretty bad... what's your experience been? Kids make your life better in every possible way. If you want an automatic built-in meaning to life, have kids. And I think there are these bad psych studies, like most psych studies, unfortunately, that say that people are unhappy when they have kids. Yeah, it's because you're catching them in the middle of changing a diaper and you're saying like, 'Are you glad you had kids or not?' Or they don't even say that. They say, 'Are you happy or not?' And they say, 'No, I'm not happy right now.' But what they don't realize is that person has found something more important than being happy in the moment. They found meaning. And the meaning comes from kids. And if you ask parents, do you regret having kids? I think it would be 99 to 1 against, you know, it would be, 'No, I don't regret having kids. I love having kids. I'm so glad I had kids.' It's incredibly rare to meet a parent that regretted having children. It's pretty good odds. It's extremely good odds. And I think a lot of people get late into life and then they can't admit that they didn't want kids, that they should have had kids. It's kind of late in the game. But a lot of times you see everybody who has a pet, right? And they're pushing them around in a stroller, right? What is that? That's a sublimated desire for children.
turns.zh: 有没有什么你认为也可能是自我纠正的，那就是如果社会上的孩子太少，生孩子的回报实际上可能会上升。现在有动力生孩子可能更容易，因为周围孩子太少，他们会得到最好的工作。他们会有机会和资源。就像每个人都想要……我想如果你从痛苦的一面来看，看看周围所有没有孩子的人。假设养老金完全消失，老年人唯一能生存的方式就是他们的孩子给他们某种津贴，比如反向的，把钱向上代际传递。你会说，嗯，那是个很好的激励。那是个好激励。我也认为人们被模因洗脑，认为孩子会让你的生活更糟，那很糟糕……你的经历是怎样的？孩子在各个方面让你的生活更好。如果你想要生活中自动内置的意义，生孩子。我认为有一些糟糕的心理学研究，像大多数心理学研究一样，不幸的是，它们说人们有孩子时不快乐。是的，因为你在他们换尿布的时候问，‘你高兴有孩子吗？’或者他们甚至不问那个。他们问，‘你快乐吗？’他们回答，‘不，我现在不快乐。’但他们没意识到那个人找到了比当下快乐更重要的东西。他们找到了意义。意义来自孩子。如果你问父母，你后悔有孩子吗？我认为会是99比1反对，他们会说，‘不，我不后悔有孩子。我爱有孩子。我很高兴我有孩子。’遇到后悔有孩子的父母极其罕见。几率很好。几率非常好。我认为很多人到了晚年，然后他们无法承认自己不想要孩子，他们本应有孩子。已经有点晚了。但很多时候你看到每个人养宠物，对吧？他们用婴儿车推着它们，对吧？那是什么？那是被升华的想要孩子的欲望。

turns.en: Malcolm Collins says that having a pet is to children what using porn is to sex. He basically thinks that it's sort of a surrogate. It's definitely in that direction. And, you know, I like pets. I like animals. I don't but I don't like the idea of like neutering or spaying something and then keeping it as a prisoner in the house and having to train it. You know, it's just I don't want to be responsible for that.
turns.zh: 马尔科姆·柯林斯说，养宠物之于孩子，就像看色情片之于性。他基本上认为这是一种替代。肯定是那个方向。而且，你知道，我喜欢宠物。我喜欢动物。但我不喜欢给动物绝育，然后把它当囚犯关在家里，还要训练它。你知道，我只是不想为此负责。

turns.en: Given that you've been thinking more about child rearing kids, what do you hope that your kids learn from their childhood?
turns.zh: 既然你一直在思考养育孩子，你希望你的孩子从童年中学到什么？

turns.en: They should just be happy and do what they want. I don't have particular goals in mind for them. I think that's another route to unhappiness.
turns.zh: 他们应该快乐，做自己想做的事。我对他们没有特定目标。我认为那是通往不快乐的另一条路。

turns.en: That's different though, right? Than learn versus goals. It's not necessarily what do they want? What do you want them to want out of life? Like what is it that you had that idea around your number one job as a parent is to provide unconditional love to your kids.
turns.zh: 但这不一样，对吧？与学习目标不同。不一定他们想要什么？你希望他们从生活中想要什么？就像你那个想法，作为父母的首要工作是给孩子无条件的爱。

turns.en: Yeah, that's it. Right. So I can be loved or I am loved unconditionally. Is that one of the things? I want my kids to feel unconditionally loved and I want them to have high self-esteem. As a consequence of that, but I don't get to choose any... All I get to choose is my output. I can output love. I can't choose what they feel. I can't choose how they behave. I can't choose what they want.
turns.zh: 是的，就是这样。对。所以我可以被爱，或者我被无条件地爱。那是其中之一吗？我希望我的孩子感受到无条件的爱，我希望他们有高自尊。作为结果，但我不能选择任何……我唯一能选择的是我的输出。我可以输出爱。我不能选择他们的感受。我不能选择他们的行为。我不能选择他们想要什么。

turns.en: I can't choose what they turn out to be. And downstream from that, there should be freedom. There should be a degree of freedom that comes from the self-esteem that comes from the unconditionality. They should make their own mistakes and learn their own lessons and have their own desires and fulfill them as is appropriate. I like any parent, I wouldn't want them to be hurt. I wouldn't want them to be unhappy. But I cannot control these things.
turns.zh: 我无法选择他们成为什么样的人。由此而来，应该有自由。这种自由来自于无条件的爱所带来的自尊。他们应该犯自己的错误，吸取自己的教训，有自己的欲望，并以适当的方式实现它们。像任何父母一样，我不希望他们受伤，不希望他们不快乐。但我无法控制这些。

turns.en: You replied to my friend Rob Henderson. He was talking about how kids fall asleep more quickly when they're being carried and you said cry it out and co-sleeping is dangerous. What's IYI science? IY is Nim TB. He talks about intellectual yet idiot. These are people who are overeducated and they deny basic common sense.
turns.zh: 你回复了我的朋友 Rob Henderson。他谈到孩子被抱着时入睡更快，你说“哭睡法”和同床睡是危险的。什么是 IYI 科学？IY 是 Nim TB。他谈到“知识分子却白痴”。这些人受过过度教育，却否认基本常识。

turns.en: Okay. So there's a lot of that that goes on in child rearing thanks to really bad studies and bad public medical directives. So, for example, a few parents maybe they're drunk or they're high or they're just other issues and they roll over their kid when they're sleeping, the kid suffocates or they neglect their kid and then is that co-sleeping having them in the bed.
turns.zh: 好吧。在育儿方面有很多这样的情况，这要归功于非常糟糕的研究和糟糕的公共医疗指令。例如，有些父母可能喝醉了、吸毒了或有其他问题，他们睡觉时翻身压到孩子，孩子窒息，或者他们忽视孩子，然后这就是同床睡吗？

turns.en: Yeah. Exactly. Or there's the modern proclamation. And so, because of that, they say, 'Well, don't co-sleep with your kids.' Well, the kids in every society through all of human history co-slept with their parents. Where else do you think they were sleeping? They weren't houses in multiple rooms.
turns.zh: 是的，没错。或者有现代的宣告。因此，他们说：“不要和孩子同床睡。”但在所有人类历史上，每个社会的孩子都和父母同床睡。你觉得他们还能睡在哪里？那时候可没有多个房间的房子。

turns.en: Yeah. Exactly. Put in the other tent. It's just nonsense. Co-sleeping has been around since the dawn of time. So has feeding kids cow milk or goat milk when breast milk runs out or is not available. Yet we're told formula, made with soy and corn syrup, which was invented recently, is somehow better than cow milk. And cow milk can be dangerous for your kids and co-sleeping is dangerous for your kids and cry it out is the right answer. All of that is nonsense.
turns.zh: 是的，没错。放在另一个帐篷里。这完全是胡说八道。同床睡从古至今一直存在。母乳耗尽或无法获得时喂孩子牛奶或羊奶也是如此。然而我们被告知，用大豆和玉米糖浆制成的配方奶，最近才发明出来，却比牛奶更好。牛奶对孩子危险，同床睡对孩子危险，而“哭睡法”才是正确答案。所有这些都毫无意义。

turns.en: I mean, it's very clear that we raise children throughout human history without these interventions. And to me, the idea that you're going to let your kid cry it out, I get why that's done for practical reasons so that you can get some sleep and you can go to work in the morning, but the reality is when you let the kid cry it out, you're letting the kid bawl until it finally gives up. I mean, the kid left by itself to cry it out in the wild, it's going to get eaten, right? It's going to get eaten by a tiger. So this kid is starting off on the wrong foundation.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，很明显，我们在整个人类历史上养育孩子都没有这些干预。对我来说，让孩子哭到睡着的想法，我理解这样做是出于实际原因，这样你就能睡会儿觉，早上能去上班，但现实是，当你让孩子哭到睡着时，你是在让孩子一直哭到最终放弃。我的意思是，如果孩子在野外独自哭到睡着，它会被吃掉，对吧？会被老虎吃掉。所以这个孩子从一开始就建立在错误的基础上。

turns.en: The one I mentioned earlier about the idea that babies don't feel pain. That's ludicrous, right? I've never heard that before. That's such a wild idea.
turns.zh: 我之前提到的那个观点，认为婴儿感觉不到疼痛。这很荒谬，对吧？我以前从未听说过。这真是个疯狂的想法。

turns.en: Yeah, I'm not saying that's 100% true. I read it on the child in the cheek quite hard. And that's an academy. I read it on Twitter and I did one level confirmation on it, but it's so ludicrous that I should probably do two or three level confirmations on it before I talk about it. But there are definitely some people who believe that enough that it was a thing in certain circles for a while.
turns.zh: 是的，我不是说那百分之百是真的。我在……孩子脸颊上很用力地读到过。那是个学院。我在推特上读到它，并做了一级确认，但它太荒谬了，我可能在谈论它之前应该做两三级确认。但确实有一些人相信它，以至于在一段时间内，在某些圈子里它是个事。

turns.en: But I think we just go through these IYI beliefs, these intellectual beliefs come from people who take a little bit of knowledge and extrapolate it too far. They think we know more than we know due to recent scientific studies and these are junk science. These are low power studies on very certain contexts that then get over applied. Behavioral psych is very guilty of this but it's true across a lot of science. So even with science, you have to be skeptical. You have to look very carefully at, does it apply in the right context or not? Is it from good sources? Did they run enough high-powered studies widely accepted?
turns.zh: 但我认为我们只是经历了这些 IYI 信念，这些知识分子信念来自那些掌握一点知识就过度外推的人。他们认为由于最近的科学研究，我们知道得比实际多，而这些是垃圾科学。这些是在非常特定背景下进行的低效研究，然后被过度应用。行为心理学在这方面罪责很大，但很多科学领域都是如此。所以即使对科学，你也必须保持怀疑。你必须非常仔细地看：它是否适用于正确的背景？它来自好的来源吗？他们是否进行了足够多的高效研究并被广泛接受？

turns.en: And there are a whole bunch of things you're just not supposed to talk about. You're not supposed to say anything negative about vaccines because god forbid what if they don't get the polio vaccine, right? And that's part of the reason why the recent vaccine debate because we've taken our worship for vaccines too far because we don't want people to not take non-essential vaccines. So it gets overdone.
turns.zh: 还有一大堆事情你根本不应该谈论。你不应该说任何关于疫苗的负面言论，因为万一他们不打小儿麻痹症疫苗怎么办，对吧？这也是最近疫苗辩论的部分原因，因为我们把对疫苗的崇拜推得太远了，因为我们不希望人们不打非必要的疫苗。所以它被过度了。

turns.en: So the same way there's this whole SIDS thing, sudden infant death syndrome, right? It's like no, kids don't suddenly mysteriously die. More likely there was neglect or there was a problem and then whoever was the caretaker doesn't want to admit to the problem or didn't recognize the problem, but kids don't just spontaneously die in the crib, right? So they talk about swaddling babies. You swaddle babies, basically tie them up, mummify them. So you constrict them so they die of SIDS where they roll over and they can't get back. I mean, it's just all this craziness around fundraising. It's a real minefield.
turns.zh: 同样，还有整个 SIDS（婴儿猝死综合征）的事情，对吧？比如不，孩子不会突然神秘地死亡。更可能是存在忽视或问题，然后照顾者不想承认问题或没有认识到问题，但孩子不会无缘无故死在婴儿床里，对吧？所以他们谈论包裹婴儿。你把婴儿包裹起来，基本上绑起来，像木乃伊一样。所以你限制他们，导致他们翻身时无法翻回来而死于 SIDS。我的意思是，所有这些围绕筹款的疯狂。这真是一个雷区。

turns.en: It's a minefield. And you have these scared parents having a kid for the first time and they open a book and they start reading how to raise children when I would argue that your natural instincts on what to do with your child are actually pretty good.
turns.zh: 这是一个雷区。你有这些第一次生孩子的害怕的父母，他们打开一本书，开始阅读如何养育孩子，而我认为你对孩子该做什么的自然本能实际上相当不错。

turns.en: It's funny when my wife and I had our first baby. I remember, at the hospital, sorry, the first one was natural birth at the birthing center. We went home. I was like, 'There you go. That's it.' And we're like, what do we do? Where's the instruction manual? You take them home and then you relax and you realize actually instincts are pretty good. You know, if the kid cries, check to see if they're clean, feed them, all that. It's like your basic instincts are actually very very good. And kids' instincts are actually very very good. They know what they want and they want things for a reason and they can encourage you to give it to them.
turns.zh: 有趣的是，当我和妻子有了第一个孩子时。我记得，在医院，抱歉，第一个是自然分娩，在分娩中心。我们回家了。我心想：“好了，就这样。”然后我们想，我们该怎么办？说明书在哪里？你把孩子带回家，然后放松下来，你意识到实际上本能相当不错。你知道，如果孩子哭，检查他们是否干净，喂他们，等等。你的基本本能实际上非常好。孩子的本能也非常好。他们知道自己想要什么，他们想要东西是有原因的，他们可以鼓励你给他们。

turns.en: Yes. It's usually children are not deficient adults who can't reason. And to some extent that's true but mostly it's not true. Mostly they have very good reasons for what they want and you as a parent mostly have communication problems with them. They can't yet communicate to you. You can't communicate to them. So early on with my kids, I tried to focus on teaching them basic explanatory theories as opposed to having them memorize things. That's just the most nol solution.
turns.zh: 是的。通常孩子不是无法推理的有缺陷的成年人。在某种程度上这是真的，但大部分不是真的。大多数情况下，他们想要什么有很好的理由，而你作为父母，主要和他们有沟通问题。他们还无法与你沟通。你也无法与他们沟通。所以早期和我的孩子在一起时，我试图专注于教他们基本的解释性理论，而不是让他们死记硬背。那是最“诺尔”的解决方案。

turns.en: I'll give you a very simple example, right? Okay. So this is Twitter and this is the how to get rich without getting lucky thread. So the first one well a simple one is you know how does knowledge get created? If you follow the critical rationalism David Deutsch philosophy, then it's by guessing and then by testing your guesses.
turns.zh: 我给你一个非常简单的例子，好吗？好的。这是推特上的“如何不靠运气致富”帖子。第一个，一个简单的例子是，你知道知识是如何创造的吗？如果你遵循批判理性主义大卫·多伊奇的哲学，那么就是通过猜测，然后测试你的猜测。

transcript.sec: Teaching through questioning and germ theory

turns.en: So whenever they ask me something like, well, why do you think that is? Well, how would we figure out if that's true? Right? So that's a basic game you can play involving them. But another one is that a lot of the rules that you teach kids have to do with hygiene, right? You must brush your teeth, cover your mouth when you cough, clean up after yourself, don't touch that, wash your hands after you do this, don't eat food off the floor. But all of these are subsumed under the germ theory of disease. So if you instead go on YouTube and show them videos of germs or have them look under a microscope at anything, they're like, 'Ah, they can infer what's going on. Yeah, there's creepy crawlies everywhere and I got to watch out for them.' And then you can talk about how if you look at humans, our real enemy are pathogens. I think a lot of aging and disease are actually downstream of our competition with pathogens over time, to a point that people still don't fully appreciate.
turns.zh: 所以每当他们问我类似的问题，比如'你为什么这么认为？'或者'我们怎么才能知道这是真的？'对吧？这就是一个你可以和他们玩的基本游戏。但另一个点是，你教给孩子的很多规则都和卫生有关，比如你必须刷牙、咳嗽时捂住嘴、收拾好自己的东西、别碰那个、做完这个要洗手、别捡地上的食物吃。但这些都可以归结到疾病细菌理论。所以如果你在YouTube上给他们看细菌的视频，或者让他们用显微镜看任何东西，他们就会说'啊，他们能推断出发生了什么。没错，到处都是脏东西，我得小心它们。'然后你就可以跟他们讲，如果你看看人类，我们真正的敌人是病原体。我认为很多衰老和疾病实际上都源于我们与病原体长期竞争的结果，这一点人们至今仍未完全认识到。

turns.en: There's a Red Queen hypothesis which is that we undergo sexual selection to mix up our genes. So every 20 years, every generation, mix up your genes. But if you look at how bacteria and viruses mutate through just random mutations, their mix-up rate on their genes and evolution rate is roughly the same as ours. Even though they go through thousands of generations in those 20 years because they're not doing sexual selection, they're doing asexual replication and mutation, their evolutionary rate is roughly equivalent to ours. So we're in a Red Queen race where we're both running at roughly the same speed using very different strategies. But a lot of how we're involved is around pathogens. Like our immune system is one of the most expensive things to run in the body; so much is about immune system optimization. That's about pathogens. Junk DNA in bacteria and CRISPR was discovered because in bacteria their DNA evolved to fight viruses, and the way it does that is by taking viral DNA and snipping it up every time there's a viral attack and storing it in their own DNA so they have a copy so they can recognize it next time it attacks. A lot of the population structure of species determines how long their lifespans are. So if in a given species there's a very high rate of infection, then you'll have these older members of the population carrying diseases that will then infect the young. So it's important for that species to get rid of the old faster. So the higher the disease rate in a given population, the less long-lived the entire population, so the older ones don't infect the younger ones. That's a hypothesis and I think it's true. It's an interesting hypothesis.
turns.zh: 有一个红皇后假说，认为我们通过有性生殖来混合基因。所以每20年，每一代，基因都会混合。但如果你看看细菌和病毒如何通过随机突变来变异，它们的基因混合率和进化率大致和我们相同。尽管它们在20年内经历了成千上万代，因为它们不进行有性生殖，而是无性复制和突变，但它们的进化速度大致和我们相当。所以我们处于一场红皇后竞赛中，双方以大致相同的速度奔跑，但策略截然不同。但我们很多方面都与病原体有关。比如我们的免疫系统是身体运行成本最高的系统之一，很多都关乎免疫系统优化。这都与病原体有关。细菌中的垃圾DNA和CRISPR的发现，是因为细菌的DNA进化来对抗病毒，其方式是在每次病毒攻击时，将病毒DNA剪下来并储存在自己的DNA中，这样它们就有了副本，下次攻击时就能识别。物种的种群结构很大程度上决定了它们的寿命。所以如果某个物种的感染率很高，那么年长的成员就会携带疾病，进而感染年轻的成员。因此，该物种需要更快地淘汰年老者。所以一个种群中疾病率越高，整个种群的寿命就越短，这样年老者就不会感染年轻者。这是一个假说，我认为它是正确的。这是一个有趣的假说。

turns.en: Homeostasis within the human body, how we're always returning to a given level of things, like that's a fundamental part of our makeup: our temperature, pH, blood pressure, and so on under homeostasis. But if you engage in any kind of signaling, like you take a peptide for example, that's a signaling molecule. You take a hormone externally, the body will counteract it. You take testosterone, the body will downregulate its own production very fast. And the body releases its own hormones in pulses rather than steady state. Why is that? Well, that's because bacteria and viruses can infect your body and trick your body. They can take it over. Like toxoplasmosis does this, rabies does this. They take over macroscopic structure, structural bodies. And small bacteria and viruses would hack our bodies and literally take them over if we didn't have defense mechanisms. And one of those defense mechanisms is homeostasis. Anytime you see something getting out of whack, you immediately push back really hard on it because like, did I just get infected? Is something trying to take me over? It's also why hormones get released in pulses at night rather than in steady state low levels because enemy bacteria can release toxins or the same signaling molecules in small quantities but they can't pulse. They can't coordinate to pulse. So your body can coordinate to pulse as a macroscopic object but microscopic objects can't coordinate to create the same pulses.
turns.zh: 人体内的稳态，我们总是回到某个设定水平，这是我们构成的基本部分：体温、pH值、血压等等都在稳态下。但如果你进行任何形式的信号传递，比如你服用一种肽，那是一种信号分子。你从外部摄入一种激素，身体会抵消它。你摄入睾酮，身体会很快下调自身产量。而且身体以脉冲形式释放自己的激素，而不是稳态。这是为什么？那是因为细菌和病毒可以感染你的身体并欺骗它。它们可以接管。比如弓形虫就是这样，狂犬病也是这样。它们接管宏观结构、结构体。而微小的细菌和病毒会入侵我们的身体，如果没有防御机制，它们真的会接管我们。而防御机制之一就是稳态。每当看到什么东西偏离正常，你就会立即强烈地反击，因为你会想：我是不是被感染了？是不是有什么东西想接管我？这也是为什么激素在夜间以脉冲形式释放，而不是以稳定的低水平释放，因为敌人细菌可以释放毒素或相同的信号分子，但量很小，而且它们无法脉冲。它们无法协调产生脉冲。所以你的身体作为一个宏观物体可以协调产生脉冲，但微观物体无法协调产生同样的脉冲。

turns.en: Oh that's cool. Yeah. So there's all I mean so you know that it's coming from you. Is that why?
turns.zh: 哦，这很酷。是的。所以这一切都意味着你知道它来自你自身。这就是原因吗？

turns.en: Correct. It's endogenous rather than exogenous. So I never knew that. And that's why we resist a lot of exogenous treatments. A lot of our medical treatments don't work. Anyway, so these are... there's a bunch more I could go on, but I think that a lot of you know, you see this in cancers where a lot of bacteria show up, like the Epstein-Barr virus shows up in a lot of cancers, and now it seems like the gut microbiome influences so many things. Basically bacteria and viruses are at the top of the food chain compared to us. Like we are top of the well-known food chain, but bacteria and viruses eat us. Fungus eats us. So these microscopic predators are our natural predators. And so a lot of aging, societal structure, hygiene, religious strictures against pork, circumcision, all of these things are designed to resist bacteria and viruses. So if you can teach children this philosophy at an early age, you shortcut all the debates.
turns.zh: 没错。这是内源性的，而不是外源性的。我以前不知道这一点。这就是为什么我们抵抗很多外源性治疗。很多医疗手段都不起作用。总之，这些是……我还可以继续说很多，但我认为，你在癌症中看到很多细菌出现，比如EB病毒出现在很多癌症中，而且现在肠道微生物组似乎影响很多方面。基本上，细菌和病毒与我们相比处于食物链顶端。就像我们是众所周知的食物链顶端，但细菌和病毒吃我们。真菌吃我们。所以这些微观捕食者是我们天然的捕食者。因此，很多衰老、社会结构、卫生、宗教对猪肉的禁忌、割礼等等，所有这些都是为了抵抗细菌和病毒。所以如果你能在孩子很小的时候就教给他们这种哲学，你就绕过了所有争论。

turns.en: How effective have you been at teaching that philosophy to children?
turns.zh: 你在向孩子传授这种哲学方面有多有效？

turns.en: That one, I think I've been pretty effective. I've drilled that one at home. The one I haven't quite gotten around to yet is evolution. Like I'm starting to do little bits of that, you know, like we came from monkeys. What does that mean? Already got them thinking about some of the deeper questions. I did ask my young son like, you know, can nothing exist? I thought that was a fun question. So, I like to throw a fun question.
turns.zh: 那个，我觉得我挺有效的。我在家里反复强调过。我还没怎么涉及的是进化论。比如我开始讲一点，比如我们是从猴子进化来的。那意味着什么？已经让他们思考一些更深层次的问题了。我问过我的小儿子，比如'无'能存在吗？我觉得那是个有趣的问题。所以我喜欢抛出有趣的问题。

turns.en: How old is he now? Like four, three. No, no, he's he's eight. Oh, right. An 8-year-old and a six-year-old.
turns.zh: 他现在多大了？四岁、三岁？不，不，他八岁了。哦，对。一个八岁，一个六岁。

turns.en: So, I asked them both like, 'Can nothing exist?' And they had pretty good answers, right? Another one we played with the other day was like, 'What is the matrix?' Okay. You know, what is this? What is all this? I just find it entertaining. It's just fun to talk about these questions with your kids. I'm not saying that one is a good way of child raising. It's not leading to any deeper learning other than maybe just have them start or continue to question the basic structure of reality and not move past it so quickly. Also to take joy. You know what's the meta lesson that's being taught there? Dad spends time asking questions to which there are not necessarily an answer because there is something enjoyable in the process of learning and trying to decipher what's happening possibly. Also, dad tries not too hard to teach people things. I don't want to be didactic.
turns.zh: 所以我问他们两个：'无'能存在吗？他们回答得相当不错，对吧？还有一次我们玩的是：'什么是矩阵？'好吧。你知道，这是什么？这一切是什么？我只是觉得这很有趣。和孩子们讨论这些问题很有意思。我并不是说这是一种好的育儿方式。这不会带来更深层次的学习，除了可能让他们开始或继续质疑现实的基本结构，而不是那么快就跳过它。还有，要享受乐趣。你知道这背后的元教训是什么吗？爸爸花时间问那些不一定有答案的问题，因为学习过程和试图解读正在发生的事情本身就有乐趣。而且，爸爸不太刻意去教别人东西。我不想变成说教。

turns.en: He helps them to arrive at it. Yeah. Correct. Correct. Dad is here to help you solve problems when you have problems and you constantly have problems. So if you come to dad, dad can help explain to you how he would solve the problem. But most of the time they don't want that. Most of the time they just want me to solve the problem, right? So sometimes they have to play it dumb. It's like why is my Wi-Fi not working on my computer? I'm like I don't know. Did you click on that thing? Look, you've got like a rebellious sovereign child. Sovereign as they may be, but sometimes they still need the dad to step in. So in addition to feeling loved and having high self-esteem, I think the most important trait that would be nice to not rob them of is agency. I want them to preserve their agency. They're born naturally agentic and willful, but a lot of child raising can beat that out of them by essentially domesticating them. That's right. And I would rather have wild animals and wolves than have well-trained dogs because I'm not going to be around to take care of them. Yeah.
