So, they're going to have to be able to look after themselves. Exactly. Yeah. A friend of mine, Parsa on Air Chat, he had a great saying. He said he wants his children to be quick to learn and hard to kill. I thought that was pretty good.
turns.zh: 他帮助他们自己找到答案。是的，没错。爸爸在这里是帮你解决问题的，你有问题，而且你总是有问题。所以如果你来找爸爸，爸爸可以解释他会怎么解决。但大多数时候他们并不想要那样，大多数时候他们只是希望我直接解决问题，对吧？所以有时候他们得装傻。比如‘为什么我的电脑连不上Wi-Fi？’我就说‘我不知道。你点那个东西了吗？’你看，你有一个叛逆的、自主的孩子。他们可能很自主，但有时还是需要爸爸介入。所以除了感受到爱和拥有高自尊之外，我认为最不应该剥夺他们的特质就是能动性。我希望他们保留自己的能动性。他们天生就有主动性和意志力，但很多育儿方式通过驯化他们，把这给打掉了。没错。我宁愿要野生动物和狼，也不要训练有素的狗，因为我不会一直在他们身边照顾他们。所以他们必须能够照顾自己。完全正确。是的。我一个朋友，Parsa，在Air Chat上，他说了句很棒的话：他希望他的孩子‘学得快，死不了’。我觉得这说得很好。

turns.en: I remember you saying just thinking about sort of future and culture and stuff like that. I remember you saying that the left had won the culture war and now they're just driving around shooting the survivors, right? After the last 6 months of change that we've seen and sort of where we're at at the moment, what do you think the future of the culture war looks like?
turns.zh: 我记得你之前说过，关于未来和文化之类的东西。我记得你说左派已经赢得了文化战争，现在他们只是在四处扫射幸存者，对吧？经过过去六个月的变化和我们目前的处境，你认为文化战争的未来会是什么样子？

turns.en: It's not over yet. They definitely won earlier rounds. They took over institutions. I think now it's much more of a fair fight. Where you have people like Elon, you know, kind of supporting so there are these different forces through history, right? Historians will argue about this. But there's a theory of the great man of history thing where it's like oh you have the Einsteins, you have the Teslas, you have the Genghis Khans and the Caesars, right? They determine the flow of history. And then there's the other point of view that no there are these massive forces at play you know demographics and geography and so on and then the particular great man doesn't matter they just come and go Napoleon doesn't matter they would have been somebody else the specific names are not important. And because of the leftist turn that our institutions took in the last few decades they now only subscribe to the great forces theory of history not the great man theory of history. But I think now we're seeing the two play out where you're seeing Trump and Elon and other individuals rising up and saying no, we resist.
turns.zh: 还没结束。他们确实赢了前几轮，接管了机构。我认为现在更像是一场公平的战斗。有像埃隆这样的人在支持，所以历史上总有这些不同的力量，对吧？历史学家会争论这个。有一种‘历史伟人’理论，就是说有爱因斯坦、特斯拉、成吉思汗、凯撒这些人，他们决定了历史的走向。另一种观点则认为，是人口、地理等巨大力量在起作用，具体的伟人并不重要，他们来来去去，拿破仑不重要，换成别人也一样，具体名字不重要。由于过去几十年我们机构的左转，他们现在只信奉‘巨大力量’的历史理论，而不是‘伟人’理论。但我认为现在我们看到两者都在上演，特朗普、埃隆和其他个人正在崛起，说‘不，我们抵抗’。

turns.en: Yeah, that's interesting.
turns.zh: 是的，这很有趣。

turns.en: And I think that unfortunately the battle between these collectivists and great forces versus individuals, it's as old as humanity itself. And it is fundamental to the species. We are not a completely individualistic species. You know, no man is an island. A single person can't do anything by themselves. But we're also not a Borg. We're not a beehive. We're not an ant colony. We're not all just drones marching along. So, which is it? We're somewhere in the middle. And the human race is always kind of bouncing between the two. We like strong leaders. We like to be led. We like to coordinate our forces and mass and do things. But at the same time, we're also all individuals and willing to break away and willing to do our own thing and everyone's always fighting to be a leader and there's always status games going on. So, there's a pendulum that's always swinging back and forth. And in modern economics, the way that manifests is between Marxism and capitalism, right? Marxism is like from each according to his ability to each according to his needs. We're all equal. There's a millennial project. We're all going to be equal in the end. And don't try and stand out, but do what's good for everybody. And there's a religious aspect to it. And then the capitalist individualist is like libertarian. Every man for himself. You just each do what you want and it'll work out for the greater good. That's Adam Smith. You know, the invisible hand of the market will feed you. The baker should bake and the butcher should butcher and the candlestick maker should make candlesticks and it'll all work out. Each person does their best and they trade. So which theory is correct? And I think there's always going to be a battle between the two. And I think the interesting thing is what's going on there's a modern flavor to it which changes it. The modern flavor is that the individual is getting more powerful because they're becoming more leverage. So someone like an Elon Musk can have the leverage of tens of thousands of brilliant engineers and producers working for him. He can have factories of robots manufacturing things. He can have hundreds of billions of dollars of capital behind him and he can project himself through media to hundreds of millions of people. That is more power than any individual could have had historically. So the great men of history are becoming greater. That said, that same leverage is increasing the gap between the haves and have-nots. So in the wealth game, more people are winning overall and the average is going up. But in the status game, there are essentially more losers. There are more invisible men and women who are getting nothing out of life and have no leverage. Relatively speaking. Objectively speaking, they might be better off. They still have phones and they still have TVs and they're not absolutist creatures though. We're relative creatures. Correct. And so to the extent that we're relative creatures, there are more losers than winners. And in a democracy, those people will outnumber the winners and they will vote the winners down. And so that's the battle that kind of goes on and the democracy has gotten very broad. And so one of my other quips is that it's not the right to vote that gives you power, it's power that gives you the right to vote. So we've confused the two. So what happened was, voting started as a way for people who had power to divide up the power, not fight amongst themselves. The winners of the revolution, the winners of the war, the people in the House of Lords and the House of Commons, they divide up power amongst themselves. to say, 'Hey, we have all the money. We have the power. We are the knights. We have the swords. We have the warriors. We could kill everybody, but we don't want to just fight each other all day long. We don't have to be Game of Thrones forever.'
turns.zh: 我认为不幸的是，这种集体主义、巨大力量与个人之间的斗争，和人类本身一样古老。这是物种的根本。我们不是一个完全个人主义的物种。你知道，没有人是一座孤岛。一个人无法独自完成任何事情。但我们也不是博格人，不是蜂巢，不是蚁群，不是所有工蚁都在行进。那么到底是哪种？我们处于中间。人类总是在这两者之间摇摆。我们喜欢强大的领导者，喜欢被领导，喜欢协调力量、聚集起来做事。但同时，我们也是独立的个体，愿意脱离，愿意做自己的事，每个人都想成为领导者，总在进行地位游戏。所以，钟摆总是在来回摆动。在现代经济中，这表现为马克思主义和资本主义之间的斗争，对吧？马克思主义是‘各尽所能，按需分配’。我们都是平等的。这是一个千年计划，最终人人平等。不要试图脱颖而出，要做对大家都有利的事。这还有宗教的一面。而资本主义个人主义则是自由意志主义：人人为己。每个人都做自己想做的事，最终会带来更大的利益。这是亚当·斯密。市场的无形之手会养活你。面包师烤面包，屠夫切肉，蜡烛匠做蜡烛，一切都会好起来。每个人都尽力而为，然后进行交易。那么哪种理论是正确的？我认为这两者之间总会有一场战斗。有趣的是，现在有一种现代风味改变了它。现代风味是，个人变得更强大，因为他们获得了更多的杠杆。像埃隆·马斯克这样的人，可以拥有数万名优秀工程师和生产者的杠杆为他工作。他可以有机器人工厂制造产品，有数千亿美元的资本支持，通过媒体向数亿人展示自己。这比历史上任何个人都拥有更多的权力。所以历史伟人变得更伟大了。但话说回来，同样的杠杆也扩大了富人和穷人之间的差距。在财富游戏中，总体上赢家更多，平均值在上升。但在地位游戏中，输家更多。有更多隐形的男男女女，他们从生活中得不到任何东西，也没有杠杆。相对而言。客观地说，他们可能过得更好。他们还有手机和电视，但我们不是绝对主义者。我们是相对主义者。没错。因此，作为相对主义者，输家多于赢家。在民主制度中，这些人会超过赢家，他们会投票把赢家拉下来。这就是正在进行的斗争，而民主已经变得非常广泛。所以我还有一个俏皮话：不是投票权赋予你权力，而是权力赋予你投票权。我们混淆了这两者。事情是这样的：投票最初是权力拥有者分配权力、避免内斗的方式。革命的赢家、战争的赢家、上议院和下议院的人，他们自己分配权力。说：‘嘿，我们拥有所有金钱和权力。我们是骑士，有剑，有战士。我们可以杀死所有人，但我们不想整天互相争斗。我们不必永远上演《权力的游戏》。’

turns.en: So, we're going to divide up power by voting among ourselves. But then, as society goes on and becomes more and more peaceful, that franchise for voting gets spread. It gets spread to people who don't have land, who don't have power, who may not be able to inflict physical violence. And then eventually, you get to the point where everybody's voting. And everybody was voting for candy and fairies and all the free things in life. And then eventually people start voting to oppress each other. The 51% in any domain vote to oppress the 49%, the tyranny of the majority. But not all of them are willing to back that up with physical power. And so you can end up in a situation where people who don't have physical power are using the institutions of the state to control the people who do have physical power. As a simple example, taking the United States, the people who don't have guns voting to disarm the people that do have guns. Well, if the people who do have guns get coordinated and care enough, you can't do that. So I think eventually these societal structures are unstable; they break down because eventually the people who have the power say, 'No, wait a minute, you don't get to vote. You only got to vote because you had power, and now you don't have power, and you're somehow trying to vote.' All of nature, all of society, all of capitalism, all of human endeavors are underpinned by physical violence. And that is a very hard truth to swallow and hard to get away from. Nature is red in tooth and claw. If you don't fight, you don't survive. You don't live, you die. And that's true of everything alive today. And humans are no different. So giving up physical power and then thinking you can exercise political power fails. Which is why every communist revolution, which is all about equality and kumbaya and brothers and sisters, ends up being run by a bunch of thugs. Because if you don't have a way to divide up the wealth based on merit, then it's always going to be based on power and influence. The thugs with the guns always win in the end. So the question is just: can you keep the thugs with the guns paid and happy in a successful society where you're allocating based on merit? Because if you can't, then you do it based on power. So I do think that this battle is not over, but that's because it never stopped. It's always been there from day one. It will continue.
turns.zh: 所以，我们打算通过投票来分配权力。但随着社会越来越和平，投票权逐渐扩大，扩展到那些没有土地、没有权力、可能无法施加暴力的人。最终，每个人都拥有了投票权。每个人都投票支持糖果、仙女，以及生活中所有免费的东西。然后，人们开始投票压迫彼此。任何领域中的51%都会投票压迫49%，这就是多数人的暴政。但并非所有人都愿意用武力来支撑这一点。于是，你可能会陷入这样一种局面：没有武力的人利用国家机构来控制有武力的人。举个简单的例子，在美国，没有枪的人投票解除有枪的人的武装。但如果持枪者协调一致并足够在意，你就做不到这一点。所以我认为，这些社会结构最终是不稳定的，它们会崩溃，因为最终掌握权力的人会说：‘不，等等，你没有投票权。你之所以有投票权，是因为你曾经拥有权力，而现在你没有权力了，你却还想投票。’整个自然界、整个社会、整个资本主义、所有人类努力，都建立在暴力之上。这是一个很难接受、也很难摆脱的残酷事实。自然界是血淋淋的。如果你不战斗，你就无法生存。你不活，就会死。今天所有活着的生物都是如此，人类也不例外。所以，放弃武力，然后以为可以行使政治权力，这是行不通的。这就是为什么每一次共产主义革命——尽管口号是平等、团结、兄弟姐妹——最终都被一群暴徒掌控。因为如果你没有办法根据功绩来分配财富，那么分配就总是基于权力和影响力。持枪的暴徒最终总是赢家。所以问题只是：在一个基于功绩分配的成功社会中，你能让持枪的暴徒得到报酬并感到满意吗？如果不能，那就只能基于权力来分配。所以我认为这场战斗还没有结束，但因为它从未停止过。它从第一天起就存在，并将继续下去。

turns.en: Is it a battle to not care about the news in an age of news saturation? All of this stuff, headlines 24 hours a day, streamed directly into your consciousness through a device in your pocket. A lot of what we've spoken about today is freedom. Freedom from having to think about things or care about things that you do not have control over or that you shouldn't or that you don't want to. And yet people are just submerged up to the bottom of their nostrils, basically drowning in worry. So how? Is it a battle to sort of stay out of the news when you're saturated in it?
turns.zh: 在新闻饱和的时代，不去关心新闻是一场战斗吗？所有这些内容，24小时的头条新闻，通过你口袋里的设备直接涌入你的意识。我们今天谈到的很多内容都是关于自由——免于思考或关心那些你无法控制、不应该或不想关心的事情的自由。然而，人们却像被淹没到鼻孔底部，基本上在担忧中溺水。那么，怎么做？当新闻铺天盖地时，置身事外是一场战斗吗？

turns.en: Yeah. I mean, as you're saying, the human brain has not evolved to handle all the world's emergencies breaking in real time, and you can't care about everything, and you'll go insane if you try. It doesn't mean you shouldn't care at all. There's no 'should.' I mean, if you want to care, go ahead and care. I would just say that you're probably better off only caring about things that are local or things that you can affect. So, if you really care about something that's in the news, then by all means care about it, but make a difference. Go do something about it. And make sure that it's your overwhelming desire and you don't have five other desires at the same time. Also, just realize the consequences of it. You're going to be unhappy until that thing gets fixed, and that thing will often be out of your control. Desire is a contract to be unhappy until you get what you want. But exactly, for the most part, that's something that is in your life. It's like 'until I lose the weight,' 'until I get the job.' Outside too. Yeah. If it's 'until the carbon dioxide parts per million are below this particular number,' that's a tough one. Or all the people with Trump derangement syndrome, right? He's living rent-free in their heads and driving them insane. And I get it. I mean, there are politicians who have definitely driven me insane as well. But it comes at a very high cost and is something that is out of your control that you cannot really influence. So it's probably good to at least be conscious of it.
turns.zh: 是的。我是说，正如你所说，人类大脑并没有进化到能实时处理世界上所有的紧急事件，你不可能关心所有事情，如果你尝试，你会发疯。这并不意味着你完全不应该关心。没有‘应该’这回事。我是说，如果你想关心，那就去关心。我只是想说，你最好只关心那些本地的事情，或者你能影响的事情。所以，如果你真的关心新闻中的某件事，那就尽管去关心，但要有所作为。去做点什么。并且确保这是你压倒性的愿望，而不是同时有五个其他愿望。同时，也要意识到后果。在那件事解决之前，你会一直不开心，而那件事往往是你无法控制的。欲望是一份让你在得到想要的东西之前一直不开心的契约。但确切地说，大多数情况下，那是你生活中的事情，比如‘直到我减肥成功’，‘直到我得到那份工作’。外部的事情也一样。如果是‘直到二氧化碳浓度低于某个数值’，那很难。或者所有患有‘特朗普精神错乱综合征’的人，对吧？他免费住在他们脑子里，把他们逼疯。我理解。我是说，有些政客也确实让我抓狂过。但这代价很高，而且是你无法控制、无法真正影响的事情。所以，至少意识到这一点可能是好的。

turns.en: You mentioned historians before. One of my friends has a question, his equivalent of Peter Thiel's question of 'What is it that you believe that most people would disagree with?' His is 'What do you think is currently ignored by the media but will be studied by historians?' You're asking me that question right now. What do I think is ignored by the media but will be studied by historians?
turns.zh: 你之前提到过历史学家。我有个朋友有一个问题，相当于彼得·蒂尔的那个问题：‘你相信什么大多数人不同意的事情？’他的问题是：‘你认为目前被媒体忽视，但会被历史学家研究的是什么？’你现在是在问我这个问题。我认为什么被媒体忽视，但会被历史学家研究？

turns.en: Well, I mean the media is only focused on very timely things, right? So it depends if you want to talk about timely or timeless. But as a simple example, if I just look at things that maybe in the next five or ten years are going to make a massive difference that people are not focused enough on. And I think within two years this will be obvious. So, like, I make a prediction, and predictions are tough, and I have to eat it in a few years. Yeah, I'm going to have to eat this in a few years. So I'm probably wrong, but two things that I pay attention to that I don't think a lot of people do pay attention to. Well, there's a couple. One is I think just how bad modern medicine is. I think people just put a lot more faith in modern medicine than is warranted. Like our best ideas for a lot of things are surgery, just cutting things out, right? Treating things that are extraneous like, 'Oh, you don't really need a gallbladder, you don't really need an appendix, or you don't really need tonsils.' Oh, that's false. Every surplus requirement. The human body is very, very efficient. All those things are needed. So I think the state of modern medicine is still pretty bad. We don't have many good explanatory theories in biology. We have germ theory of disease, we have evolution, we have cell theory, we have DNA genetics, morphogenesis, embryogenesis, and not much else. Everything else is rules of thumb, memorization. A affects B because A affects C affects D, but we don't understand the underlying explanation. It's all just words pointing to words pointing to words. So biology is still in a very sorry state, and because we are not allowed to take risks that might kill people, we just don't experiment enough in biology. So a lot of treatments are just outright banned by large regulatory bodies. So we just don't have the innovation.
turns.zh: 嗯，我的意思是媒体只关注非常时效性的事情，对吧？所以这取决于你想谈时效性还是永恒性。但举个简单的例子，如果我看看未来五到十年内可能产生巨大影响、但人们关注不够的事情。我认为两年内这些就会变得显而易见。所以，我做一个预测，预测很难，几年后我得自食其言。是的，几年后我得自食其言。所以我可能错了，但我关注的两件事，我认为很多人并不关注。嗯，有几件。一是现代医学有多糟糕。我认为人们对现代医学的信任超过了它应得的。比如，我们对很多疾病最好的想法就是手术，直接切除，对吧？把多余的东西处理掉，比如‘哦，你不需要胆囊，你不需要阑尾，你不需要扁桃体。’哦，那是错的。每一个多余的器官都有其用途。人体非常非常高效。所有这些器官都是必需的。所以我认为现代医学的状况仍然很糟糕。我们在生物学中没有很多好的解释性理论。我们有疾病细菌理论，我们有进化论，我们有细胞理论，我们有DNA遗传学，形态发生，胚胎发生，除此之外没有多少了。其他一切都是经验法则、死记硬背。A影响B是因为A影响C影响D，但我们不理解背后的解释。一切都只是词语指向词语指向词语。所以生物学仍然处于非常可怜的状态，而且因为我们不允许冒可能致人死亡的风险，我们在生物学中实验不够。所以很多治疗方法直接被大型监管机构禁止。所以我们根本没有创新。

turns.en: So I think we're still in the stone age when it comes to biology and we got a long ways to go. And I think people will look back aghast at this. And I think this is Brian Johnson's point. He's like, you know, let's be more extreme. Let's try to live forever. We must be more experimental. And I'll start as an end of one and start experimenting on myself. But even there, I disagree with Brian in many things like, you know, taking huge amounts of supplements. I think we just don't know supplements outside of their natural context like just eat liver, man. But that's fine. And I wouldn't be vegan either, but I really appreciate that he's experimenting. He's good-natured about it, he shares everything. So we need more people like that. So I think the state of biology people will look back and say wow that was in the dark ages.
turns.zh: 所以我认为在生物学方面，我们仍然处于石器时代，还有很长的路要走。我认为人们会对此感到震惊。我认为这就是布莱恩·约翰逊的观点。他说，让我们更极端一点。让我们尝试永生。我们必须更加实验性。我将从自己开始，开始在自己身上做实验。但即使在那里，我在很多事情上也不同意布莱恩，比如服用大量补充剂。我认为我们不了解补充剂在自然背景之外的作用，比如直接吃肝脏就好。但这没关系。我也不会成为素食主义者，但我很欣赏他在做实验。他性格很好，分享一切。所以我们需要更多像他这样的人。所以我认为人们回顾生物学现状时会说，哇，那真是黑暗时代。

turns.en: I think another thing that we'll look back on is I think we still continue to underestimate how important drones are going to be in warfare. The future of all warfare is drones. There will be nothing else on the battlefield. Because I think of the end state of drones as autonomous bullets. Not even guided autonomous like they're self-directed. So if that's the future we're headed towards, and it is, why would you have an armed force? There's going to be no aircraft carriers, there's going to be no tanks, there's going to be no infantry men, there's just going to be autonomous bullets. Buy autonomous bullets against your autonomous bullets. Whichever ones win, the other side just surrenders because it's over.
turns.zh: 我认为另一件我们会回顾的事情是，我们仍然继续低估无人机在战争中的重要性。所有战争的未来都是无人机。战场上将没有其他东西。因为我认为无人机的最终形态是自主子弹。甚至不是制导自主，而是自我导向。所以如果那是我们走向的未来，而且确实如此，为什么还要有武装力量？将没有航空母舰，没有坦克，没有步兵，只有自主子弹。用你的自主子弹对抗对方的自主子弹。哪一方赢了，另一方就投降，因为结束了。

turns.en: I think a third piece that is going to be kind of unexpected is the GLP-1s, which I know you and I have privately discussed before. I think these are the most breakthrough drugs since antibiotics. They're probably more important than statins. They're sort of miracle drugs. There are downsides, but the downsides and side effects are so minor compared to the upsides beyond just weight loss. They also seem to be addiction breakers. They seem to lower many kinds of cancer. They almost metabolically reverse aging up to a certain point. And I think they're going to bend the curve on health care costs. And the big question people are going to be asking over the next 5 years is why are Americans paying thousands of dollars a month for this when people overseas are getting them for free or I can order them from China for free or whatever. Maybe if I were Bernie Sanders the platform I would be running on is I would say okay we're going to pay hundreds of billions of dollars to Novo and Eli Lilly and we're just going to make these free or there are hundreds of analogs of these things that work. These are not going to be limited to just the few that are being used today. Just take one of them or two of them, make them free, and I think it'll make a big difference.
turns.zh: 我认为第三件会有些出乎意料的事情是GLP-1类药物，我知道我们之前私下讨论过。我认为这些是自抗生素以来最具突破性的药物。它们可能比他汀类药物更重要。它们有点像奇迹药物。有缺点，但与除了减肥之外的好处相比，缺点和副作用非常小。它们似乎还能打破成瘾。它们似乎能降低多种癌症的风险。它们几乎能在一定程度上逆转代谢衰老。我认为它们将改变医疗成本的曲线。未来5年人们会问的一个大问题是，为什么美国人每月要为此支付数千美元，而海外的人可以免费获得，或者我可以从中国免费订购等等。如果我是伯尼·桑德斯，我的竞选纲领会是，好吧，我们向诺和诺德和礼来支付数千亿美元，然后让这些药物免费，或者有数百种有效的类似物。这些药物不会仅限于今天使用的几种。只要拿其中一两种，让它们免费，我认为就会产生巨大影响。

turns.en: And as you and I were discussing earlier, this does bend a lot of people out of shape who got there the old-fashioned way, and they want to see obesity as a moral failing on people's parts and it lowers their status if they are suddenly signal is less of a signal. Correct. Absolutely.
turns.zh: 正如我们之前讨论的，这确实让很多通过传统方式达到目标的人感到不爽，他们想将肥胖视为人们的道德缺陷，而如果突然信号不再那么明显，他们的地位就会降低。没错。完全正确。

turns.en: Yeah. So they are incentivized to say, 'Oh, well, you don't know the downsides.' It's irresponsible to suggest it's going to cause cancer. Have fun losing bone and muscle mass. But none of that stuff is really true. The cancer stuff is actually beneficial. I know people who are now taking these things for anti-aging reasons. They're already fit, but they just want to age better and have a stronger insulin metabolism. And there's evidence now these things put off dementia, Alzheimer's, colon cancer. It's insane. Cardiovascular disease, the list of benefits is insane. There's no free lunch. But this is a class of drugs that prevents you from taking other drugs into your body. It prevents you from taking too much sugar, too many calories in an era of abundance, prevents you from smoking, prevents you from even... there's an organization called Casper that is now doing a study on heroin addictions and they're showing that this can lower opioid overdoses and heroin addiction. So there's a lot of overwhelming medical evidence coming out and I think something like 10% of the population might have tried these things.
turns.zh: 是的。所以他们有动机说，‘哦，好吧，你不知道副作用。’暗示它会导致癌症是不负责任的。祝你失去骨骼和肌肉质量愉快。但这些都不是真的。关于癌症的部分实际上是有益的。我认识一些人现在为了抗衰老而服用这些药物。他们已经健康了，但他们只是想更好地衰老，拥有更强的胰岛素代谢。现在有证据表明这些药物能延缓痴呆、阿尔茨海默病、结肠癌。这太疯狂了。心血管疾病，好处清单太疯狂了。没有免费的午餐。但这是一类能阻止你将其他药物摄入体内的药物。它阻止你在富足时代摄入过多的糖和卡路里，阻止你吸烟，甚至阻止你……有一个叫Casper的组织现在正在研究海洛因成瘾，他们表明这可以降低阿片类药物过量和海洛因成瘾。所以有大量压倒性的医学证据出现，我认为大约10%的人口可能已经尝试过这些药物。

turns.en: I think that's the number I've seen as well. It's massive. I think it's about 50% of the population say that they would like to try it.
turns.zh: 我认为这也是我看到的数字。非常庞大。我认为大约50%的人口表示他们想尝试。

turns.en: Exactly. So I think the body positivity movement is dead and we always kind of knew it was a scam. I mean it's dying very very quickly.
turns.zh: 没错。所以我认为身体自爱运动已经死了，我们一直都知道这是个骗局。我是说它正在非常非常快地消亡。

turns.en: Equipped like you can never be too rich, too thin or too clean, right? And immediately a whole bunch of people went nonlinear my mention like what do you mean too thin and what about the hygiene hypothesis and obviously there's always exceptions but people want to be thin and fit and people want to be clean back to the pathogen discussion that we had.
turns.zh: 就像你永远不会太富有、太瘦或太干净，对吧？然后立刻有一大堆人反应激烈，比如你说太瘦是什么意思？卫生假说呢？显然总有例外，但人们想要苗条健康，人们想要干净，回到我们之前讨论的病原体话题。

turns.en: So I think overall that there's going to be huge demand for these things and our modern medical system is not built to supply these. Well, I don't hold it against the pharmas. I think the farmers did their job by creating the thing, but I think next we need to step up and figure out how to make it broadly and cheaply available as opposed to just milk it for only people on obesity who can get Medicare to sign off for it or people paying out of pocket at very very high prices.
turns.zh: 所以我认为总体而言，对这些药物的需求会很大，而我们现代的医疗体系并不适合供应这些药物。我不怪制药公司。我认为制药公司完成了他们的工作，创造了这个东西，但我认为接下来我们需要站出来，想办法让它广泛且廉价地可用，而不是仅仅榨取那些肥胖患者（通过医保批准）或自掏腰包支付极高价格的人。

turns.en: The benefits of societal distribution of the safer GLP-1s is so large that whichever politician tackles that is going to be richly rewarded.
turns.zh: 更安全的GLP-1类药物在社会层面推广的好处如此之大，以至于任何解决这个问题的政治家都会得到丰厚的回报。

turns.en: Well, obesity is the number one source of malnutrition worldwide. There's twice as many people that are obese than are starving. So about half a billion people are starving and a billion are obese. So many problems are downstream of that. Like look at how much of the federal budget goes into dialysis because of kidney failure. And why is that? It's because of diabetes, right? So many of the problems that we have in modern society are downstream of obesity. And fitness is so important. And yes, in some people these things cause muscle and bone loss, but not in the people who are eating high protein and working out hard. So they can be taken in a way that's safer. And some versions of these like literal glutai, the original one, they've been around for decades, and the others have been around for about a decade. So we already have, as you said, 10% of the population taking them. So they're already quite widely distributed. A good sample size.
turns.zh: 嗯，肥胖是全球营养不良的头号原因。肥胖人数是饥饿人数的两倍。大约有5亿人挨饿，10亿人肥胖。很多问题都源于此。比如看看联邦预算中有多少用于肾衰竭透析。为什么？因为糖尿病，对吧？所以现代社会的很多问题都源于肥胖。而健康非常重要。是的，在某些人身上，这些药物会导致肌肉和骨骼流失，但那些摄入高蛋白并努力锻炼的人不会。所以它们可以以更安全的方式服用。其中一些版本，比如原始的利拉鲁肽，已经存在了几十年，其他的也大约存在了十年。所以正如你所说，已经有10%的人口在服用它们。所以它们已经相当广泛地分布了。很好的样本量。

turns.en: Yeah, it's a great sample size.
turns.zh: 是的，样本量很大。

turns.en: What more do you need? Like if you have a bacterial infection that's eating you, I don't say, 'Oh, I have this antibiotic, but it's going to raise your blood pressure.' It's like, 'No, take the antibiotic.' If you're going to kill yourself, I say, 'Take this antipsychotic and stay alive a little longer and solve it.' I don't say, 'Oh, it's going to cause your heart rate to go up by three beats a minute.' I don't worry about that. So similarly, if you're poisoning yourself with toxins and overuse of substances that you shouldn't be using, either heroin, alcohol, cigarettes, sugar, or just sheer calories, take this GLP1. They also improve digestion. You just have less food matter going through your stomach. They lower cancer risks across the board. There are quite a few cancers that lower. Cardiovascular, I mean, I don't know what else to tell you. I've been very surprised by the negative reception whenever you have a conversation about GLP1s. I think a lot of it may be people who are basically saying, 'You should work harder, you should be fit like I did.' It's lowering their status. Think about all the nutritionists and doctors and trainers who are now being put out of business in a way. It's kind of like why does the American military keep buying aircraft carriers in the age of drones? There's an incentive bias. There's a very strong motivated reasoning. But it doesn't matter. 10% of people are on it. Everybody wants to be fit. It's going to spread like wildfire.
turns.zh: 你还需要什么？比如你得了细菌感染，我不会说：‘哦，我有抗生素，但它会升高你的血压。’而是说：‘不，吃抗生素。’如果你要自杀，我会说：‘吃这种抗精神病药，多活一会儿，解决问题。’我不会说：‘哦，它会让你的心率每分钟增加三下。’我不担心那个。同样，如果你用毒素和过度使用不该用的物质毒害自己，比如海洛因、酒精、香烟、糖，或者纯粹的热量，那就吃GLP1。它们还能改善消化。你胃里经过的食物就少了。它们全面降低癌症风险。有好几种癌症风险降低。心血管，我是说，我不知道还能告诉你什么。每次谈到GLP1时，负面反应都让我很惊讶。我认为很多可能是那些基本上在说‘你应该更努力，你应该像我一样健康’的人。这降低了他们的地位。想想所有那些营养师、医生和教练，他们在某种程度上正在被淘汰。这有点像为什么美国军方在无人机时代还继续购买航空母舰？存在激励偏差。有非常强烈的动机推理。但这没关系。10%的人在用。每个人都想健康。它会像野火一样蔓延。

turns.en: I was just thinking as you were talking that when we think about health, a lot of people get captured by the way they were brought up, the habits they had from childhood, or what mom and dad did, or genetic predisposition. I think you have as many reasons as many people to feel hard done by challenges you had earlier in your life. Is getting past your past a skill? Sort of not being owned today by your history. Sort of not having that victimhood mentality.
turns.zh: 你说话时我就在想，当我们谈到健康时，很多人被他们的成长方式、童年习惯、父母的做法或遗传倾向所束缚。我认为你和很多人一样，有理由因为早年的挑战而感到委屈。摆脱过去是一种技能吗？有点像今天不被你的历史所左右。有点像没有那种受害者心态。

turns.en: Yeah, I did have a tough childhood, but I don't think about it. There are a couple of things going on there. One is I did process it quite a bit. I thought about it, but I thought about it to get rid of it. I didn't think about it to dwell on it or to indulge. I wanted to be successful. I wanted more than anything else to rise past that. So I couldn't have that as a burden on me. So I had to get rid of it. To the extent that I dealt with it, it was for the express purpose of getting rid of it, not to create an identity or story or to reflect upon it or to say look at me, look at what I've accomplished and look how great I am. So I got rid of it. I think at some point you wrestle with that thing and then you just realize you're never going to untangle the whole thing. It's a Gordian knot problem. Like Alexander found that tangled knot in India and they said the famous conqueror will come and will untie this knot nobody else can untie. He took one look at it, pulled out a sword, and just cut it. So at some point you just have to cut your past. If your past is bothering you, you will eventually get tired of trying to untangle that knot and you will just drop it because you will realize life is short and the more you want to accomplish in this life, the less time you have to unravel that thing. So I just wanted to actually get things done. So I had no time to deal with it. So I just cut it. It's like a really bad relationship. But in this case, it's a bad relationship with your own history. So you just drop it.
turns.zh: 是的，我确实有一个艰难的童年，但我不去想它。这里面有几件事。一是我的确处理了很多。我想过它，但我想它是为了摆脱它。我不是为了沉溺或放纵而想它。我想要成功。我最想要的就是超越它。所以我不能让它成为我的负担。所以我必须摆脱它。就我处理它的程度而言，完全是为了摆脱它，而不是为了创造身份或故事，或者反思它，或者说看看我，看看我取得了什么成就，看看我有多伟大。所以我摆脱了它。我认为在某个时刻，你会与那件事搏斗，然后你意识到你永远无法解开整个事情。这是一个戈尔迪之结问题。就像亚历山大在印度发现的那个缠结的结，他们说著名的征服者会来解开这个没人能解开的结。他看了一眼，拔出剑，直接砍断了它。所以在某个时刻，你只需要切断你的过去。如果你的过去困扰你，你最终会厌倦试图解开那个结，你会直接放弃它，因为你会意识到生命短暂，你越想在这一生中完成更多，你解开那件事的时间就越少。所以我只想真正把事情做完。所以我没有时间去处理它。所以我直接切断了它。就像一段非常糟糕的关系。但在这里，是你与自己历史的糟糕关系。所以你就放弃它。

turns.en: I think so much of what we've spoken about today is on the shortness of life and the fact that every moment is precious. You had to talk about that the most fundamental resource in your life is not time, it's attention.
turns.zh: 我认为我们今天谈到的很多内容都是关于生命的短暂，以及每一刻都很珍贵。你不得不谈到，你生命中最基本的资源不是时间，而是注意力。

turns.en: That's right. I used to think the currency of life, people think it's money. Yes, money is important and it does let you trade certain things for time, but it doesn't really buy you time. Ask Warren Buffett how much time money can buy you or Michael Bloomberg. They're rich as Scrooge, but they can't buy more time. Brian Johnson notwithstanding. So you can't trade money for time. Money is not the real currency of life. And time itself doesn't even mean that much because as we talked about before, a lot of time can be wasted because you're not really present for it. You're not paying attention. So the real currency of life is attention. It's what you choose to pay attention to and what you do about it. So back to the point about the news media, you can put your attention on the news, but that's how you're spending the real currency of life. So just be aware of that. If you want to, that's fine. There's no right or wrong here. Maybe it is your destiny to pick something in the news, learn about that problem, adopt that problem, and solve it. But just be careful because your attention is the only thing that you have and that can also be captured by your own past.
turns.zh: 没错。我以前认为生命的货币，人们认为是钱。是的，钱很重要，它确实让你用某些东西换取时间，但它并不能真正买到时间。问问沃伦·巴菲特钱能买多少时间，或者迈克尔·布隆伯格。他们像斯克鲁奇一样富有，但他们买不到更多时间。布莱恩·约翰逊除外。所以你不能用钱换时间。钱不是生命的真正货币。时间本身甚至没那么重要，因为正如我们之前谈到的，很多时间可以被浪费，因为你并没有真正活在当下。你没有在注意。所以生命的真正货币是注意力。是你选择关注什么以及你对此做什么。所以回到关于新闻媒体的话题，你可以把注意力放在新闻上，但那就是你在花费生命的真正货币。所以只要意识到这一点。如果你想这样，那没问题。这里没有对错。也许你的命运就是挑选新闻中的某件事，了解那个问题，采纳那个问题，并解决它。但要小心，因为你的注意力是你唯一拥有的东西，它也可能被你的过去所捕获。

turns.en: Yes. You can fritter it away on anything you like. Is there an advantage to starting out as a loser?
turns.zh: 是的。你可以把它浪费在任何你喜欢的事情上。从失败者开始有优势吗？

turns.en: Absolutely. Because if you're a loser, then you'll want to be a winner and then you'll develop all the characteristics that will help you be a winner in life. That said, I wouldn't sentence my kids to it. I don't think you can artificially do that. It's sort of like imagine that you were born a serf 300 years ago and then somehow you managed to escape off the farm and you become a land owner and then eventually you become minor nobility and aristocrat. Are you going to put your kids back on the farm and say you're going to be a serf again? I know they all like those stories. The kids themselves like those stories because it says I came from the school of hard knocks. My dad made me go shovel hay for a summer. It's not real. You're not going to trick them. I think what you can do is cultivate an appreciation and gratitude for what you have. And the only way to do that is just evidence it yourself. Just show yourself how you spend money, how you respect it, what you do with it, how you take care of people, who you're responsible for. And the more resources you have, the greater the tribe you can take care of. So when you have no resources, you're struggling to take care of yourself.
turns.zh: 绝对有。因为如果你是个失败者，你就会想成为赢家，然后你会培养所有帮助你在生活中成为所谓赢家的特质。不过，我不会让我的孩子也这样。我不认为你能人为地做到这一点。这有点像想象你300年前生为农奴，然后不知怎么逃出了农场，成了地主，最终成了小贵族和贵族。你会把你的孩子放回农场，说你再做一次农奴吗？我知道他们都喜欢那些故事。孩子们自己也喜欢那些故事，因为它说我是从艰苦学校出来的。我爸爸让我去铲了一个夏天的干草。这不是真的。你骗不了他们。我认为你能做的是培养对你所拥有的东西的欣赏和感激。而唯一的方法就是你自己以身作则。展示你自己如何花钱，如何尊重它，用它做什么，如何照顾人，你对谁负责。你拥有的资源越多，你能照顾的部落就越大。所以当你没有资源时，你连照顾自己都很挣扎。

turns.en: And at that point, it's good to be selfish because you can't save somebody else if you can't even save yourself. Yes. So you take care of yourself and you become the best version of yourself. But there are too many men who are able, fit, and have some money who are doing nothing with their lives. They're just sitting at home doing nothing, just indulging in themselves. Maybe they go on dates and they get DoorDash. Like I have no respect for that. I think there's nothing worse in society than a lazy man because he's leaving it all on the table. He's leaving his potential on the table. It's bad for him. So the next thing you do is you go and you have a family and you take care of your family. Take care of that tribe. Then you take care of your extended family. You take care of your cousins, brothers, uncles, grandmothers, aunts, you know, sisters, everybody that you can. And then if you have more resources beyond that, then you go take care of your local tribe. You take care of your people. You start trying to do some good for the world. And if you have more resource than that, you go take care of an even bigger tribe. And that's how you earn both respect and self-confidence and you live up to your potential.
turns.zh: 在那个时候，自私一点是好的，因为如果你连自己都救不了，你就救不了别人。是的。所以你要照顾好自己，成为最好的自己。但是有太多有能力、健康、有点钱的男人，他们什么都不做，只是坐在家里无所事事，沉溺于自我。也许他们出去约会，点个外卖。我对此毫无敬意。我认为社会上最糟糕的就是懒惰的男人，因为他把一切都浪费了，他把自己的潜力浪费了。这对他不好。所以接下来你要做的是组建家庭，照顾你的家人。照顾好那个部落。然后照顾你的大家庭。照顾你的堂表亲、兄弟、叔叔、祖母、阿姨、姐妹，所有你能照顾的人。然后如果你有更多的资源，你就去照顾你当地的部落。照顾你的人民。你开始尝试为世界做些好事。如果你有更多的资源，你就去照顾一个更大的部落。这就是你赢得尊重和自信的方式，也是你发挥潜力的方式。

turns.en: So the more you have, the more is rightfully expected of you. And I think it's a good compact with society when highly capable people express and flex that capability by giving more and more and by doing more and more. And society rewards them with the one thing they can't get otherwise which is status. Right? So society should give you status in exchange for it. They should say, 'Okay, you did a good job. You took care of more people than just yourself and just the people immediately around you.' And that's what an alpha male to me is. An alpha male is not the one who gets to eat first. The alpha male eats last. The alpha male feeds everybody else first and then gets to eat last. And they do that out of their own self-respect and pride. And society rewards them by calling them an alpha and giving them status.
turns.zh: 所以你拥有的越多，人们对你的期望就越高。我认为这是一个很好的社会契约：当有能力的人通过给予更多、做得更多来表达和施展他们的能力时，社会会用他们无法通过其他方式获得的东西来奖励他们，那就是地位。对吧？所以社会应该用地位来交换。他们应该说，‘好吧，你做得很好。你照顾了更多的人，不仅仅是自己和身边的人。’这就是我对阿尔法男性的定义。阿尔法男性不是那个先吃饭的人。阿尔法男性最后吃。阿尔法男性先喂饱所有人，然后自己最后吃。他们这样做是出于自尊和骄傲。社会通过称他们为阿尔法并给予他们地位来奖励他们。

turns.en: I wonder whether some of the pushback we've got against rich, wealthy, powerful people is disincentivizing. It is like who was it? Zuck who donated money at Zuckerberg General's hospital and they wanted to pull his name off of it. I mean that's... I didn't see that but that's that kind of stuff backfires, right? You should reward people for doing what... were you saying before you don't just need to... in fact actually actively avoid castigating people if you want their behavior to change when they get something wrong. Look at reinforcing it when they get something right. It's happening at a societal level as well.
turns.zh: 我想知道，我们对富人、有钱人、有权势的人的一些抵制是否在起反作用。比如是谁？扎克伯格捐钱给扎克伯格综合医院，他们却想把他的名字从上面去掉。我是说……我没看到，但这种事情适得其反，对吧？你应该奖励人们做正确的事……你之前说过，你不需要……实际上，如果你想改变他们的行为，当他们做错事时，要主动避免谴责他们。当他们做对时，要加以强化。这在社会层面也在发生。

turns.en: Correct. I mean, like the guys who make a lot of money and go out and buy sports teams, I wouldn't do that, right? But the one who goes out and builds a hospital or builds a rocket to take people to the moon, or rescue some astronauts, you should be rewarding him for that.
turns.zh: 没错。我的意思是，那些赚了很多钱然后去买体育队的人，我不会那样做，对吧？但是那些去建医院或者造火箭把人送上月球，或者营救宇航员的人，你应该奖励他。

turns.en: Naval, I really appreciate you. I hope that this has lived up to whatever weird daydreams you've been having. What have you got coming up? What can people expect from you over the next however long?
turns.zh: Naval，我真的很感激你。我希望这没有辜负你那些奇怪的白日梦。你接下来有什么计划？在接下来的时间里，人们可以期待你什么？

turns.en: Expect nothing. That's the most Naval way that we could have finished this, dude.
turns.zh: 什么都别期待。这是最Naval的方式，伙计。

turns.en: It's been a long time coming and I really do appreciate you for being here today. But I do hope you deliver something.
turns.zh: 这一天等了很久，我真的很感谢你今天能来。但我确实希望你有所产出。

turns.en: Oh, I think you have. So, thank you. Thanks for having me.
turns.zh: 哦，我觉得你已经有了。所以，谢谢你。谢谢邀请我。

turns.en: Thank you, too. Thanks for getting in my mind. And hopefully now you're out. We'll see. Maybe even worse now you've got the real memories to stick. I don't know. The reason to win the game is to be free of it. The reason to do the podcast is to be done with it. All right. Wow. You made it to the end. Congratulations. Well, if you enjoyed that, you're going to love my full-length conversation with the one and only Alander Boton from the School of Life right here. Go on.
